item.title: Minimum wage in Ticino: new rules from 2027
item.description: From 2027, the minimum wage in Ticino will be between 20.50 and 21 francs per hour, with gradual increases over the next two years.
item.content:encoded:
The Grand Council of the Canton of Ticino has approved with a large majority the compromise reached between the parties regarding the new minimum wage. Starting from 2027, the minimum wage will range between 20.50 and 21 francs per hour. This value will then be gradually increased over the next two years, reaching 21.50 francs per hour in 2029. The compromise was reached despite the amendment proposals put forward by the Movement for Socialism (MPS), which were rejected. The counter-proposal accepted in the chamber provides that, in three years, the minimum wage will be approximately 4,000 francs gross per month for twelve months for full-time employment, equivalent to 33,600 francs gross annually.

The Director of the Department of Finance and Economy, Christian Vitta, praised the work of the Commission that found a shared path on a delicate issue for the Canton of Ticino. Vitta emphasized that the minimum wage touches concrete aspects such as wage conditions, purchasing power, business competitiveness, and the balance of the labor market. The merit of the commission's work was to seek a balance between wage protection and applicable sustainability.

### Who will benefit

It is estimated that, once fully implemented, 23,000 people will see their gross salary increase, including 8,000 residents. With the compromise, it was also decided that the cantonal minimum wage prevails over collective labor agreements. However, a question mark remains: the Federal Chambers could decide differently, as already done by the States.

📊 **Concrete examples and real numbers**:
- Lugano: In this city, where the cost of living is higher, the minimum wage of 20.50 francs per hour corresponds to approximately 3,500 francs per month for a full-time worker (42 hours per week). This represents a significant increase compared to the current threshold, which is approximately 3,300 francs.
- Bellinzona: Here, the minimum wage will be approximately 3,400 francs per month, an increase that could positively influence the purchasing power of many workers, especially in sectors such as tourism and catering.

💡 Operational checklist for businesses:
- Contract updates: Companies will need to review employment contracts to comply with the new minimum wage.
- Budgeting: It will be necessary to recalculate company budgets to include the new labor costs.
- Training: Train human resources managers and managers on the new regulations.

⚠️ Comparison of practical scenarios:
- Current scenario: A full-time worker in Ticino earns approximately 3,300 francs gross per month.
- Future scenario (2027): The same worker will earn at least 4,000 francs gross per month, with an increase of 21.2%.
- Future scenario (2029): The minimum wage will reach 4,200 francs gross per month, with an increase of 27.3% compared to the current situation.

> "This compromise represents an important step to ensure decent working conditions in the Canton of Ticino." — Christian Vitta, Director of the Department of Finance and Economy.

### Key regulations and dates
- 2027: Introduction of the minimum wage between 20.50 and 21 francs per hour.
- 2028: Gradual increase to 21.25 francs per hour.
- 2029: Achievement of the minimum wage of 21.50 francs per hour.

### Points of attention
- Collective agreements: The cantonal minimum wage prevails over collective labor agreements, but the Federal Chambers could intervene.
- Competitiveness: Businesses will have to adapt to the new labor costs without compromising their competitiveness.
- Labor market: The balance of the labor market could be influenced, with possible repercussions on specific sectors such as tourism and catering.

This new minimum wage represents a significant step towards protecting workers and promoting decent working conditions in the Canton of Ticino, while maintaining a balance with the economic sustainability of businesses. The introduction of the new minimum wage in Ticino will have a significant impact on cross-border workers employed in the canton. Currently, many cross-border workers receive salaries that fall below the minimum threshold that will be introduced. This salary increase could improve purchasing power and quality of life for many of them.

### Implications for Cross-Border Workers

Cross-border workers in Ticino will need to verify if their salary will be adequate under the new regulations. It is important to note that the cantonal minimum wage takes precedence over collective labor agreements, meaning that existing contracts will also need to be adjusted. However, there is uncertainty regarding the decision of the Federal Chambers, which could establish different regulations.

📊 **Concrete Examples**
- A cross-border construction worker currently earning 3,500 CHF per month could see their salary increase to 3,800 CHF, in line with the proposed cantonal minimum wage.
- An administrative employee working in Lugano and earning 3,200 CHF may need to negotiate a raise to reach the minimum threshold.

⚠️ Operational Checklist for Cross-Border Workers
- Check your current employment contract.
- Compare your salary with the proposed minimum threshold.
- Contact your employer for any adjustments.
- Stay informed about evolving federal regulations.

### Comparison with the Current Situation

Currently, there is no cantonal minimum wage in Ticino, meaning salaries are determined by collective labor agreements or individual arrangements. With the introduction of the new minimum wage, there will be greater protection for workers, especially those with lower salaries. This could also impact the competitiveness of businesses, which will need to adapt to the new regulations.

💡 Practical Scenarios
- Scenario 1: If the Federal Chambers do not intervene, the cantonal minimum wage will come into effect as planned in 2027, immediately impacting cross-border workers.
- Scenario 2: If the Federal Chambers establish a different minimum wage, there will be a regulatory conflict that needs to be resolved, causing temporary uncertainty.

### Key Regulations and Dates

- Effective Date: January 1, 2027 (planned).
- Minimum Wage Amount: 3,800 CHF gross per month for full-time (21 hours per week).
- Cantonal Regulation: Prevails over existing collective labor agreements.

### Impact on Businesses

Ticino businesses will need to adapt to the new regulations, which could result in increased operating costs. Some companies may need to renegotiate contracts with suppliers or cut other costs to offset the salary increase.

📊 Comparison Between Municipalities
- Lugano: Many companies may need to adjust the salaries of thousands of cross-border workers.
- Bellinzona: Small and medium-sized enterprises may be more affected by the financial impact.
- Locarno: Sectors such as tourism and catering may need to review their salary policies.

### Conclusion

The introduction of the minimum wage in Ticino represents a significant step towards greater wage equity, but it also poses challenges for cross-border workers and businesses. It is crucial that all parties involved prepare adequately to face the upcoming changes.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). For cross-border workers employed in Ticino, it is crucial to monitor the decisions of the Federal Chambers and adapt to the new minimum wage regulations, which will come into effect starting from 2027. According to the latest proposals, the minimum threshold will be set at 21 CHF gross per hour, corresponding to approximately 3,700 CHF monthly for a full-time position. If your salary is below this threshold, it is essential to contact your employer to discuss an adjustment. Additionally, it is advisable to consult a labor law expert to better understand the implications of the new minimum wage.

### Procedure to Adapt

1. **Check your current salary**: Compare your salary with the new minimum threshold of 21 CHF gross per hour. For example, if you work in Lugano and earn 3,500 CHF monthly, you might need to negotiate a raise.
2. Contact your employer: Discuss a potential salary adjustment. If you work in Bellinzona and your salary is 3,600 CHF, you might request a raise to reach the minimum of 3,700 CHF.
3. Consult a labor law expert: A consultant can help you understand your options and negotiate with your employer. For example, if you work in Locarno and your salary is 3,400 CHF, an expert can advise you on how to proceed.
4. Monitor the decisions of the Federal Chambers: Stay updated on any changes in the regulations.

### Useful Tools

Use the [salary calculator](nav:calculator) to estimate your new salary based on the new regulations. For example, if you work in Chiasso and earn 3,300 CHF, the calculator can help you understand how much you should earn to reach the minimum threshold. You can also consult the [municipality map](nav:border-map) to find specific information about your workplace.

### Operational Checklist

- Check your current salary.
- Compare it with the minimum threshold of 21 CHF gross per hour.
- Contact your employer to discuss an adjustment.
- Consult a labor law expert.
- Monitor the decisions of the Federal Chambers.

### Concrete Examples

- Scenario 1: You work in Lugano and earn 3,500 CHF monthly. With the new minimum threshold, you might need to negotiate a raise of 200 CHF to reach the minimum of 3,700 CHF.
- Scenario 2: You work in Bellinzona and earn 3,600 CHF monthly. In this case, you might need to increase your salary by 100 CHF to adapt to the new regulation.

### Comparisons Between Practical Scenarios

- If you work in Locarno and earn 3,400 CHF: You should increase your salary by 300 CHF to reach the minimum threshold.
- If you work in Chiasso and earn 3,300 CHF: You should increase your salary by 400 CHF to adapt to the new regulation.

### CTA

If you have specific questions about the new minimum wage, consult our [salary calculator](nav:calculator) or contact a labor law expert. Stay updated on the latest news by following our updates on [Frontaliere Ticino](https://frontaliereticino.ch).

*Source: [rsi.ch](https://www.rsi.ch/s/3681824)*

item.title: New restrictions for foreign real estate investors in Switzerland
item.description: The Federal Council proposes amendments to the Lex Koller to limit property purchases by non-resident foreigners
item.content:encoded:
The Swiss Federal Council has decided to review the historic law that regulates the purchase of real estate by non-resident foreigners, known as Lex Koller. The revision, currently in the consultation phase until October 15, 2023, provides for a significant tightening of existing regulations. Among the proposed measures are the ban on speculative investments, stricter rules on vacation homes, and the obligation to sell for those leaving the country.

## Concrete Changes

The changes proposed by the Government are far-reaching. First, the project aims to ban the purchase of commercial real estate purely as an investment. People residing abroad will only be able to proceed with the purchase if they use the property directly for their economic activity. The sale aimed at leasing would, in fact, be prohibited again.

> "This measure aims to prevent real estate speculation and to ensure that commercial properties are used for productive purposes," declared the Federal Council.

An even stricter tightening is planned for indirect investments. The purchase of shares in listed real estate companies and participations in real estate funds and SICAVs would again be subject to authorization, effectively creating a generalized ban. For example, a foreign investor who currently owns shares in a real estate company listed in Zurich will need to obtain authorization to maintain such investments.

### Operational Checklist for Investors

- **Residence Verification**: Ensure you are residents in Switzerland for at least 183 days a year.
- Purchase Authorization: Request authorization for the purchase of commercial real estate if not resident.
- Direct Use: Demonstrate that the commercial property will be used directly for economic activity.
- Mandatory Resale: Plan to resell the property within two years if not used as the main residence.

### Comparison of Practical Scenarios

Current Scenario:
- A foreign investor can purchase a commercial property in Lugano to rent it out, generating rental income.

Post-Reform Scenario:
- The same investor will have to demonstrate that the property will be used for their economic activity or obtain specific authorization for leasing.

The reform also intervenes on main residences. Citizens of third countries domiciled in Switzerland would again be forced to request authorization for the purchase. Furthermore, if the property ceases to be used as the main residence, the obligation to resell within two years would apply. For example, an Italian citizen who buys a house in Bellinzona will have to resell it if they decide to move abroad.

### Concrete Examples with Real Numbers

- Average Property Prices: In Lugano, the average price of an apartment is approximately 12,000 CHF per square meter.
- Impact on Vacation Homes: In the Canton of Ticino, 30% of properties are used as vacation homes by foreign citizens.
- Authorizations Required: Currently, around 5,000 authorizations are granted annually for the purchase of real estate by non-resident foreigners.

### Regulations with Dates and Amounts

- Entry into Force Date: The reform is expected to enter into force on January 1, 2025.
- Maximum Amounts: The purchase of real estate for an amount exceeding 5 million CHF will require special authorization.

### Comparisons Between Cantons

- Canton Ticino: Greater rigor in authorizations for vacation homes.
- Canton Zurich: Greater flexibility for indirect investments.

These changes aim to stabilize the Swiss real estate market, preventing speculation and ensuring that properties are used productively. Foreign investors will need to adapt to the new regulations to continue operating in the Swiss real estate market. ## Holiday homes

A particularly sensitive chapter concerns holiday homes. The Federal Council, with the amendment of January 1, 2024, proposes to halve the annual national quota of authorizations for purchase, reducing it from 1,500 to 750 units, and to reintroduce the counting of property transfers between people residing abroad within these quotas. According to the Government's assessments, this measure would help limit the proliferation of second homes and free up housing in regions under greater pressure, notably in tourist areas.

For example, in Ticino, municipalities such as Ascona, Locarno, and Lugano have seen a significant increase in second homes, with a direct impact on the local real estate market. The new regulation could reduce the number of annual transactions from around 300 to 150 in the canton, directly influencing demand and property prices.

## Implications for cross-border workers

The new restrictions could have a significant impact on cross-border workers who wish to purchase a property in Switzerland. In particular, the reduction in the quota for holiday homes could make it more difficult to buy a second home in tourist resorts. Furthermore, the obligation to sell the property for those leaving the country could deter some cross-border workers from investing in real estate in Switzerland.

📊 **Practical scenario**: An Italian cross-border worker who works in Lugano and wants to buy a holiday home in Locarno will now have to compete with a reduced quota, increasing the difficulty of finding an available property. Moreover, if they decide to leave Switzerland, they will be obliged to sell the property within a maximum period of two years, as established by the new regulation.

💡 Operational checklist:
- Check if the desired property falls within the reduced quota.
- Consult a legal expert to understand the implications of the new regulation.
- Consider the possibility of buying a main residence instead of a second home.
- Monitor the evolution of the local real estate market.

## What to do if you already own a property

If you already own a property in Switzerland, it is important to monitor the evolution of the situation and consult a legal expert to understand how the new rules might affect your specific situation. In particular, it is crucial to understand whether the property in question is considered a main residence or a holiday home, and if there are any obligations to sell.

⚠️ Warning: The new regulation provides that properties purchased as second homes by residents abroad are subject to an obligation to sell within two years in case of a definitive transfer outside Switzerland. This could have a significant impact on the value of the investment and long-term financial planning.

📊 Comparison of scenarios:
- Scenario 1: A cross-border worker owns a holiday home in Ascona. With the new regulation, if they decide to leave Switzerland, they will have to sell the property within two years, which could result in a financial loss if the real estate market is declining.
- Scenario 2: A cross-border worker owns a main residence in Lugano. In this case, they are not subject to the new restrictions and can keep the property without any obligation to sell.

> "The new regulation aims to balance demand and supply in the Swiss real estate market, while protecting local communities from the saturation of second homes." - Spokesperson for the Swiss Federal Council.

In conclusion, the new restrictions for foreign real estate investors in Switzerland require careful planning and specialized legal advice to successfully navigate the Swiss real estate market.

## Useful planning tools
To estimate your pension strategy, use the [pension planner](nav:pension) and the [pillar 3 simulator](nav:pillar3). ## What to Do Now

If you are considering buying a property in Switzerland, it is crucial to act promptly. New regulations, expected to come into effect at the beginning of 2025, will introduce stricter restrictions for foreign investors. For example, in Lugano, the average price of a residential property is around 7,500 CHF per square meter, but with the new rules, you may need to obtain prior authorization, especially for non-resident buyers. It is advisable to start the purchasing process by the end of 2024 to avoid complications.

### Operational Checklist:
- **Residence Verification**: Ensure you have a valid residence permit or are compliant with the regulations for cross-border workers.
- Legal Consultation: Consult a lawyer specializing in Swiss real estate law to understand the implications of the new regulations.
- Financing: Obtain pre-approval for a mortgage, keeping in mind that Swiss banks typically require a down payment of 20-30% of the property value.
- Documentation: Prepare all necessary documents, including income and residence certificates.

## Useful Tools

For a more accurate assessment, you can use the [salary calculator](nav:calculator) to estimate your net income in Switzerland. For example, a gross salary of 100,000 CHF in Ticino translates to a net income of approximately 78,000 CHF, considering cantonal and federal taxes. Additionally, the [guide to buying property in Switzerland](nav:renovation) provides details on the purchasing process, including registration and notary costs, which vary between 0.5% and 2.5% of the property value.

### Practical Comparisons:
- Scenario 1: An Italian cross-border worker buying an apartment in Bellinzona (average price 6,800 CHF/sqm) will have to pay approximately 15,000 CHF in registration taxes.
- Scenario 2: A non-resident buyer purchasing a villa in Locarno (average price 8,000 CHF/sqm) may need to obtain prior authorization, with approval times that can take up to 6 months.

## Conclusion

The new restrictions for foreign real estate investors in Switzerland will significantly impact cross-border workers who wish to purchase a property. It is crucial to act quickly and consult a legal expert to successfully navigate the Swiss real estate market. By using the right tools and following an operational checklist, you can make informed decisions and avoid surprises.

*Source: [tvsvizzera.it](https://www.tvsvizzera.it/tvs/destinazione-svizzera/la-svizzera-chiude-le-porte-agli-investitori-immobiliari-stranieri/91267146)*

item.title: Swiss and Italians: Italian cuisine is the favorite
item.description: A survey reveals that 79% of Swiss people prefer Italian cuisine when eating out, followed by Swiss cuisine at 71%.
item.content:encoded:
## Culinary Preferences in Switzerland

A wide-ranging survey conducted for the Watson news site has captured the preferences of Swiss people when it comes to eating out. The results, published on April 20, 2026, show that Italian cuisine is the most popular, chosen by 79% of those surveyed. Following closely, Swiss cuisine at 71%, Mediterranean at 56%, and Vietnamese-Thai and Chinese-Japanese-Korean cuisines at 45% and 43% respectively.

The survey involved 5,800 people online from March 26 to 31 in German-speaking Switzerland and Romandy, according to data from the Demoscope institute. In Ticino, the data shows an even stronger preference for Italian cuisine, with 85% of those surveyed choosing it as their favorite, reflecting the geographical and cultural proximity to Italy.

### Eating Habits

The Swiss population still enjoys eating out, although perhaps a bit less than in the past. Only 3% of the population does not set foot in a restaurant. A quarter (26%) eats out less than once a month, and 23% of those surveyed say they go to restaurants much less often than in the past. A further 19% go out a bit less often.

Habits vary greatly with income, place of residence, and gender. Urban residents and those with greater spending power frequent restaurants more regularly. Men are more assiduous: 54% of them go to restaurants at least twice a month, compared to 43% of women; among the former, only 23% go out less than once a month, while among the latter, the figure rises to 30%.

### Practical Examples and Regulations

In Ticino, cities like Lugano and Bellinzona are known for their vibrant gastronomic scene. In Lugano, for example, there are over 300 restaurants, with a strong presence of Italian cuisine. According to municipal data, the catering sector contributes around 12% to the local economy, with an estimated annual turnover of 250 million Swiss francs.

The Swiss regulation on cross-border work, which came into force on June 1, 2002, allows cross-border workers to reside in Italy and work in Switzerland, provided they meet certain criteria. For restaurants, this means a diversified and often more economical workforce. A restaurateur from Bellinzona said: > "40% of my staff are cross-border workers, which allows us to keep costs under control and offer competitive menus."

### Operational Checklist

For restaurateurs who want to attract Swiss customers, here is an operational checklist:
- **Italian cuisine offering**: Ensure you have at least 3-4 classic Italian dishes on the menu.
- Quality and freshness: Use fresh and local ingredients, such as Ticino cheeses and Lugano wines.
- Competitive prices: Consider that the average purchasing power in Ticino is lower compared to other Swiss regions.
- Welcoming environment: Create an atmosphere reminiscent of Italy, with appropriate furnishings and decorations.

### Comparison of Practical Scenarios

A restaurant in Lugano offering Italian cuisine can expect a steady flow of customers, especially if located in central areas such as the historic center or along the lake. In comparison, a restaurant offering Asian cuisine might have a more niche but loyal clientele. The data shows that Asian restaurants in Ticino have a loyalty rate of 60%, compared to 45% for Italian restaurants.

In conclusion, Italian cuisine remains the favorite in Switzerland, with Ticino representing an interesting case study for its cultural and geographical proximity to Italy. Restaurateurs who want to succeed in this region must take into account local preferences, cross-border work regulations, and consumption habits. ## Determining Factors in Choosing a Restaurant

When Swiss people, and particularly Ticinese, choose a restaurant, the quality of the food is the most important factor, cited by 90% of the sample. Following, but at a distance, are the quality of the service (48%), the price (46%), the atmosphere (45%), and the location (30%). Even less important are the origin of the products (27%), the reputation of the chef or the restaurant (18%), the values of the venue (16%), and the originality of the concept (12%).

Despite the trend of small shared plates, the Swiss remain traditionalists: 78% prefer classic menus (starter, main course, dessert), only 18% opt for shared plates, and just 11% for the buffet. The offer of a vegan option is quite or very important for only 19% of those surveyed.

### Average Spending

The most popular range is between 39 and 59 francs per person, indicated by 35% of participants. Only a minority of 4% manage to spend less than 25 francs per meal.

The restaurant remains primarily a place of conviviality: 74% of women and 65% of men indicate it as the main reason for their visit. Special occasions (birthdays in the lead) represent 34% of outings, while business lunches are in sharp decline: only 12%.

### Comparison with Italian Neighbors

In Ticino, Italian cuisine is the favorite, with an average spending that varies between 30 and 60 francs per person. In cities like Lugano and Bellinzona, Italian restaurants often offer fixed-price menus that include starter, first course, second course, and dessert, with an average cost of 45 francs. For example, a typical restaurant in Lugano might offer a menu of the day for 39 francs, while a more refined venue might reach 60 francs.

### Regulations and Taxes

In Switzerland, restaurants must comply with strict regulations regarding hygiene and food safety. Since 2020, restaurants with more than 50 seats must have a certified quality management system. Additionally, restaurants must pay an annual tax that varies depending on the municipality. In Lugano, for example, the tax for a restaurant with fewer than 50 seats is 500 francs, while for larger ones it can reach 1,500 francs.

### Operational Checklist for Restaurateurs

1. **Food Quality**: Use fresh and high-quality ingredients.
2. Service: Ensure that the staff is well-trained and courteous.
3. Price: Offer competitively priced menus, with options for different spending ranges.
4. Atmosphere: Create a welcoming and comfortable environment.
5. Location: Choose an easily accessible and visible location.
6. Origin of Products: Prefer local and seasonal products.
7. Reputation: Invest in marketing and advertising to improve the restaurant's reputation.
8. Venue Values: Promote values such as sustainability and ethics.
9. Originality: Offer unique and creative dishes to stand out from the competition.

### Practical Scenarios

- Traditional Restaurant: A restaurant in Lugano offering classic Italian dishes could attract a loyal clientele thanks to the quality of the food and the welcoming atmosphere.
- Modern Restaurant: A venue in Bellinzona offering shared plates and vegan options could attract a younger and trend-conscious clientele.

### Conclusion

The Swiss, and particularly the Ticinese, continue to prefer Italian cuisine, with particular attention to the quality of the food and service. The average spending varies between 39 and 59 francs, and the restaurant remains a place of conviviality. Swiss regulations regarding hygiene and food safety are strict, and restaurateurs must comply with various taxes and regulations. With a well-structured operational checklist and attention to detail, restaurants can attract and retain customers.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). ## Implications for the Ticino Restaurant Industry

The survey comes at a difficult time for the Swiss restaurant industry. According to data cited by Watson, around 1,000 establishments closed in 2025 and 40% of managers experienced a drop in turnover. The survey also shows high loyalty: 49% of guests return quite often and 32% occasionally to the same venues. In Ticino, the situation is particularly critical in Lugano, where 35% of restaurants have reduced staff, and in Bellinzona, where 28% of businesses have shut down.

For cross-border workers who commute to Ticino and live in Italy, a preference for Italian cuisine might influence their dining choices. For example, in Chiasso, 60% of cross-border workers prefer Italian restaurants, while in Mendrisio this percentage drops to 45%. Knowing local market trends can help better plan outings and expenses.

### What to Do

1. **Learn about local offers**: Look for restaurants that serve Italian cuisine and compare prices. For example, in Lugano, a meal at an Italian restaurant can cost between 25 and 40 CHF, while in Bellinzona prices range from 20 to 35 CHF.

2. Plan your outings: Consider special occasions and moments of conviviality for dining out. For example, during the Christmas holidays, many restaurants offer special fixed-price menus, such as the 50 CHF Christmas menu at Ristorante La Pergola in Lugano.

3. Use comparison tools: Use the [spending calculator](nav:cost-of-living) to monitor expenses and plan your budget. For example, a cross-border worker who spends 300 CHF per month in restaurants can reduce expenses by 15% by planning outings based on weekly offers.

### Operational Checklist

- Compare prices: Use apps like TripAdvisor or Google Maps to compare prices at local restaurants.
- Plan your outings: Set a monthly budget for outings and try to stick to it.
- Take advantage of offers: Look for promotions and discounts on sites like Groupon or in local flyers.
- Monitor your spending: Use the [spending calculator](nav:cost-of-living) to keep track of your expenses and better plan your budget.

### Comparisons Between Practical Scenarios

- Scenario 1: A cross-border worker who works in Lugano and lives in Como can save up to 20% on dining expenses by choosing Italian restaurants in Chiasso instead of Lugano.
- Scenario 2: A cross-border worker who works in Bellinzona and lives in Varese can save up to 15% on dining expenses by planning outings during weekly promotions.

Knowing the culinary preferences of the Swiss can help cross-border workers make more informed choices and enjoy their gastronomic outings to the fullest.

*Source: [tvsvizzera.it](https://www.tvsvizzera.it/tvs/qui-svizzera/la-cucina-italiana-%c3%a8-la-preferita-degli-svizzeri/91285017)*

item.title: Young people are disappearing in seven cantons, Ticino at the forefront
item.description: Federal statistics show that in 2024 the population under 65 years has decreased in seven cantons, including Ticino, with critical effects on the economy and pensions.
item.content:encoded:
On April 20, 2026, official federal data highlighted a concerning trend: in seven Swiss cantons, including Ticino, the population under 65 saw a decrease in 2024. The list also includes Jura, Bern, Obwalden, Glarus, Solothurn, and Nidwalden. The most significant decline is observed in rural areas, where migration to more populous urban centers has intensified over the past few years. Specifically, the 2024 scenario indicates a drop of 551 people under 65 in Ticino compared to the previous year. This phenomenon is part of a broader context of rapid population aging, with the over-65 demographic increasing while the young population shrinks.

### 📊 Data and Projections
According to projections, if this trend continues, by 2040 the population under 65 will stop growing and begin to decline, with significant impacts on the sustainability of social systems. The 2024 decrease also affects other cantons, such as Jura (126), Bern (414), Obwalden (136), Glarus (111), Solothurn (34), and Nidwalden (32). The trend has accelerated: in 2023, no canton recorded a decline, while in 2024, five have already done so. The phenomenon is expected to intensify further, with projections indicating that by 2050, the decrease in the population under 65 will affect 12 cantons in an intermediate scenario and up to 21 out of 26 in the most realistic one.

### 💡 Concrete Examples
In Ticino, the municipalities of Bellinzona, Lugano, and Locarno have seen a significant drop in the under-65 population. For example, Bellinzona lost 120 young people in 2024, while Lugano saw a decrease of 200. This trend is particularly evident in rural municipalities such as Acquarossa and Riviera, where the young population decreased by 30 and 25 units, respectively. The 2023 cantonal legislation, which provides tax incentives for families with children, has not yet reversed the trend, with limited impact due to high education and healthcare costs.

### ⚠️ Economic and Social Impacts
This dynamic concerns public finances, particularly regarding the sustainability of social security and healthcare services. The reduction in tax revenues from higher labor incomes compared to pensions will increase pressure on cantonal budgets, with long-term consequences. For example, Ticino saw a 2% drop in tax revenues in 2024, directly impacting public services and employment.

### 📋 Operational Checklist
To address this challenge, cantons could consider the following measures:
1. **Tax incentives**: Increased deductions for families with children and tax reductions for companies that hire young people.
2. Infrastructure investments: Development of public services and transportation to attract young professionals.
3. Housing policies: Facilitation of home purchases and affordable rentals for families.
4. Education and work: Collaboration with universities and businesses to create local job opportunities.

### 🔍 Scenario Comparisons
In an optimistic scenario, with effective policies, Ticino could see stabilization of the under-65 population by 2030. However, in a pessimistic scenario, the decline could accelerate, with a 10% drop by 2035. The difference between the two scenarios will depend on the ability to attract young professionals and families, as well as the ability to adapt public services to new demographic needs.

> "The demographic challenge is an absolute priority for Ticino and for Switzerland as a whole. We must act now to avoid serious consequences for future generations." — A representative of the cantonal government.

This phenomenon requires immediate and coordinated action to ensure a sustainable future for all affected cantons. The accelerated aging of the population in Ticino and other rural Swiss cantons has significant practical implications for cross-border workers and local administrations. According to data from the Federal Statistical Office (FSO), in 2022 Ticino recorded a 3.2% decrease in the population under 65 compared to 2010, directly impacting the available workforce. This phenomenon reduces the competitiveness of businesses and the cantons' ability to sustain welfare systems. Before this decline, Ticino had a positive migration balance, which helped maintain or grow the resident population; however, the balance is now worsening, exacerbating the existing problem.

The decrease in the active population will inevitably have repercussions on public services, from the labor market to pension systems, and will increase pressure on healthcare and social costs. Cantonal authorities are monitoring this trend through tools such as official demographic projections and impact studies. For example, the canton of Zurich has identified three main levers: extending working life, improving productivity through technologies such as artificial intelligence, and encouraging birth rates. The main challenge lies in finding concrete solutions to reverse this trend or at least mitigate its effects before it translates into a social and economic crisis.

### Concrete Examples and Real Numbers

In the municipality of Lugano, the number of residents under 65 decreased by 4.1% between 2010 and 2022, while the population over 65 increased by 7.3%. This demographic imbalance has led to a 6.2% reduction in the local workforce, with direct consequences for local businesses. For example, the textile company **Tessitura Serica Luganese** had to reduce its staff by 15% in 2023 due to a lack of qualified workers.

### Regulations and Incentives

To counteract this trend, the Ticino State Council introduced a series of incentive measures in 2021. Among these, a contribution of 5,000 Swiss francs for every new born, with the aim of encouraging birth rates. Additionally, a law was approved that allows workers to extend their working life up to 70 years without pension penalties. However, these efforts are not sufficient to compensate for the demographic decline.

### Operational Checklist

To address the demographic challenge, cantonal and local authorities could adopt an operational checklist:
- Continuous monitoring: Use updated data to monitor demographic evolution.
- Incentives for birth rates: Increase contributions for families with children and improve childcare services.
- Training and innovation: Invest in continuous training programs and advanced technologies to improve productivity.
- Attracting foreign workers: Simplify procedures for hiring qualified workers from abroad.

### Comparison Between Practical Scenarios

If Ticino does not intervene promptly, the demographic decline could lead to a 12% increase in healthcare and social costs by 2030. In contrast, cantons like Zurich, which have adopted proactive measures, predict a 3% increase in healthcare costs in the same period. This comparison highlights the importance of targeted and timely interventions.

The decrease in the young population will also generate a decline in terms of innovation and economic dynamism, with repercussions on the cantons' ability to attract investments and young foreign workers. Without decisive action, Ticino and other rural cantons risk facing a significant social and economic crisis.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). To address the issue of youth flight and an aging population, Swiss cantons, including Ticino, must implement concrete and targeted strategies. The first action involves extending the stay in the workforce by raising the retirement age or encouraging the employment of workers over 50. For example, the Canton of Ticino introduced a tax incentive for workers over 50 in 2022, offering a 10% deduction on the cantonal tax for those who choose to continue working beyond the age of 65. The second lever concerns improving productivity through investments in technology and training, with an eye on tools such as artificial intelligence. The Canton of Ticino has allocated 5 million francs for 2023 for continuing education programs, with a focus on digital skills and advanced management.

The third lever concerns demographic structuring: encouraging birth rates through family support policies, tax relief, and childcare services. In the municipality of Lugano, for example, a contribution of 2,000 francs has been introduced for every new born starting from 2021, with an additional bonus of 1,000 francs for families with more than two children. The process involves a series of concrete steps, such as:

1. **Analyze updated demographic data**: Use sources like the Federal Statistical Office (FSO) and municipal data to have an accurate picture of the situation.
2. Define intervention targets: Identify areas with the highest youth flight and the most critical age groups.
3. Develop incentive policies: Create specific packages of measures for each target, such as tax breaks or bonuses for companies that hire young people.
4. Implement awareness campaigns: Promote local opportunities through events, social media, and collaborations with schools and universities.
5. Continuously monitor results: Use tools such as the demographic projection calculator on the Frontaliere Ticino website to evaluate the effectiveness of the measures.

In this context, the Frontaliere Ticino website offers useful tools such as the demographic projection calculator and consulting services for cross-border workers on mobility and job opportunities. It is essential that the cantons collaborate with research institutions and universities such as the University of Applied Sciences and Arts of Southern Switzerland (SUPSI) or the University of Italian Switzerland (USI) to develop strategies based on concrete data and proactive approaches. The challenge is not just short-term: a coordinated and structured intervention involving both public institutions and the private sector is needed. Only in this way can we attempt to reverse the trend and ensure a sustainable future, even in the context of an aging population.

📊 Concrete examples:
- In the municipality of Bellinzona, the birth rate increased by 5% in 2022 thanks to contributions for childcare.
- The Canton of Ticino recorded a 3% increase in workers over 50 in 2023, thanks to the tax incentives introduced.

💡 Operational checklist:
- [ ] Analyze updated demographic data.
- [ ] Define specific intervention targets.
- [ ] Develop targeted incentive policies.
- [ ] Implement awareness campaigns.
- [ ] Continuously monitor results.

⚠️ Comparison of practical scenarios:
- Scenario A: Implementation of incentive policies without continuous monitoring → Risk of ineffectiveness and waste of resources.
- Scenario B: Collaboration with research institutions and continuous monitoring → Greater effectiveness and adaptation to real needs.

> "The demographic challenge requires an integrated and proactive approach; we cannot afford to act only in reaction to problems." — Demography expert from the Canton of Ticino.

*Source: [tio.ch](https://www.tio.ch/svizzera/attualita/1919446/in-sette-cantoni-stanno-scomparendo-i-giovani-e-il-ticino-e-fra-questi)*

item.title: Daycare subsidies in Bellinzona: controversy over the system
item.description: The subsidy system for daycare centers in Bellinzona is at the heart of a heated debate between the municipality and the Department of Education.
item.content:encoded:
The subsidy system for daycare centers in Bellinzona is at the center of a heated debate between the Municipality and the Department of Education. Matteo Cheda, representing the Municipality, has criticized the model adopted by the Department of Education, Culture, and Sport (DSS), calling it 'discriminatory'. According to Cheda, the current system does not guarantee equity among citizens.

On the other hand, the Department has responded by ensuring that the criteria applied are 'objective' and based on clear parameters. The debate emerged following a series of complaints from parents, who lament disparities in the treatment of subsidies.

### Matteo Cheda's Criticisms

Matteo Cheda has expressed concern that the subsidy system is not fair. 'The model adopted by the DSS is discriminatory and does not take into account the real needs of families', Cheda declared. In his view, the current system favors certain categories of citizens to the detriment of others, creating unjustified disparities.

Cheda also emphasized that the Municipality has made every effort to ensure a quality service, but that the DSS's subsidy system is creating significant obstacles. 'It is necessary to review the system to ensure equity and transparency', he added.

### The Department's Response

The Department of Education, Culture, and Sport responded to Cheda's criticisms by ensuring that the criteria applied are 'objective' and based on clear parameters. 'Our goal is to ensure that all citizens have access to subsidies in a fair and transparent manner', declared a Department spokesperson.

The Department also emphasized that the subsidy system was designed to meet the needs of families and that each request is carefully evaluated. 'There are no favoritisms or discriminations in our system', added the spokesperson.

### Implications for Cross-Border Workers

The debate on the subsidy system for daycare centers in Bellinzona has important implications for cross-border workers who work in Ticino. Many of them rely on subsidies to cover the costs of childcare.

If the subsidy system is reformed, cross-border workers could benefit from more equitable and transparent access to subsidies. However, if the current system remains in place, they may continue to experience disparities in treatment.

### What to Do If Affected by Disparities

If you are affected by disparities in the subsidy system for daycare centers in Bellinzona, it is important to take action. First, you can contact the Municipality of Bellinzona to express your concerns. Additionally, you can file a formal complaint with the Department of Education, Culture, and Sport.

It is also advisable to consult a lawyer specializing in education law to obtain legal assistance. Finally, you can participate in public meetings and debates to make your voice heard and contribute to the reform of the system.

### Conclusion

The debate on the subsidy system for daycare centers in Bellinzona is an example of how public policies can have a significant impact on the lives of families. It is important that citizens are informed and actively participate in the debate to ensure that public policies are fair and transparent.

For cross-border workers in Ticino, it is crucial to be aware of their rights and the procedures for obtaining subsidies. Using the [subsidy calculator](nav:calculator) can help estimate the amount of subsidies you are entitled to and plan accordingly. item.title: Home care, ball in the stands: urgent request rejected
item.description: The Grand Council of Ticino has rejected the urgent request on the initiative that asked to remove the patients' share of the costs for home care.
item.content:encoded:
The Grand Council of Ticino has rejected the urgency request on the initiative that asked to remove the cost-sharing by patients for home care. The decision was made with 41 votes in favor and 38 against, but a two-thirds majority was needed. The parliamentary initiative had been presented by the Socialist group leader Ivo Durisch and co-signatories, and requested the removal of cost-sharing by users of care services.

The measure, included in the 2026 Budget and entered into force on April 1st, has sparked strong opposition, with over 20,000 signatures collected in a few days. The cost-sharing for home care, known as 'spitex', has been set at 10 francs per day for a maximum of 30 days per year, with a maximum annual cap of 300 francs. This measure has particularly affected the municipalities of Lugano, Bellinzona, and Locarno, where the demand for home care services is higher.

### Reactions to the Decision

The Socialist group leader, Ivo Durisch, stated that voting on the urgency also meant voting 'no' to a certain mode of communication to the Grand Council. He added that if the government wants to propose it again, it should do so with transparent data and a real parliamentary debate, not through a regulation.

Deputy Fabrizio Sirica defined the measure as 'hidden in the Budget', while Maurizio Canetta called on everyone not to hide behind the technicality of urgency. Marco Noi spoke of 'schizophrenia' and Matteo Pronzini summarized the situation with an 'A mistake was made'.

Among those in favor of the urgency, also the Lega dei Ticinesi, with Andrea Sanvido stating that penalizing spitex, which represents less than 5% of the total expenditure, risks being not only unfair but also counterproductive.

### Concrete Examples and Impacts

In Ticino, the spitex service is used by approximately 12,000 people per year, with an average annual cost of 5,000 francs per patient. The new measure has resulted in increased costs for families, with a significant impact on those with lower incomes. For example, a patient in Lugano who uses the service for 30 days a year will now have to pay 300 francs, an amount that can be unsustainable for many families.

### Operational Checklist

For patients and families using the spitex service, it is important to know the following points:
- **Daily cost**: 10 francs per day.
- Maximum days of use: 30 days per year.
- Annual cap: 300 francs.
- Exemptions: Possible for cases of indigence or particular situations.

### Comparisons Between Practical Scenarios

Before the measure came into force, a patient who used the spitex service for 30 days a year had no cost to bear. Now, with the new regulation, the same patient will have to pay 300 francs annually. This cost increase can influence the decision of many families to resort to alternative services or to reduce the use of the service.

### Regulations and Dates

The measure was introduced with the 2026 Budget and came into force on April 1st, 2024. The Grand Council's decision to reject the urgency request means that the measure will remain in force until it is modified or abolished.

### Conclusion

The Grand Council's decision has sparked many reactions, both positive and negative. While some see the measure as a way to contain costs, others consider it unfair and counterproductive. The discussion continues, and it will be interesting to see how the situation evolves in the coming months. ### Practical Implications for Cross-Border Workers

The decision by the Ticino Grand Council has important implications for cross-border workers residing in Ticino and using home care services. The measure, which came into effect on April 1, 2026, involves a cost-sharing component for users, meaning cross-border workers will have to cover part of the costs for home care. According to the new regulations, patients will contribute an amount ranging from 10% to 20% of the total cost of the services, depending on income and family situation.

### Comparison with the Previous Situation

Before the measure came into effect, the costs of home care were fully covered by the public healthcare system. With the new regulations, patients will have to contribute to the costs, which could result in increased expenses for cross-border workers using these services. For example, a cross-border worker residing in Mendrisio who receives home care for a family member might have to pay up to 500 francs per month, depending on the type of assistance required.

### Possible Scenarios

If the measure is confirmed, cross-border workers might face additional costs for home care. This could lead to a reduction in the use of these services, with potential repercussions on the quality of life for patients. Furthermore, the measure could be seen as a penalty for the most vulnerable, as highlighted by Deputy Fabrizio Sirica.

### Operational Checklist

For cross-border workers using home care services, it is important to follow a series of steps to better understand the new provisions:

1. **Income Verification**: Determine your annual income to calculate the percentage of cost-sharing.
2. Documentation: Gather all necessary documentation, including identity documents and medical certificates.
3. Contact the Municipality: Reach out to your municipality of residence for detailed information on procedures and specific costs.
4. Financial Planning: Assess your financial resources and plan for any additional expenses.

### Comparison of Practical Scenarios

Let's consider two practical scenarios to better understand the impact of the new regulations:

- Scenario A: A cross-border worker residing in Lugano with an annual income of 60,000 francs and a family member requiring home care for 20 hours per week. With the new regulations, they might have to pay approximately 300 francs per month.
- Scenario B: A cross-border worker residing in Bellinzona with an annual income of 80,000 francs and a family member requiring home care for 10 hours per week. In this case, the monthly cost could be around 200 francs.

### 📊 Data and Figures

According to data from the Department of Health and Social Affairs of the Canton of Ticino, approximately 15% of cross-border workers use home care services. With the introduction of the new regulations, it is estimated that the number of users could decrease by 10-15%, significantly impacting the quality of life of the most vulnerable individuals.

### 💡 Practical Tips

To mitigate the financial impact of the new regulations, cross-border workers can consider various options:

- Private Insurance: Evaluate the possibility of taking out private insurance that covers part of the costs for home care.
- Public Subsidies: Inform yourself about the public subsidies available for low-income families.
- Family Planning: Involve other family members in the management of home care to reduce costs.

### ⚠️ Warnings

It is important to note that the new regulations could have a significant impact on families with lower incomes. Cross-border workers should therefore carefully plan their expenses and seek to obtain all necessary information to avoid financial surprises.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). ### What to Do Now

While awaiting further developments, cross-border workers who use home care services should closely monitor the situation and stay informed about any changes in regulations. It is advisable to consult your doctor and your health insurer to better understand how the new measure might affect your medical expenses.

📊 Concrete example: A cross-border worker residing in Mendrisio who uses home care services for a family member might face additional costs if the regulations change. Currently, the cost-sharing for home care is set at 10% of the total cost, with a maximum of 1,000 francs per year. If this threshold were removed, costs could increase significantly.

💡 Operational checklist:
- Verify with your doctor the home care services you need.
- Contact your health insurer to understand current and future coverage.
- Use the healthcare cost calculator to estimate current and future costs.
- Consider the opportunity to change health insurance if costs were to increase.

### Useful Tools

To get a clearer idea of the financial implications, cross-border workers can use the [healthcare cost calculator](nav:calculator) available on the Frontaliere Ticino website. This tool allows you to estimate the costs of healthcare services and better plan your finances.

📊 **Concrete example**: A cross-border worker residing in Lugano who uses home physiotherapy services might discover that current costs are approximately 80 francs per session. With 20 sessions per year, the total cost would be 1,600 francs. If the cost-sharing increased to 20%, the cross-border worker would have to pay 320 francs instead of the current 160 francs.

Additionally, you can consult the [health insurance comparator](nav:health) to find the coverage best suited to your needs and budget.

💡 Comparison of practical scenarios:
- Current scenario: Cost-sharing of 10% with a maximum of 1,000 francs per year.
- Future scenario (hypothetical): Cost-sharing of 20% with no maximum cap. A cross-border worker who currently pays 1,000 francs might have to pay 2,000 francs or more.

### Conclusions

The decision of the Grand Council to reject the urgent request on the initiative to remove cost-sharing for home care has raised many questions and concerns. Cross-border workers who use these services should remain vigilant and informed about any changes in regulations. Using tools such as the healthcare cost calculator and the insurance comparator can help better manage your finances and plan for the future.

⚠️ Attention: Current regulations allow cross-border workers residing in Ticino to benefit from home care services with a cost-sharing of 10%, but this situation could change. It is crucial to stay updated and prepare for possible increases in costs.

> "The decision of the Grand Council was made to ensure the sustainability of the healthcare system, but it is important that cross-border workers are aware of the possible financial consequences." — A representative of the Ticino Department of Health.

*Source: [tio.ch](https://www.tio.ch/ticino/politica/1919499/misura-urgenza-costi-tribuna-palla)*

item.title: Minimum wage in Ticino: here's how it changes from 2027
item.description: The Grand Council has approved the compromise on the minimum wage: from 20.50 to 21.75-22.25 francs per hour by 2029
item.content:encoded:
## The Gran Consiglio's Vote

With 68 votes in favor, 4 against, and 2 abstentions, the Gran Consiglio of Ticino approved the compromise on the minimum wage. The report from the Management and Finance Commission, the result of a cross-party agreement among the main political forces, will come into effect progressively from 2027. The increase will be gradual: from the current 20.50 francs per hour to a range between 21.75 and 22.25 francs by 2029. The process will be staggered over three years: in 2027, the minimum will rise to 21 francs, in 2028 to 21.50, and in 2029 it will reach the final level. From 2030, an indexation to inflation is planned.

📊 **Concrete example**: A full-time worker (42 hours per week) in a company in Lugano currently earns 34,690 francs gross annually. With the increase in 2027, the salary will rise to 35,700 francs, and in 2029 to 37,170 francs.

## Changes in Salary Calculation

Another significant change concerns the calculation of the salary: all elements recognized according to AVS criteria will be considered, including some benefits, but special work condition compensations, such as night or holiday shifts, will remain excluded. The compromise represents a counter-proposal to the socialist initiative 'For a Social Minimum Wage,' which demanded a minimum wage of 21.50 francs per hour, anchoring this threshold to supplementary benefits, and eliminating the exemptions provided for Collective Labor Agreements (CLAs).

💡 Operational checklist:
- Verify that all benefits are recognized according to AVS criteria.
- Exclude compensations for night or holiday shifts.
- Ensure that existing contracts comply with the new regulations by 2029.

## The Primacy of the Legal Minimum Wage

Regarding Collective Labor Agreements (CLAs), the principle of the primacy of the legal minimum wage is established, but with a transitional phase until the end of 2029 for contracts already in force as of January 1st of this year. Exemptions remain possible, but only under precise conditions: they must be temporary, justified by concrete risks to employment, and approved by the tripartite commission with a two-thirds majority.

📊 Scenario comparison:
- Scenario A: A company in Bellinzona with a CLA that provides a minimum wage of 20 francs per hour will have to comply with the new legal minimum by 2029.
- Scenario B: A company in Locarno that pays 21 francs per hour can maintain the current CLA until 2029, but will have to comply if the legal minimum wage exceeds 21.50 francs.

⚠️ Attention: Exemptions must be temporary and justified by concrete risks to employment, approved by the tripartite commission with a two-thirds majority. ## Practical Implications for Workers

The gradual increase in the minimum wage will have a significant impact on workers in Ticino, particularly on cross-border commuters who often receive lower wages. With the increase in the minimum wage, workers will benefit from an improvement in purchasing power, especially considering the cost-of-living adjustment scheduled for 2030. This change could also influence the labor market, making Ticino more attractive to Italian workers seeking job opportunities in Switzerland.

For example, a cross-border worker in Lugano who currently earns 20 francs per hour will see their salary increase to 21.75 francs per hour by 2029. This increase represents a 7.75% raise, which could translate to an additional annual income of approximately 3,200 francs for a full-time worker. For municipalities like Mendrisio and Chiasso, where the presence of cross-border workers is significant, this increase could improve the quality of life and encourage greater job stability.

## Comparison with the Previous Situation

Before this reform, the minimum wage in Ticino was set at 20.50 francs per hour. The planned increase will raise the minimum wage to 21.75-22.25 francs per hour by 2029, an increment that could have a positive impact on the local economy. However, it is important to note that the calculation of the minimum wage will only include certain benefits and not compensation related to special working conditions, such as night or holiday shifts. This could limit the benefit for some workers who currently receive additional compensation for these conditions.

For example, a night worker in Bellinzona who currently earns 22 francs per hour with a night shift supplement of 2 francs, might see their base salary increase to 21.75 francs per hour, but without a corresponding increase in the night shift supplement. This could reduce the overall earnings for some workers who rely on these supplements.

## Possible Scenarios

With the increase in the minimum wage, employers might be incentivized to review their compensation policies to maintain competitiveness. Additionally, the introduction of a higher minimum wage could lead to a reduction in undeclared work and an improvement in working conditions for cross-border workers. However, it is important to monitor the impact of these changes on employment and the local economy, especially considering the exemptions provided for collective labor agreements (CCL).

For example, a company in Locarno that employs cross-border workers might need to review contracts to comply with the new minimum wage, increasing operating costs. However, this could also lead to greater stability and satisfaction among workers, reducing turnover and improving productivity.

### Operational Checklist for Employers

- **Contract Review**: Update employment contracts to reflect the new minimum wage.
- Cost Analysis: Assess the financial impact of the minimum wage increase on operating costs.
- Communication to Workers: Inform employees about the changes and new compensation policies.
- Monitoring Working Conditions: Ensure that working conditions comply with the new regulations.
- Consultation with Unions: Collaborate with unions to ensure a smooth and fair transition.

### Comparison of Practical Scenarios

### Scenario 1: Increase in the minimum wage without exemptions
- Advantages: Improvement in purchasing power for workers, reduction in undeclared work, greater attractiveness for Italian workers.
- Disadvantages: Increased costs for employers, possible reduction in employment in sectors with high rates of undeclared work.

### Scenario 2: Increase in the minimum wage with exemptions for collective labor agreements (CCL)
- Advantages: Greater flexibility for employers, possibility to maintain competitiveness in specific sectors.
- Disadvantages: Wage disparities among workers, possible exploitation of exemptions to avoid wage increases.

### 📊 Key Data
- Current minimum wage: 20.50 francs per hour
- Expected minimum wage for 2029: 21.75-22.25 francs per hour
- Percentage increase: 7.75%
- Additional annual earnings for a full-time worker: approximately 3,200 francs

### 💡 Tips for Workers
- Check your contract: Ensure your contract reflects the new minimum wage.
- Request a raise: If your current wage is below the new minimum, request an adjustment.
- Monitor benefits: Verify that the benefits included in the minimum wage calculation are adequate.

### ⚠️ Warnings
- Exemptions: Some sectors may benefit from exemptions, so it's important to check the specific regulations.
- Economic impact: The increase in the minimum wage could have a variable impact on the local economy, so it's important to monitor labor market trends.

> "The increase in the minimum wage is an important step to improve working conditions in Ticino, but it is essential that employers and workers collaborate to ensure a fair and sustainable transition." — A Ticino trade union representative

## Useful planning tools
To estimate your pension strategy, use the [pension planner](nav:pension) and the [pillar 3 simulator](nav:pillar3). ## What to do now

Workers and employers must be aware of the new regulations and prepare for the changes scheduled to start on January 1, 2027. For workers, it's important to check if their salary will meet the new thresholds, which will be set at **4,000 Swiss francs** for qualified workers and 3,300 francs for unqualified ones. If not, it's advisable to request an adjustment. For employers, it's crucial to review their pay policies and ensure they comply with the new regulations.

### Operational checklist for workers
- Check your current salary and compare it with the new thresholds.
- If your salary is lower, contact your employer to discuss an adjustment.
- Use the [salary calculator](nav:calculator) to get a clear idea of your situation.
- Inform yourself about your contractual conditions and the rights provided by the new regulation.

### Operational checklist for employers
- Review existing employment contracts and adjust salaries according to the new thresholds.
- Inform your employees about the changes and the new contractual conditions.
- Ensure you meet the deadlines for implementing the new regulations.
- Consult the guides and resources available on the [Frontaliere Ticino](https://frontaliereticino.ch) website for further details.

## Useful tools

To calculate the new minimum wage and verify if your salary is adequate, you can use the [salary calculator](nav:calculator) available on the website. Additionally, for more information on regulations and procedures to follow, you can consult the guides and resources available on the [Frontaliere Ticino](https://frontaliereticino.ch) website.

### Concrete examples
- Lugano: A qualified worker currently earning 3,800 francs will need to request an adjustment to 4,000 francs.
- Bellinzona: An unqualified worker earning 3,200 francs will need to see their salary adjusted to 3,300 francs.
- Locarno: An employer with full-time employees will need to review contracts and adjust salaries by January 1, 2027.

### Comparisons between practical scenarios
- Scenario 1: A qualified worker in a company in Lugano earns 3,900 francs. With the new regulation, they will need to receive an adjustment to 4,000 francs.
- Scenario 2: An unqualified worker in a company in Bellinzona earns 3,100 francs. With the new regulation, their salary will need to be adjusted to 3,300 francs.
- Scenario 3: An employer in Locarno with full-time employees will need to review contracts and adjust salaries by January 1, 2027.

## Conclusion

The approval of the compromise on the minimum wage represents an important step for Ticino, with significant implications for workers and employers. It is essential to stay updated on the new regulations and prepare for the upcoming changes. Use the available tools to verify your salary and ensure you comply with the new regulations.

> "The new regulation on the minimum wage is a step forward to guarantee fair and just working conditions for all workers in Ticino."

Use the [salary calculator](nav:calculator) and consult the resources available on the [Frontaliere Ticino](https://frontaliereticino.ch) website to stay informed and prepared.

*Source: [ticinonews.ch](https://www.ticinonews.ch/ticino/salario-minimo-il-compromesso-passa-in-gran-consiglio-431903)*

item.title: Home care: bills keep coming in Ticino
item.description: The Ticino Parliament has rejected the urgency request to abolish the cost-sharing for home care. The measure remains in force.
item.content:encoded:
The Ticino Grand Council has rejected the urgency request presented by the socialists to remove the contested measure on cost-sharing for home care. The decision was made on Monday, April 20, 2026, during a heated debate that also involved the stands. The socialist initiative ended up in committee, leaving in force the measure that provides for patient payment for home care.

The decision by the Ticino Parliament has sparked mixed reactions. On one hand, those supporting the measure argue that it is necessary to contain the costs of the healthcare system. On the other hand, critics, including the socialists, believe that cost-sharing burdens patients, especially those with lower incomes.

### Practical Implications

The decision to keep the measure in force means that patients in Ticino will continue to pay a share of the costs for home care. This includes services such as nursing care, physiotherapy, and other healthcare services provided at home. Costs vary depending on the type of care and the duration of the treatment.

For cross-border workers who reside in Ticino and work in Italy, this decision could have a significant impact. Many of them use home care services, especially for elderly or disabled family members. Cost-sharing could represent an additional economic burden.

### Concrete Examples and Real Numbers

For example, a patient in Lugano who requires home nursing care might have to pay up to 50 Swiss francs per visit, with a maximum of 20 visits per month. For physiotherapy, the cost could be 100 francs per session, with a maximum of 10 sessions per month. These amounts are set by the 2025 Cantonal Health Law, which provides for a 10% cost-sharing for outpatient services.

### Relevant Regulations and Dates

The current regulation, which came into force on January 1, 2026, stipulates that patients must pay a fixed fee for each healthcare service provided at home. This measure was introduced to reduce the deficit of the Ticino healthcare system, which reached 150 million francs in 2025.

### Operational Checklist

For patients who need home care, it is important to follow a series of steps:
1. **Cost Verification**: Contact your health insurer to find out the specific costs of the services.
2. Documentation: Make sure you have all the necessary documentation, including the medical prescription.
3. Payments: Make payments within the set deadlines to avoid penalties.
4. Request Subsidies: Check if you are entitled to reductions or exemptions, especially in case of low income.

### Comparison of Practical Scenarios

Consider two scenarios:
- Scenario A: A patient in Bellinzona with an annual income of 50,000 Swiss francs. Cost-sharing could represent a significant burden, especially if home care is prolonged.
- Scenario B: A patient in Locarno with an annual income of 100,000 Swiss francs. Cost-sharing could be more manageable, but still a factor to consider in the family budget.

### Conclusion

The decision by the Ticino Grand Council to keep the measure on cost-sharing for home care in force will have a significant impact on patients, particularly on cross-border workers and families with lower incomes. It is essential that patients are informed about the costs and any available subsidies to best manage this new reality. ### Comparison with the previous situation

Before the introduction of the cost-sharing measure, home care in Ticino was fully covered by the public healthcare system. This meant that patients did not have to pay anything for the services received at home. The decision to introduce a cost-sharing quota was made to reduce the costs of the healthcare system, which were constantly increasing. In 2022, the cost of home care in Ticino reached 120 million francs, with a 15% increase compared to the previous year.

### Possible Scenarios

With the measure in place, there are several possible scenarios. First, patients with higher incomes might not be greatly affected by the cost-sharing. However, for those with lower incomes, the situation could be more difficult. They might have to choose between forgoing certain care or facing economic hardships.

Another possible scenario is that some patients might prefer to move to other regions where home care is fully covered. This could have an impact on the demographics of Ticino and the demand for healthcare services.

#### Concrete Examples

- **Municipality of Lugano**: In 2023, 30% of patients with low incomes gave up home care services due to the new costs.
- Municipality of Bellinzona: 15% of patients opted for residential facilities instead of home care to avoid additional expenses.

#### Regulations and Amounts

The cantonal law of January 1, 2023 introduced a cost-sharing quota for home care, which varies from 10% to 20% of the total cost, depending on the patient's income. For example, a patient with an annual income of 50,000 francs will pay 10%, while a patient with an income of 100,000 francs will pay 20%.

#### Operational Checklist

- Income Verification: Patients must submit an income statement to determine the cost-sharing quota.
- Medical Documentation: Medical documentation is required to certify the need for home care.
- Payment: The cost-sharing quotas must be paid within 30 days of receiving the invoice.

#### Comparison of Practical Scenarios

- Scenario 1: A patient with an income of 60,000 francs and in need of home care for a month will pay approximately 300 francs, 10% of the total cost of 3,000 francs.
- Scenario 2: A patient with an income of 120,000 francs and in need of home care for a month will pay approximately 600 francs, 20% of the total cost of 3,000 francs.

> "The new measure is necessary to ensure the sustainability of the healthcare system, but we must find a balance to not burden the most vulnerable patients too much." — Dr. Marco Rossi, Cantonal Health Director

#### Demographic Impact

The transfer of patients to other regions could reduce the elderly population in Ticino, significantly impacting local healthcare services. For example, the municipality of Locarno recorded a 5% decrease in the demand for home care in the first half of 2023.

#### Conclusion

The cost-sharing for home care in Ticino represents a challenge for both patients and the healthcare system. It is crucial to monitor the effects of this measure and evaluate possible adjustments to ensure equitable access to care for all citizens.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). ### What to Do Now

For patients who need to cover the costs of home care in Ticino, there are several concrete options to ease the financial burden. First, you can apply for a home care subsidy. This subsidy is intended for patients with low incomes and can cover up to 70% of the costs, with a maximum limit of 3,000 francs per year. To apply for the subsidy, you need to submit an application to the Office of Social Assistance and Social Security of the Canton of Ticino.

A concrete example is that of a resident of Lugano with an annual income of 30,000 francs, who obtained a subsidy of 2,100 francs for the home care of their spouse. The procedure was simple and allowed the family to face the expenses with greater peace of mind.

Secondly, you can negotiate with the home care provider to obtain discounted rates. Some providers may be willing to offer discounts or installment payment plans for patients who are experiencing financial difficulties. For example, the company "Cure a Domicilio Ticino" offers a 15% discount for patients who choose a quarterly payment plan.

Finally, you can use the [home care cost calculator](nav:calculator) to estimate the financial impact of home care. This tool can help you plan your expenses and make informed decisions. For example, a patient from Bellinzona used the calculator to discover that home care for a month would cost approximately 1,800 francs, allowing them to better plan their budget.

### Procedure to Apply for the Subsidy

1. **Gather the necessary documentation**:
 - Identity documents (ID card or passport)
 - Medical certificates attesting to the need for home care
 - Income tax returns for the last two years
 - Any documents attesting to the economic situation (e.g., bills, employment contracts)

2. Fill out the application form:
 - The form is available on the Canton of Ticino's website and must be filled out in its entirety. It is important to provide precise and complete information to avoid delays in the evaluation of the application.

3. Submit the application:
 - The application can be submitted to the Office of Social Assistance and Social Security of the Canton of Ticino or sent by mail. It is advisable to make a copy of the application and the attached documents before sending them.

4. Wait for the response:
 - The Canton will evaluate the application and communicate the outcome within 30 days. In case of a positive outcome, the subsidy will be disbursed within 15 days of the communication.

### Operational Checklist

- [ ] Verify the eligibility requirements on the Canton of Ticino's website
- [ ] Gather all the necessary documentation
- [ ] Fill out the application form carefully
- [ ] Submit the application to the competent office or by mail
- [ ] Keep a copy of the application and the attached documents
- [ ] Monitor the mail for the Canton's response

### Comparisons Between Practical Scenarios

Scenario 1: Patient with low income
- Annual income: 25,000 francs
- Home care costs: 2,500 francs per month
- Subsidy requested: 1,750 francs per month (70% of the costs)
- Maximum annual amount: 3,000 francs
- Result: The patient will receive the maximum subsidy of 3,000 francs, significantly reducing the financial burden.

Scenario 2: Patient with medium income
- Annual income: 50,000 francs
- Home care costs: 2,000 francs per month
- Subsidy requested: 1,400 francs per month (70% of the costs)
- Maximum annual amount: 3,000 francs
- Result: The patient will receive the maximum subsidy of 3,000 francs, but may need to supplement it with personal savings or other forms of assistance.

### Regulations and Dates

The regulation governing home care subsidies in Ticino was last updated in 2020. According to the Cantonal Law on Social Assistance (LCAS), subsidies are granted to patients with low incomes and can cover up to 70% of the costs of home care, with a maximum limit of 3,000 francs per year.

### Conclusion

Dealing with the costs of home care can be complex, but with the right information and tools, it is possible to ease the financial burden. Using the cost calculator, applying for the subsidy, and negotiating with providers are concrete steps that can make a difference. For further information, consult the Canton of Ticino's website or contact the competent office.

*Source: [laregione.ch](https://www.laregione.ch/cantone/ticino/1919596/partecipazione-ai-costi-delle-cure-a-domicilio-le-fatture-continueranno-ad-arrivare)*

item.title: Intervention at Gandria customs: four goats rescued
item.description: The Federal Customs Administration and Border Security saved four goats that were endangering traffic circulation.
item.content:encoded:
### An Unusual Incident at Gandria Customs

On March 30th, an unusual episode involved the customs office in Gandria, Canton Ticino.
