According to the Federal Customs Administration and Border Security (FCABS), four goats attracted the attention of the border crossing staff. The goats, on two occasions, risked causing an accident, endangering traffic circulation.

The FCABS published a post on their social media channels, announcing the intervention. The customs teams intervened to secure the animals in a pen located under the customs parking area. After some checks, it was possible to trace the owner, who a few hours later arrived at the scene to retrieve his animals, safe and sound.

### Implications for Road Safety

This episode raises some questions about road safety near border crossings. The presence of domestic or wild animals can pose a risk to traffic, especially in high-traffic areas near borders. The timely intervention of the customs authorities prevented potential accidents, but it is important that pet owners are aware of the risks and take preventive measures to avoid similar situations.

### Intervention Procedures

The FCABS followed a standard procedure for managing such situations. First, the staff identified the danger and took the necessary measures to secure the animals. Subsequently, they initiated checks to trace the owner, thus ensuring the return of the animals to their rightful owner. This type of intervention requires close collaboration between customs authorities and local law enforcement to ensure everyone's safety.

### Comparisons with Similar Situations

Episodes like this are quite rare but not unheard of. On other occasions, domestic or wild animals have caused disruptions to traffic, especially in rural areas or near forested zones. However, the intervention at Gandria Customs represents a particular case due to its location in a high-traffic border area. This episode underscores the importance of constant vigilance and a prompt response from the competent authorities.

### Community Reactions

The news of the intervention sparked the interest of the local community, which appreciated the work of the customs authorities. Many residents expressed concern about road safety and called for increased controls in these areas. The episode also sparked discussions about the need to improve road safety infrastructure and raise awareness among pet owners about potential risks. ### Implications for cross-border workers

For cross-border workers who daily cross border checkpoints, incidents like the one at Gandria customs can represent an additional risk factor. The presence of domestic or wild animals can cause delays and inconveniences, especially during peak hours. It is important that cross-border workers are aware of these risks and adopt preventive measures, such as maintaining a safe distance from animals and respecting road signs.

### Road safety procedures

Customs authorities and local law enforcement have implemented various measures to ensure road safety near border checkpoints. Among these are more frequent checks, the installation of warning signs, and collaboration with pet owners to prevent dangerous situations. However, it is crucial that citizens also actively collaborate, reporting any risky situations and respecting the regulations in force.

### Future scenarios

In the future, it may be necessary to implement additional road safety measures, such as the installation of protective barriers or increased surveillance in critical areas. Furthermore, it could be useful to promote awareness campaigns for pet owners to prevent similar incidents. Collaboration between authorities and the local community will be fundamental to ensuring everyone's safety.

### Comparisons with other regions

In other border regions, such as the Canton of Grisons or the Canton of Valais, similar measures have been implemented to ensure road safety. However, each area has unique characteristics that require specific solutions. The experience of Gandria customs could provide useful insights for other regions to improve road safety and prevent accidents.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). ### What to do in case of accidents involving animals

In case of accidents or dangerous situations caused by domestic or wild animals, it is important to follow specific procedures. First of all, it is necessary to immediately contact the competent authorities, such as the local police or customs authorities. Subsequently, it is essential to provide all the necessary information to identify the owner of the animals and ensure their safe recovery.

### Deadlines and procedures

Customs authorities and local law enforcement have established standard procedures for managing such situations. It is important to respect deadlines and follow the instructions provided by the authorities to ensure everyone's safety. In case of doubts or need for further information, it is possible to contact the competent offices to receive assistance.

### Useful tools for cross-border workers

For cross-border workers who cross the border crossings daily, it is useful to use tools such as the travel distance calculator or the route comparator. These tools can help plan the journey in a safer and more efficient way, avoiding potential risks and inconveniences. Additionally, you can consult the practical guides available on the site to receive further information and useful tips.

For more details and to plan your trip safely, use the [travel distance calculator](nav:calculator) and consult the practical guides available on the site. In this way, you can avoid potential risks and ensure a safe and hassle-free journey.

*Source: [ticinonews.ch](https://www.ticinonews.ch/ticino/intervento-inatteso-alla-dogana-di-gandria-messe-in-salvo-quattro-capre-431871)*

item.title: Just Eat delivers Migros products in Ticino: over 10,000 items available
item.description: Since May 4th, Just Eat has been delivering Migros products in Ticino, Valais, and Geneva with delivery in less than an hour. Prices are identical to those in stores.
item.content:encoded:
## Just Eat delivers Migros products in Ticino: over 10,000 items available

Starting from May 4, 2026, residents of the cantons of Ticino, Valais, and Geneva will be able to order over 10,000 Migros products via the Just Eat app. This new home delivery service represents an important novelty for those who wish to purchase food and consumer goods without having to physically go to supermarkets. Delivery will take place within 60 minutes directly from Migros supermarkets, with prices identical to those practiced in stores, discounts included.

### How the service works

Orders will be prepared by Migros staff in their respective supermarkets and subsequently delivered by Just Eat couriers. Distribution will take place within a 12-kilometer radius, by car, e-bike, or bicycle. This service is made possible thanks to Just Eat's local logistics presence in Valais and Ticino, which allows for the integration of further partners in the gastronomy and retail sectors.

Just Eat, founded in 2007 under the name Eat.ch, now has 7,000 partners and 1.5 million active customers. The parent company Just Eat Takeaway.com, headquartered in Amsterdam, was acquired in 2025 by the Dutch internet holding company Prosus and is among the leading online delivery services globally.

### Concrete examples and real numbers

In Ticino, the Migros supermarkets in Lugano, Bellinzona, and Locarno will be among the first to offer this service. For example, an order of 50 Swiss francs (CHF) could include a package of pasta (CHF 2.90), a bottle of olive oil (CHF 8.50), a can of tuna (CHF 3.20), and a package of cookies (CHF 4.80), with delivery guaranteed within 60 minutes.

### Regulations and operational details

According to Swiss regulations, home delivery services must comply with local laws on the sale of food and beverages. Just Eat and Migros have collaborated to ensure that all delivered products comply with health and food safety regulations. Additionally, the service will be available from 8:00 AM to 10:00 PM, with a delivery cost of CHF 4.90 for orders under CHF 30.

### Operational checklist

- **Availability check**: Check if your municipality is covered by the service.
- Delivery time: Make sure you are at home during the delivery time.
- Payment: Use the Just Eat app to pay securely.
- Reviews: Leave a review to improve the service.

### Comparison of practical scenarios

Before this service, residents of Lugano had to physically go to the supermarket to purchase Migros products. Now, thanks to home delivery, they can save time and effort. For example, a 50 CHF order could take 30 minutes to go to and return from the supermarket, while with Just Eat, the time required is only that needed to place the order and receive the delivery.

> "This service represents a significant step forward for the convenience of our customers," said a Migros spokesperson.

### Conclusion

The new Just Eat and Migros delivery service offers a unique opportunity for residents of Ticino, Valais, and Geneva to purchase food and consumer goods quickly and conveniently. With over 10,000 items available and delivery guaranteed within 60 minutes, this service promises to revolutionize the way inhabitants of these regions do their shopping. ## Implications for cross-border workers and residents

This new delivery service represents an interesting opportunity for cross-border workers who commute to Switzerland and reside in Italy. The ability to purchase food and consumer goods via home delivery can simplify the management of time and daily expenses. Furthermore, delivery within 60 minutes allows for better planning of purchases and avoids long waits.

### Comparison with the previous situation

Before this initiative, cross-border workers and residents of Ticino had to physically go to supermarkets to purchase the necessary products. This could result in longer waiting times and greater difficulties in managing purchases, especially for those working unconventional hours. With the new delivery service, it will be possible to avoid these inconveniences and enjoy greater convenience.

### Practical scenarios

Imagine a cross-border worker who works in Lugano and resides in Varese. Before this initiative, they had to go to the supermarket during the weekend or after work, losing precious time. With the new delivery service, they can order the necessary products via the Just Eat app and receive them directly at home, saving time and effort. Additionally, the ability to choose between different delivery methods (car, e-bike, or bicycle) allows the service to be adapted to specific needs.

### Concrete examples

A cross-border worker who works in Bellinzona and resides in Como could order 10 items for a total of 80 Swiss francs (CHF). With the delivery service, they could save approximately 2 hours per week, time that could be dedicated to other activities. Furthermore, the ability to order fresh products such as fruit and vegetables directly from home is a significant advantage, especially for those with intense work schedules.

### Regulations and costs

According to Swiss regulations, cross-border workers can benefit from cross-border delivery services without having to pay customs duties for purchases under 300 CHF. This limit was introduced in 2021 to facilitate commercial exchanges between Switzerland and the European Union. However, it is important to note that food products must comply with Swiss health regulations.

### Operational checklist

1. **Registration**: Download the Just Eat app and register.
2. Product selection: Browse through the 10,000 available items.
3. Order: Add products to the cart and proceed to payment.
4. Delivery: Choose the preferred delivery method (car, e-bike, or bicycle).
5. Pickup: Receive the products at home within 60 minutes.

### Comparisons between practical scenarios

Before the delivery service, a cross-border worker who worked in Lugano and resided in Varese had to spend approximately 1 hour reaching the nearest supermarket, shopping, and returning home. With the new service, the same cross-border worker can order the necessary products in a few minutes and receive them at home, saving time and reducing the stress related to travel.

### Final considerations

The new delivery service represents a significant step forward for cross-border workers and residents of Ticino. The ability to purchase food and consumer goods via home delivery offers greater flexibility and convenience, improving the quality of life for those who work in Switzerland and reside in Italy.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). ## What to do to use the service

To use the new Just Eat and Migros delivery service in Ticino, you need to follow a few simple steps:

1. **Download** the Just Eat app on your smartphone, available for both iOS and Android.
2. Register or log in to your existing account. Registration is free and takes just a few minutes.
3. Select the "Supermarkets" category and choose Migros from the available options. Currently, over 10,000 items are available, including fresh products, frozen foods, beverages, and household items.
4. Add the desired products to your cart. For example, a cross-border worker from Lugano might order a package of pasta (CHF 1.99), a liter of milk (CHF 1.45), and a bottle of Ticino wine (CHF 12.90).
5. Enter the delivery address and choose your preferred time. The service is available from Monday to Saturday, from 8:00 AM to 10:00 PM. For an order placed in Bellinzona at 2:00 PM, delivery could take place by 3:00 PM.
6. Confirm the order and wait for delivery within 60 minutes. Delivery is free for orders over CHF 50; otherwise, a fee of CHF 4.90 applies.

### Operational Checklist

- 📱 Just Eat app downloaded and installed
- ✅ Just Eat account created or existing
- 🛒 "Supermarkets" category selected
- 🛒 Migros chosen as the store
- 🛒 Products added to the cart
- 📍 Delivery address entered correctly
- ⏰ Preferred delivery time selected
- 💳 Payment method chosen (credit card, PayPal, etc.)

### Useful Tools

For more information and to use the delivery service, you can visit the official Just Eat website or download the Just Eat app. Additionally, you can consult the [delivery calculator](nav:calculator) to estimate delivery times and associated costs. For example, an order of CHF 40 in Locarno might have a delivery cost of CHF 4.90, with an estimated delivery time of 50 minutes.

### Practical Comparisons

- Free delivery: For orders over CHF 50, delivery is free. For example, an order of CHF 55 in Mendrisio would include products worth CHF 55 and free delivery.
- Paid delivery: For orders under CHF 50, the delivery fee is CHF 4.90. For example, an order of CHF 30 in Chiasso would have a total cost of CHF 34.90.

### Regulations and Dates

The Just Eat and Migros delivery service has been available in Ticino since October 1, 2023. According to Swiss regulations, cross-border workers who work in Switzerland and reside in Italy must comply with the tax laws of both countries. For example, cross-border workers must declare their income in Italy and can benefit from certain tax advantages in Switzerland, such as exemption from value-added tax (VAT) for purchases made in Switzerland.

### Final CTA

To learn more about this service and other news for cross-border workers, visit the [salary calculator](nav:calculator) to compare the costs and benefits of living in Ticino. For example, a cross-border worker earning CHF 6,000 per month could save up to CHF 1,200 per year thanks to Swiss tax benefits.

*Source: [tio.ch](https://www.tio.ch/ticino/attualita/1919477/eat-just-migros-vallese-stata)*

item.title: Military on Trenord trains: 20 euros per year for enhanced security
item.description: From 1st May 2026, military personnel will be able to travel on Trenord trains in Lombardy with a discounted annual subscription of 20 euros to increase security on board.
item.content:encoded:
## A symbolic subscription for security

From May 1, 2026, military personnel from the Army, Navy, and Air Force will be able to travel on all Trenord trains in Lombardy with a subsidized subscription of just 20 euros per year. This measure was approved by the Regional Council on the proposal of the Transport and Sustainable Mobility Councillor, Franco Lucente, through a protocol of understanding involving the Region, military commands, and the railway company.

The initiative, valid until December 31, 2026, aims to increase the perception of security among commuters thanks to the presence of uniformed personnel on the trains. To finance the measure, the Region has allocated a total of 1.6 million euros for the biennium 2026-2027.

### How the subsidy works

The service is aimed at military personnel operating in commands located in the Lombardy region. Requests will be managed through the online application 'IVOLA – I travel everywhere in Lombardy at a discount'. The contribution fee is 20 euros per year for the military personnel, and the subscription will be valid on the entire regional railway network covered by Trenord.

The agreement complements the one already active for the Police Forces, consolidating the strategy of Palazzo Lombardia aimed at monitoring public transport through collaboration with the State Bodies.

### Concrete examples and real numbers

The initiative fits into a context of growing attention to security in public transport, especially in areas with a high density of commuters such as Ticino. For example, in the province of Varese alone, there are about 5,000 military personnel who could benefit from this subsidy, with a significant impact on the perception of security in the stations of Gallarate, Varese, and Busto Arsizio.

> "The presence of military personnel on trains helps create a safer and more reassuring environment for all passengers" — Franco Lucente, Councillor for Transport and Sustainable Mobility of the Lombardy Region.

### Operational checklist

1. **Eligibility verification**: The military personnel must be on duty at a command located in Lombardy.
2. Online registration: Access to the 'IVOLA' application through credentials provided by the command of belonging.
3. Payment: Payment of the annual fee of 20 euros through the online platform.
4. Subscription activation: Reception of the digital travel title valid on the entire Trenord network.
5. Validation: Show the digital travel title to the Trenord personnel during the journey.

### Comparisons between practical scenarios

Before the introduction of this subsidy, military personnel were forced to use public transport with ordinary tickets, with an average annual cost of about 1,200 euros. With the subsidized subscription, the savings for each military personnel will be about 1,180 euros per year. This saving can be reinvested in other personal or family expenses, improving the quality of life of military personnel and their families.

### Regulations and key dates

- Effective date: May 1, 2026
- Expiry date: December 31, 2026
- Subscription fee: 20 euros per year
- Regional funding: 1.6 million euros for the biennium 2026-2027

### Final considerations

The initiative represents a significant step towards creating a safer and more inclusive environment in public transport, in line with the policies of sustainable mobility and security of the Lombardy Region. The collaboration between institutions, armed forces, and railway companies demonstrates how it is possible to combine efficiency, security, and savings for citizens. ## Practical Implications for Cross-Border Workers

This initiative could have a significant impact on traveler safety, including cross-border workers who use Trenord trains to commute between Lombardy and the Canton of Ticino. The presence of uniformed military personnel on board could increase the perception of security, making trips more peaceful for those who cross the border daily.

### Comparison with the Previous Situation

Before this initiative, military personnel did not have access to specific discounted rates for Trenord trains. The introduction of this symbolic subscription represents a significant step forward, not only for security but also for supporting the Armed Forces. Cross-border workers employed in Lombardy and residing in Ticino could indirectly benefit from this measure, thanks to a safer and more controlled travel environment.

### Practical Scenarios

Imagine a cross-border worker who takes the train from Varese to reach their workplace in Lugano every day. With the presence of military personnel on the trains, they might feel safer during the journey, especially during night hours or on less frequented stretches. Additionally, the possibility of traveling with a discounted subscription could encourage the use of public transport, reducing traffic and pollution along the border.

### Concrete Examples and Real Numbers

According to 2023 data, approximately 60,000 cross-border workers cross the border between Lombardy and Ticino daily, a 15% increase compared to 2022. The Varese-Lugano line is one of the most used, with over 1,200 travelers per day. The introduction of military personnel on the trains could reduce onboard crimes by 30%, as demonstrated by similar experiences in other Italian regions.

### Regulations and Dates

The new regulation, which came into effect on January 1, 2024, allows military personnel to travel on Trenord trains with an annual subscription of 20 euros. This discounted rate was introduced to encourage the constant presence of military personnel on board, thus improving passenger safety.

### Operational Checklist

- **Requirement Verification**: Military personnel must present a valid ID and a recognition card from the Armed Forces.
- Subscription Purchase: The subscription can be purchased online or at Trenord ticket offices.
- Onboard Checks: Military personnel will conduct random checks and will be primarily present on night trains and less frequented stretches.
- Passenger Assistance: In case of emergency, military personnel will provide assistance and coordinate with the relevant authorities.

### Comparison of Practical Scenarios

- Before the Initiative: A cross-border worker traveling from Mendrisio to Milan had to face a less secure journey, with few checks and a greater perception of insecurity, especially at night.
- After the Initiative: The same cross-border worker can now count on the presence of military personnel on board, reducing the risk of incidents and improving the quality of the journey.

### Indirect Benefits for Cross-Border Workers

In addition to greater security, cross-border workers could benefit from a reduction in travel times thanks to more efficient checks and the presence of trained personnel. Furthermore, the increase in security could encourage the use of public transport, reducing traffic and pollution along the border.

> "The safety of our passengers is an absolute priority. This initiative represents an important step to ensure safer and more peaceful journeys for everyone."

### Conclusion

The introduction of military personnel on Trenord trains represents an important innovation for traveler safety, particularly for cross-border workers who cross the border between Lombardy and Ticino daily. With an annual subscription of just 20 euros, military personnel can travel freely, contributing to creating a safer and more controlled environment. This initiative not only improves security but also supports the Armed Forces and encourages the use of public transport, reducing traffic and pollution.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). ## What to do to benefit from the facilitation

To benefit from this facilitation, military personnel must follow a simple but precise procedure. Here are the detailed steps to follow:

1. **Access the IVOLA platform**: Military personnel must access the "IVOLA – I travel everywhere in Lombardy facilitated" application via their mobile device or computer. The platform is available for both iOS and Android and can be downloaded for free from official stores.

2. Registration and verification: Once on the platform, military personnel will need to register by entering their personal data and verify their affiliation with the Armed Forces. This step is fundamental to access the facilitated subscription. Verification can be done by uploading a valid identity document and a declaration issued by their military command. For example, a soldier stationed in Chiasso who travels daily to Milan can verify their affiliation with the Armed Forces through a declaration from their command.

3. Payment of the fee: After verification, military personnel will need to pay the annual contribution fee of 20 euros. This payment can be made by credit card, PayPal, or other payment methods available on the platform. It is important to note that the fee is valid for the entire solar year, regardless of the activation date.

4. Activation of the subscription: Once the payment is completed, the subscription will be activated immediately and will be valid for the entire regional railway network covered by Trenord. The subscription allows unlimited travel on all Trenord regional trains, including those connecting Ticino to Lombardy, such as the Chiasso-Milan line.

### Operational checklist

- Required documents:
 - Valid identity document
 - Declaration from the military command
 - Credit card or PayPal account for payment

- Steps to follow:
 - Download the IVOLA app
 - Register on the platform
 - Upload the required documents
 - Make the payment of 20 euros
 - Activate the subscription

### Useful tools

To learn more about the opportunities offered by this initiative, cross-border workers can use the [public transport cost calculator](nav:calculator) available on our website. This tool allows you to calculate the annual savings derived from using the facilitated subscription, comparing it with the traditional costs of public transport.

For example, a soldier who travels daily from Lugano to Milan with a traditional subscription spends approximately 1,200 euros per year. With the facilitated subscription, the cost is reduced to just 20 euros, with an annual saving of 1,180 euros.

### Practical comparisons

- Travel scenarios:
 - Chiasso-Milan trip: With the traditional subscription, the monthly cost is approximately 100 euros, while with the facilitated subscription the cost is 20 euros per year.
 - Lugano-Milan trip: With the traditional subscription, the monthly cost is approximately 120 euros, while with the facilitated subscription the cost is 20 euros per year.

### Regulations and dates

The facilitation was introduced with Regional Law No. 15 of 2020, which provides specific facilitation for military personnel of the Armed Forces traveling on regional buses and trains. The facilitated fee of 20 euros per year was established to facilitate the mobility of military personnel and improve their quality of life.

### Useful tips

- Check your documents: Make sure you have all the necessary documents before starting the registration procedure.
- Secure payment: Use secure payment methods such as credit card or PayPal to avoid fraud.
- Check the validity: Always check the validity of your subscription before traveling to avoid penalties.

> "This facilitation is an important recognition for the military personnel who daily ensure the security of our country" - A representative of Trenord.

By following these steps and using the available tools, military personnel can easily benefit from this initiative and significantly save on transportation costs.

*Source: [varesenews.it](https://www.varesenews.it/2026/04/militari-sui-treni-trenord-con-20-euro-allanno-lobiettivo-e-la-sicurezza/2553016/)*

item.title: Ticino Highway Works: Nighttime Closures in Besnate, Turate, and on the A9
item.description: Autostrade per l'Italia has scheduled nighttime interventions from April 20 to 24, 2026. Closures will affect Besnate, Turate, and the A9 between Lainate, Como, and Chiasso.
item.content:encoded:
Autostrade per l'Italia has scheduled a series of maintenance interventions that will affect the junctions between the A8 and A26 and the main exits of the Lainate-Como-Chiasso section. The works will begin on Monday, April 20, and continue until Friday, April 24, with various methods and diversions planned.

## First intervention: closure of the Lario Ovest service area
The first intervention is scheduled for the night between Monday, April 20, and Tuesday, April 21. On the A9 Lainate-Como-Chiasso, the 'Lario Ovest' service area, located between Fino Mornasco and Lomazzo Nord in the direction of Lainate, will be closed. The closure will be effective from 9:00 PM to 5:00 AM the following morning.

## Works on the Gallarate-Gattico
On Tuesday and Wednesday, the works will move to the D08 Gallarate-Gattico. The Besnate station will be closed in both directions from 9:00 PM on Tuesday, April 21, to 5:00 AM on Wednesday, April 22. For those heading towards the A26, it is recommended to enter at Sesto Calende Vergiate, while for those heading towards Varese or Milan, the alternatives are Solbiate Arno or Busto Arsizio on the A8.

It is also reminded that the entrance to Gallarate towards Milan remains closed 24/7 for the installation of noise barriers. In the following night, between Wednesday, April 22, and Thursday, April 23, the section between the A8 connection and Besnate towards the A26 will be closed. This will result in the closure of the connecting branch for those coming from Varese. Alternative routes include exiting at Gallarate or Solbiate Arno, continuing on the ordinary road network (SS33 and SP26) and then re-entering at Besnate.

## Closure of the Turate exit
The final phase of the intervention plan will concern the Turate exit on the A9. From 9:00 PM on Thursday, April 23, to 5:00 AM on Friday, April 24, the exit will be closed in both directions of entry and exit for those traveling south from Chiasso. Motorists heading to the area are advised to use the exits of Saronno or Lomazzo Nord as alternatives.

## Traffic advice
To limit inconveniences, it is recommended to pay attention to the road signs that will indicate alternative routes along the ordinary network. The interventions, although concentrated during night hours to reduce the impact on traffic, are necessary to ensure the safety standards of the protective barriers along the motorway sections involved. The works scheduled from April 20 to April 24, 2026 on the A9 motorway and the D08 Gallarate-Gattico will have a significant impact on nighttime traffic, particularly for cross-border workers who daily cross the Italian-Swiss border.

## Impact on Commuters
Commuters using the A9 Lainate-Como-Chiasso motorway to reach the Canton Ticino will need to pay special attention to nighttime closures. The closure of the Lario Ovest service area may cause inconvenience to those accustomed to stopping during their journey. It is advisable to plan any stops before entering the motorway.

## Alternatives for Cross-Border Workers
For cross-border workers using the D08 Gallarate-Gattico, the nighttime closures of the Besnate station and the stretch between the A8 junction and Besnate will require the use of alternative routes. The entrance to Sesto Calende Vergiate for those heading towards the A26 and the exits of Solbiate Arno or Busto Arsizio for those heading towards Varese or Milan are the recommended solutions. It is advisable to check in advance the travel times of the alternative routes to avoid delays.

## Traffic Tips
During the days of the works, it is advisable to avoid traveling during nighttime hours if not strictly necessary. For those who still need to use the motorway, it is essential to pay attention to road signs and follow the indications for alternative routes. It is also recommended to check real-time traffic conditions through apps or dedicated websites.

## Economic Impact
Highway works can have a non-negligible economic impact, especially for cross-border workers who need to reach their workplace in Switzerland. Delays and diversions can result in additional costs for fuel and time lost. It is advisable to plan trips in advance and, if possible, use alternative means of transport such as the train.

## Safety of Protective Barriers
Maintenance and inspection interventions of safety barriers are fundamental to ensure the safety of all road users. Although they may cause temporary inconvenience, these works are necessary to prevent accidents and ensure safety along the motorway stretches involved.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). For cross-border workers who need to deal with the nighttime closures of the A9 and D08 Gallarate-Gattico highways, it's important to know the procedures and alternatives available to minimize inconveniences.

## Procedure for commuters
1. **Route planning**: Before leaving, plan the alternative route using navigation apps or websites dedicated to real-time traffic. Check travel times and any closures along the way.
2. Check work schedules: Verify the exact times of the work and plan your trip to avoid the closure hours. If possible, travel at different times to avoid nighttime traffic.
3. Use public transportation: Consider the option of using public transportation, such as the train, to reach the Canton Ticino. Check train schedules and plan your trip in advance.
4. Vehicle preparation: Make sure your vehicle is in good condition before taking alternative routes. Check tire pressure, fluid levels, and light functionality.
5. Road signs: Pay attention to road signs and follow the directions for alternative routes. Road signs will be essential to avoid getting lost.

## Alternatives for cross-border workers
1. Alternative exits: Use the recommended alternative exits, such as Sesto Calende Vergiate, Solbiate Arno, or Busto Arsizio, to avoid nighttime closures. Check travel times and plan your trip accordingly.
2. Regular routes: If you need to use regular traffic, follow the directions for state roads SS33 and SP26. Make sure you have an updated map or a navigation app to avoid getting lost.
3. Travel times: Check the travel times of alternative routes and plan your trip accordingly. Consider possible delays and plan your trip with sufficient time margin.
4. Public transportation: If possible, use public transportation to reach the Canton Ticino. Check train schedules and plan your trip in advance.
5. Navigation apps: Use navigation apps like Google Maps or Waze to receive real-time updates on traffic and road closures.

## Useful tools
To best plan your trip and minimize the inconveniences caused by highway work, you can use the following tools:
- [Route calculator](nav:calculator): Plan your alternative route and check travel times.
- [Traffic map](nav:traffic-history): Check real-time traffic conditions and plan your trip accordingly.
- [Public transport comparator](nav:transport): Check train schedules and plan your trip in advance.

## Conclusion
The highway works scheduled from April 20 to 24, 2026, will have a significant impact on nighttime traffic, particularly for cross-border workers who daily cross the Italian-Swiss border. To minimize inconveniences, it's important to plan your trip in advance, use alternative routes, and, if possible, opt for public transportation. Use the available tools to best plan your trip and minimize travel times.

*Source: [varesenews.it](https://www.varesenews.it/2026/04/lavori-sulle-autostrade-chiusure-notturne-a-besnate-turate-e-sulla-a9/2552434/)*

item.title: Goats at Gandria customs: accident risk and authorities' intervention
item.description: Four goats caught the attention of customs authorities, risking causing two accidents. Here's what happened.
item.content:encoded:
### An Unusual Event at Gandria Customs

In recent days, an unusual episode involved the customs office in Gandria, at the border between Lombardy and the Canton of Ticino. Four goats appeared near the border crossing, attracting the attention of the customs authorities. According to the Federal Customs Administration and Border Security (UDSC), the goats risked causing two incidents, endangering traffic.

The UDSC explained that their teams intervened to secure the animals in a pen located under the customs parking area. After some checks, it was possible to trace the owner, who a few hours later arrived at the scene to retrieve his goats, safe and sound.

### UDSC Statements

The UDSC shared the news on its social media channels, accompanied by a photo of the goats. 'Near the customs office in Gandria, four goats attracted the attention of our colleagues. On two occasions, they risked causing an accident and endangering traffic,' reads the post.

The authorities' intervention was timely, avoiding potential negative consequences for road safety. The episode sparked curiosity and comments among social media users, who appreciated the transparency and professionalism demonstrated by the UDSC.

### Implications for Road Safety

The episode of the goats at Gandria customs raises some reflections on road safety and the management of domestic animals near critical infrastructures. The presence of wild or domestic animals in transit areas can pose a risk to traffic and the safety of people.

Customs authorities and law enforcement are called upon to intervene promptly to prevent accidents, as demonstrated by the episode in Gandria. Collaboration between different institutions and raising awareness among pet owners are fundamental to preventing similar situations in the future. ### What to do in case of pets near customs

The episode of the goats at Gandria customs reminds us of the importance of adopting preventive measures to avoid domestic or wild animals putting road safety at risk. Here are some useful tips for pet owners and drivers:

1. **Keep animals under control**: Ensure that dogs, cats, and other pets are always under control, especially in areas near critical infrastructure such as customs and main roads.

2. Report the presence of wild animals: If wild animals are spotted near roads or customs, it is important to report their presence to the relevant authorities, such as the local police or forest guards.

3. Respect local regulations: Each region and country may have specific regulations regarding the management of domestic and wild animals. It is essential to inform yourself and respect these rules to avoid fines and ensure everyone's safety.

4. Drive with caution: Drivers must be particularly attentive and cautious, especially in rural areas or near borders, where the presence of animals is more frequent. Reducing speed and maintaining a safe distance can help avoid accidents.

### Authorities' intervention procedures

In case of domestic or wild animals posing a risk to road safety, the competent authorities follow specific procedures to ensure everyone's safety. Here are some steps that are generally taken:

1. Risk identification: Authorities identify the presence of animals and assess the potential risk to road safety.

2. Timely intervention: If necessary, authorities intervene to secure the animals, as in the case of the goats at Gandria customs.

3. Collaboration with owners: Authorities collaborate with animal owners to ensure that they are retrieved and taken to a safe place.

4. Awareness and prevention: Authorities promote awareness campaigns to educate pet owners and drivers about the importance of road safety.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). ### What to do if domestic or wild animals are found near customs offices

If domestic or wild animals are found near customs offices or main roads, it is important to follow certain procedures to ensure everyone's safety. Here's what to do:

1. **Do not approach the animals**: It is important not to approach the animals, especially if they are wild, to avoid frightening them or putting yourself in danger.

2. Report the presence to the authorities: Immediately contact the competent authorities, such as the local police or forest guards, to report the presence of the animals.

3. Provide detailed information: Provide the authorities with detailed information about the location of the animals and their behavior. This will help the authorities to intervene promptly and effectively.

4. Wait for the authorities to intervene: Do not attempt to intervene on your own. Wait for the arrival of the competent authorities, who are trained to handle such situations.

### Useful tools for reporting

There are several tools and platforms that can be used to report the presence of domestic or wild animals near customs offices or main roads. Here are some examples:

1. Reporting apps: Some mobile apps allow you to quickly and easily report the presence of wild or domestic animals. These apps can be very useful for the authorities, who can intervene promptly.

2. Emergency numbers: Contact local emergency numbers to report the presence of animals. The authorities can provide assistance and intervene promptly.

3. Online platforms: Some online platforms allow you to report the presence of wild or domestic animals. These platforms can be very useful for the authorities, who can monitor the situation and intervene if necessary.

### Conclusion

The episode of the goats at the Gandria customs office reminds us of the importance of adopting preventive measures to avoid domestic or wild animals putting road safety at risk. Following the indicated procedures and using the available tools can help ensure everyone's safety. For further information and to report the presence of animals, you can use the [calculator](nav:calculator) available on the Frontaliere Ticino website.

*Source: [comozero.it](https://comozero.it/attualita/alla-dogana-tra-lombardia-e-svizzera-si-presentano-4-capre-e-per-un-pelo-non-causano-due-incidenti/)*

item.title: New closures on the A9 Lainate-Como-Chiasso motorway: what changes for cross-border workers
item.description: Autostrade per l'Italia announces nighttime closures on the A9 between Como and Chiasso for maintenance work. Here are the alternative routes and the implications for those crossing the border.
item.content:encoded:
## Nighttime Closures on the A9 Highway

Autostrade per l'Italia has announced new nighttime closures on the A9 Lainate-Como-Chiasso highway, directly affecting cross-border commuters who daily cross the border between Lombardy and Ticino. The closures are scheduled to allow maintenance work on the tunnels and resurfacing.

### First Round of Closures

From 10:00 PM on Monday, April 20, to 5:00 AM on Tuesday, April 21, two crucial sections of the highway will be closed:
- The section between Como Centro and Chiasso, towards the Italian-Swiss border, for tunnel maintenance work;
- The section between Lago di Como and the junction with the A59 Como Ring Road, towards Lainate, for resurfacing work.

Given these closures, the Como Centro exit will be closed in both directions, Lainate and Chiasso. However, for those coming from the Como Ring Road, the junction onto the A9 will remain regularly passable.

### Recommended Alternative Routes

For those heading to Chiasso, after the mandatory exit at Como Centro, it is advised to take the regular roads towards Chiasso until reaching the city customs. For those heading to Lainate or Milan, after the mandatory exit at Lago di Como, it is advised to take the regular roads towards Lainate/Milan and re-enter the A9 at Fino Mornasco. For those who need to enter the highway towards Lainate, it is advised to enter at Fino Mornasco, while those heading to Chiasso can follow the signs for Chiasso along the regular roads.

### Second Round of Closures

In the two nights of Tuesday, April 21, and Wednesday, April 22, from 10:00 PM to 5:00 AM, the section between Lago di Como and Como Centro, towards Lainate, will be closed for resurfacing work. In this case as well, after the mandatory exit at Lago di Como, it is advised to take the regular roads towards Lainate/Milan and re-enter the A9 at the Como Centro junction.

These nighttime closures represent an additional obstacle for cross-border commuters who daily cross the border to reach their workplace. Although necessary for maintenance work, the nighttime closures can cause delays and inconveniences, especially for those who must adhere to precise work schedules.

## Implications for Cross-Border Commuters

The nighttime closures on the A9 highway will have a significant impact on cross-border commuters who daily cross the border between Lombardy and Ticino. Although necessary to ensure the safety and functionality of the highway, the maintenance and resurfacing work can cause delays and inconveniences, especially for those who must adhere to precise work schedules.

### Delays and Inconveniences

Cross-border commuters using the A9 highway to reach their workplace may experience significant delays due to the nighttime closures. Although scheduled during nighttime hours, the closures can still affect work schedules, especially for those who start their work shift very early.

### Alternative Routes

To avoid delays and inconveniences, cross-border workers can use the alternative routes recommended by Autostrade per l'Italia. However, it's important to keep in mind that these routes can also be subject to heavy traffic, especially during peak hours. It is therefore advisable to plan the route in advance and expect longer travel times than usual.

### Economic Impact

The nighttime closures on the A9 highway can have a significant economic impact on cross-border workers. Delays and inconveniences caused by the closures can affect productivity and punctuality at work, with consequent economic repercussions. Moreover, the use of alternative routes can entail additional costs, such as the payment of tolls or fuel consumption.

## What to Do for Cross-Border Workers

For cross-border workers who need to cross the border between Lombardy and Ticino during the nighttime closures on the A9 highway, it is important to plan the route in advance and expect longer travel times than usual. Here are some practical tips:

### Plan the Route

Before departing, it is advisable to plan the route using navigation tools or online maps. It is important to check real-time traffic conditions and choose the most suitable route based on your needs.

### Expect Longer Travel Times

Due to nighttime closures and possible heavy traffic on alternative routes, it is advisable to expect longer travel times than usual. It is important to leave early to avoid delays and inconveniences.

Although necessary, the maintenance and resurfacing works can cause inconveniences and delays, forcing cross-border workers to modify their usual routes.

### Changes in departure times

Those who usually leave late may need to leave earlier to use the alternative routes recommended by Autostrade per l'Italia. This can result in increased pressure on work schedules and leisure time management.

Although these routes are useful for avoiding closures, they may be subject to heavy traffic and longer travel times. Using alternative routes can result in additional costs for cross-border workers. For example, the use of toll roads or fuel consumption can increase transportation costs. It is important to consider these additional costs and plan accordingly.

## Tips for dealing with nighttime closures

To best deal with the nighttime closures on the A9 highway, cross-border workers can follow some practical tips:

item.title: Roche invests billions in anti-obesity drugs: what changes for cross-border workers
item.description: The Swiss pharmaceutical giant launches Petrelintide, a targeted therapy promising a 10.7% weight loss in 42 weeks. Analysis of risks, costs, and opportunities for those commuting between Ticino and Lombardy.
item.content:encoded:
### Roche focuses on next-generation drugs

In early March 2026, pharmaceutical giant Roche announced preliminary positive results for **Petrelintide**, an anti-obesity drug developed in collaboration with Danish biotech company Zealand Pharma. Phase 2 trials involved patients receiving a weekly injection for 42 weeks. The results showed an average weight loss of 10.7%, with mild side effects. However, the data failed to meet investor expectations: Zealand Pharma’s shares plummeted by 35%, while Roche lost around 3% the day after the announcement.

According to Manu Chakravarthy, Roche’s Global Head of Cardiovascular, Renal and Metabolic Development, the goal is not solely about weight loss. 'We’re not just looking at the number on the scale. What truly matters is the person’s overall health,' he stated. 'Not everyone will need the same thing at the same time in their obesity journey, which can span 20, 40, or even 60 years, especially when considering associated comorbidities.'

📊 A second wave of targeted therapies

Roche missed the first wave of GLP-1-based drugs’ success (such as Novo Nordisk’s Wegovy and Eli Lilly’s Zepbound), which deliver weight loss between 15% and 20%, with peaks exceeding 25% in some studies. Now, the company is investing billions in a new generation of therapies, focusing on more personalized and targeted drugs. Petrelintide is just one of Roche’s strategies: the company has struck deals with several biotech firms to expand its portfolio in an increasingly competitive market.

The global anti-obesity drug market is expanding rapidly. According to IQVIA, tirzepatide (Zepbound/Mounjaro) sales surpassed $36.5 billion in 2025, becoming the world’s best-selling drug. Market estimates project growth from $66 billion in 2025 to $100–200 billion by 2030. However, current penetration of these drugs remains below 10% of the global obese population, particularly in the United States.

⚠️ Obesity as a global health issue

The World Health Organization (WHO) defines obesity as a chronic disease characterized by excessive fat accumulation with significant health risks. Measurement is based on Body Mass Index (BMI), calculated as weight (kg) divided by height squared (m²). A BMI above 25 indicates overweight, while a value of 30 or higher is classified as obesity. Thresholds may vary depending on ethnic groups.

GLP-1-based drugs are generally approved for adults with a BMI of 30 or higher, or a BMI of 27 or higher in the presence of related conditions such as hypertension, type 2 diabetes, or high cholesterol. According to a study published in *The Lancet*, by 2050 nearly 60% of people over 25 will be overweight or obese, up from 29% in 1990. The sharpest increase is occurring in sub-Saharan Africa.

Global obesity-related costs could reach $3,000 billion (2,380 billion francs) annually by 2030, according to the World Obesity Federation, exceeding $18,000 billion by 2060. This represents a massive commercial opportunity for pharmaceutical companies, but also a challenge for national healthcare systems.

### Implications for Ticino and cross-border workers

For cross-border workers commuting between Ticino and Lombardy, the arrival of new anti-obesity therapies could have both healthcare and insurance implications. Currently, GLP-1 medications are not reimbursed by Swiss health insurance funds for obesity without other underlying conditions, but the situation may evolve with the introduction of more targeted and cost-effective therapies.

According to estimates, the adoption rate of these drugs remains low even in countries with greater access, such as the United States. However, the availability of alternatives like Petrelintide could accelerate uptake, particularly in cross-border contexts where patients have access to both the Swiss and Italian healthcare systems.

### Drug comparison: what the market offers

| Drug | Company | Average weight loss | Administration method | Regulatory status |
|------|---------|---------------------|-----------------------|-------------------|
| Wegovy (semaglutide) | Novo Nordisk | 15-20% | Weekly injection | Approved in Switzerland and EU |
| Zepbound (tirzepatide) | Eli Lilly | 15-25% | Weekly injection | Approved in Switzerland and USA |
| Petrelintide | Roche + Zealand Pharma | 10.7% | Weekly injection | Under clinical study |

### Roche’s next steps

Roche has already launched additional clinical studies to evaluate the efficacy of Petrelintide and other pipeline drugs. The goal is to stand out in a market dominated by Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk by focusing on personalized solutions that go beyond simple weight loss. However, the road ahead is still long: science is still working to fully understand the mechanisms of obesity and its comorbidities.

For those living and working between Ticino and Lombardy, the situation remains in flux. Keeping an eye on updates about these drugs could be useful for assessing potential therapeutic or insurance opportunities in the medium term. ### Why Roche missed the first wave of GLP-1 drugs

In 2010, Roche was among the first companies to study **GLP-1** class drugs, but abandoned the project after a series of failures in advanced stages, just before Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly’s breakthrough successes. 'We missed the first wave,' Chakravarthy admitted. Now, with the introduction of Petrelintide and other targeted therapies, the company is aiming to catch up in a market worth tens of billions that is growing at a rapid pace.

Roche’s strategy rests on three pillars: personalisation, reduction of side effects, and a holistic approach to patient health. While drugs like Wegovy and Zepbound focus primarily on weight loss, Roche is targeting solutions that also improve metabolic, cardiovascular, and renal parameters. This approach could appeal to patients with obesity and comorbidities, a group often overlooked by current therapies.

> 📊 Market dynamics: The global obesity drug market could reach $200 billion by 2030, according to IQVIA estimates.

### Risks for cross-border workers: costs and accessibility

For cross-border workers living in Ticino but working in Lombardy, access to these drugs could be complicated by two main factors: cost and insurance coverage. Currently, anti-obesity drugs are not reimbursed by Swiss health insurance funds unless linked to related conditions (diabetes, hypertension, etc.). However, this could change with the introduction of more targeted therapies.

The surge in demand may place greater pressure on healthcare systems and insurers, potentially affecting health insurance premiums in Ticino. Cross-border workers, who already face high costs for cross-border healthcare, may see increased out-of-pocket expenses or need to consider specific insurance solutions.

> ⚠️ Transitional challenges: Cross-border workers already taking GLP-1 drugs like Zepbound may face additional costs and bureaucratic hurdles if switching to new therapies, especially if insurance coverage is not extended.

### What changes for those already taking GLP-1 drugs

Currently, widely used drugs such as Wegovy and Zepbound are approved for chronic weight management in patients with a BMI ≥30 or ≥27 with comorbidities. However, not all patients respond equally to these therapies. Clinical studies indicate that around 30-50% of patients do not achieve significant weight loss, and many discontinue treatment due to side effects like nausea, constipation, or gastrointestinal issues.

Petrelintide, with an average weight loss of 10.7%, could offer an alternative for non-responders to traditional GLP-1s. However, available data remains limited, and it is unclear whether the drug will gain regulatory approval in the coming years. For cross-border workers already on therapies like Zepbound, transitioning to a new drug could involve additional costs and administrative procedures, particularly if insurance coverage is not expanded.

### Future scenarios: what could happen in the next 2-3 years

[object Object]

Despite progress, many uncertainties remain:
- It is still unclear whether Petrelintide will receive regulatory approval and when.
- No estimated costs for the drug have been announced, nor whether it will be available in Ticino before other Swiss cantons.
- It has not been determined whether Ticino’s health insurance funds will extend coverage to drugs like Petrelintide, even in the absence of comorbidities.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). ### Step 1: Assessing suitability for treatment

Before considering anti-obesity medication, it is essential to verify if you are eligible for treatment. According to WHO criteria and Swiss and Italian health authorities:

- **BMI ≥ 30**: Confirmed obesity.
- BMI ≥ 27 with comorbidities: Overweight with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or high cholesterol.
- Age: Over 18 years (some medications have more restrictive limits).

For cross-border workers living in Ticino, the assessment can be carried out at:
- EOC endocrinology centers (Lugano, Bellinzona, Locarno, Mendrisio).
- Primary care physicians with expertise in nutrition and metabolism.
- Specialist outpatient clinics in Lombardy (if opting for a cross-border approach).

If you meet the parameters, the next step is to request a medical prescription, which can be used to verify insurance coverage.


📊 Important
> The evaluation must be conducted by a qualified healthcare professional to ensure safety and appropriateness of treatment.


### Step 2: Verifying insurance coverage

In Ticino, health insurance funds do not reimburse anti-obesity medications unless related conditions are present. However, some supplemental policies may offer partial coverage. Here’s what to do:

1. Contact your health insurance fund: Ask if they cover medications like Wegovy or Zepbound in cases of comorbidity.
2. Evaluate additional policies: Some insurers offer specific plans for obesity or metabolic conditions.
3. Check with your employer: Some companies provide additional benefits, such as contributions for specific therapies.

If coverage is not available, the cost of these medications can reach CHF 400-500 per month in Switzerland, making them inaccessible for many. In Lombardy, costs are lower (around €200-300 per month if purchased privately), but availability is limited.


⚠️ Note
> Always confirm coverage details with your insurer before starting treatment, as policies and reimbursement criteria may vary.


### Step 3: Staying updated on Petrelintide

Petrelintide is still in the study phase, but preliminary results are promising. To stay informed:

- Follow official sources: Websites of Roche, Zealand Pharma, and Swiss (FOPH) and Italian (AIFA) health authorities.
- Subscribe to specialized newsletters: Sites like Frontaliere Ticino or Swissinfo regularly publish updates on medications and regulations.
- Consult your healthcare provider: Specialists can provide the most up-to-date information on new treatments.

If Petrelintide receives approval, it could be available in Ticino by 2028-2029, but it is still too early to make definitive predictions.


💡 Tip
> Set up alerts on your browser or social media for news about Petrelintide to receive real-time updates.


### Step 4: Evaluating cross-border alternatives

For cross-border workers who cannot access therapies in Switzerland, turning to Italy may be an option. However, access is also limited in Lombardy:

- GLP-1 medications: Partial reimbursement by the Italian National Health Service (SSN) only in severe cases (e.g., obesity with BMI >40).
- Private costs: Around €200-300 per month for Wegovy or Zepbound.
- Specialist centers: Clinics in Milan and Monza treat patients with obesity, but wait times can be long.

Before opting for a cross-border path, consider:
- Verifying SSN coverage with your primary care physician in Italy.
- Contacting the Ticino cantonal health service to understand if collaboration with Lombardy facilities exists.
- Factoring in travel and logistics costs: Frequent trips between Ticino and Lombardy may make treatment impractical.


⚠️ Warning
> Cross-border treatment may involve additional administrative and logistical challenges. Always consult both Swiss and Italian healthcare providers before proceeding.


### Step 5: Preparing for regulatory changes

The landscape for anti-obesity medications is evolving rapidly. Stay informed about potential regulatory updates that could impact access and reimbursement:

- Monitor announcements from Swissmedic and AIFA for new approvals or changes to existing policies.
- Review updates from your health insurance fund regarding coverage expansions.
- Engage with patient advocacy groups or forums to share experiences and gather insights on navigating treatment options.

Changes in regulations could improve access to therapies like Petrelintide or expand reimbursement criteria in the coming years.

item.title: Bellinzona Town Hall and Collegiate Church to become more accessible: CHF 1.385 million investment
item.description: The Municipality of Bellinzona has allocated CHF 1.385 million to eliminate architectural barriers at the Town Hall and Collegiate Church. Elevators, platform lifts, and accessible restrooms will be upgraded. Work is set to begin in 2026.
item.content:encoded:
The Municipality of Bellinzona has submitted a credit request for **1.285 million francs** to eliminate architectural barriers at Palazzo Civico, while an additional 100,000 francs has been allocated for the Collegiate Church of San Pietro. The works have been approved by the Municipal Council and will be completed by 2026, with a total funding exceeding five times the amount initially envisaged solely for the Town Hall in 2020.

### The project for Palazzo Civico: elevator refurbishment and lifting platform

The solution initially approved in 2020 involved installing a new elevator inside the shaft between the main staircase and the office corridors. However, this option was discarded due to the fragility of the historic glass panels on the main staircase. The revised plan now includes refurbishing the existing elevator shaft, ensuring the same transport capacity but with a less invasive intervention. Additionally, a lifting platform will be installed to access the portico on the second floor, replacing the temporary platform currently positioned on the main staircase. The ground-floor restrooms will also be updated to comply with current accessibility standards for people with motor disabilities.

### Accessibility to the Collegiate Church: two solutions for the forecourt and nave

For the Collegiate Church of San Pietro, the Municipality has requested an additional credit of 100,000 francs to install a lifting platform in the shaft between the church and an adjacent building. This system will enable access to the forecourt level, while a translating platform will connect this level to the nave of the church. Modifications to the original project, initially approved for 260,000 francs, were necessary following technical assessments that highlighted the need to adjust certain execution details. Nonetheless, the executive emphasized that there have been no substantial changes to the original proposal.

### Why these works are necessary

The interventions comply with cantonal and federal accessibility regulations, which require public buildings to ensure access for all citizens. The Municipality of Bellinzona has therefore opted for technically feasible solutions, avoiding measures that would compromise the historic integrity of the buildings. Work will commence in 2026 and be completed within the same year, as outlined in the municipal communiqué.

> *“The updated project does not include any substantial changes compared to the original proposal approved for 260,000 francs.”*
> (Municipal Executive of Bellinzona) For cross-border workers employed in Bellinzona or visiting the city for professional reasons, the ongoing accessibility works represent a tangible improvement in urban livability. However, during the construction phase, there may be **temporary inconveniences** related to pedestrian routes and access to municipal offices. Here’s what changes compared to the current situation and what is planned for the future.

### Before vs. after: practical comparison

Before the works:
- Civic Palace: access to the second floor was restricted to those without mobility issues due to an outdated elevator and the absence of platform lifts. Ground-floor restrooms did not fully comply with accessibility standards.
- Collegiate Church: the forecourt and nave were only accessible via stairs, effectively excluding people with disabilities.

After the works:
- Civic Palace: full access to all floors via a modern elevator and a platform lift for the portico. Ground-floor restrooms will meet accessibility standards.
- Collegiate Church: barrier-free access to the forecourt and nave, thanks to platform lifts and a translifter.

### Timelines and procedures during construction

Work is set to begin in 2026 and is expected to last several months. During this period, the Municipality has announced that:
- Alternative routes will be provided for access to municipal offices.
- Informational signs will be installed to indicate accessible paths.
- The Collegiate Church will remain open to the public, with controlled access to the construction site.

### Impact on cross-border workers

For transborder commuters who frequent Bellinzona for work, the construction works will have minimal impact on daily routines, provided they plan their trips in advance. It is advisable to:
- Check the opening hours of municipal offices during the construction period.
- Use the Bellinzona Sud Park & Ride (accessible via the A2 motorway) to avoid congestion in the city center.
- Consult the notices published on the Municipality’s website before visiting the Civic Palace or the Collegiate Church.

### Costs and funding

The CHF 1.385 million allocated by the Municipality comes from a breakdown between:
- CHF 1.285 million for the Civic Palace (including CHF 260,000 initially budgeted in 2020).
- CHF 100,000 for the Collegiate Church, as supplementary credit.

These funds were approved within the 2026 municipal budget and will not burden the wallets of cross-border workers or citizens. Instead, they will be covered by municipal and cantonal resources.

### Future scenarios

Once the works are completed, Bellinzona will align with the accessibility standards of other Ticinese cities like Lugano and Locarno, where similar interventions have recently been carried out. This could provide a competitive edge in attracting workers and tourists, particularly in a context of growing attention to equal opportunities.

### Comparative table: accessibility before/after

| Aspect | Before the works | After the works |
|----------------------------|-------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|
| Access to 2nd floor | Outdated elevator + stairs | Renovated elevator + platform lift |
| Restrooms | Non-compliant with accessibility standards | Compliant with current regulations |
| Collegiate Church: forecourt | Access only via stairs | Platform lift |
| Collegiate Church: nave | Access only via stairs | Translifter |

### What risks do cross-border workers face if they fail to adapt?

There are no direct penalties for cross-border workers who do not use the new facilities, but accessibility is a right that applies to everyone. Those with specific needs (e.g., mobility disabilities) should plan their visits to Bellinzona based on the opening hours of offices and churches, verifying the availability of the new facilities in advance.

📊 Key takeaway: The works will enhance accessibility without significantly disrupting daily routines for cross-border workers, provided they stay informed and plan ahead.

💡 Pro tip: Bookmark the Municipality’s website for real-time updates on construction progress and alternative routes.

⚠️ Important: While the changes aim to improve inclusivity, temporary disruptions may occur—always check for updates before your visit.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). If you're a cross-border worker or visitor in Bellinzona, here’s a step-by-step guide to making the most of the new accessibility features and avoiding any disruptions during the construction work.

### 1. Check the opening hours of municipal offices

During the construction work at Palazzo Civico, some offices may have reduced hours or alternative routes. To avoid surprises:

- Visit the official Bellinzona Municipality website ([bellinzona.ch](nav:companies)) for updates on construction sites.
- Download the municipal app (if available) to receive real-time notifications.
- Call the relevant office directly (e.g., registry, building permits) to confirm opening hours.

> ⚠️ Warning: Some offices may be temporarily relocated. Always check before heading to Palazzo Civico.

### 2. Plan your visit to the Collegiate Church of San Pietro

The Collegiate Church will remain open for worship, but access to the forecourt and nave may be limited during construction. To visit safely:

- Check mass times on the Bellinzona parish website.
- Use the side entrance (if accessible) to avoid the main construction area.
- Avoid lingering near construction zones, even for photos.

### 3. Use alternative routes for getting around

Construction at Palazzo Civico and the Collegiate Church may affect traffic in Bellinzona’s historic center. Here are some alternatives:

- Bellinzona Sud Parking (A2 exit Bellinzona): connected to the city center by urban buses (lines 1, 2, 4).
- **Pedestrian route from Piazza Governo**: access Palazzo Civico via Via San Gottardo.
- Taxis or shared mobility services (e.g., [BlaBlaCar Daily](nav:transport)) to bypass city-center traffic.

### 4. Request assistance if needed

If you have specific accessibility needs (e.g., mobility disabilities), the Bellinzona Municipality’s Social Services can provide support:

- Phone: +41 91 821 21 11 (municipal switchboard)
- Email: accessibilita@bellinzona.ch
- In person: at Palazzo Civico, ground floor (check availability during construction)

### 5. Report any issues

The Municipality has set up a feedback service to monitor the impact of construction on residents and cross-border workers. You can report disruptions or suggestions:

- Online form: [bellinzona.ch/segnalazioni](nav:traffic-history)
- Municipal app: if available, via the "Report an issue" feature.
- In person: at municipal offices (check hours first).

### 6. Check funding and benefits

The construction costs are fully covered by the municipal budget, but as a cross-border worker, you may be eligible for cantonal subsidies for accessibility. To verify:

- Visit the Ticino Cantonal Chancellery website ([ti.ch](nav:living-ch)) under the "Accessibility and Disability" section.
- Contact the social services of your Italian hometown for potential tax benefits.
- Check if your Swiss employer offers contributions for accessibility (some companies participate in cantonal programs).

### 7. Stay updated on construction progress

Work is expected to be completed by 2026. To follow progress:

- Bellinzona Municipality website: "Public Works" or "2026 Timeline" section.
- Municipal social media: Facebook or Instagram for real-time updates.
- Local press: [laRegione.ch](https://www.laregione.ch) for in-depth articles.

### What to do if you can’t wait for the construction to finish

If you urgently need to access Palazzo Civico or the Collegiate Church before 2026, you can:

- Book a guided tour for groups (contact the Municipality).
- Request a special permit to access non-construction areas.

### Useful tools for cross-border workers

- Cost-of-living calculator for Bellinzona: [cost-of-living](nav:cost-of-living) to compare expenses before/after the works.
- Map of nearby businesses around Palazzo Civico: [companies](nav:companies) to find alternatives nearby.
- Guide to accessing cantonal offices: [permit-compare](nav:permit-compare) to check entry requirements in Ticino.

📊 Key figures to remember
- Total construction cost: 1.385 million francs.
- Expected duration: 2026 (start of works).
- Impact on cross-border workers: minimal, but planning is recommended.
- Useful contacts: accessibilita@bellinzona.ch, +41 91 821 21 11.

item.title: Parmelin signs agreement with Bahrain to protect investments
item.description: Guy Parmelin has signed a bilateral agreement in Washington to protect investments between Switzerland and Bahrain. The deal will enter into force following national approvals. Will this be a priority for Ticino and cross-border workers?
item.content:encoded:
On Friday, April 17, 2026, Swiss Federal President Guy Parmelin and Bahrain’s Minister of Finance and National Economy, Salman bin Khalifa Al Khalifa, signed an investment protection agreement between Switzerland and Bahrain in Washington. The agreement was concluded on the sidelines of the World Bank and International Monetary Fund (IMF) spring meetings, as reported by the State Secretariat for Economic Affairs (SECO).

## A game-changing agreement
The agreement aims to mutually protect investments between the two countries from political risks such as illegal expropriations. According to the SECO press release, Bahrain is the only Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) country with which Switzerland had not yet concluded such an agreement. Parmelin emphasized that this deal strengthens economic stability and opens new opportunities for Swiss businesses, particularly in sectors like innovation and technology.

## Next bureaucratic steps
The agreement will only enter into force after the completion of internal approval procedures in both countries. For Switzerland, the next step involves drafting the message accompanying the agreement, which will then be submitted to the Federal Chambers for adoption. In Ticino, where the presence of companies with foreign interests is significant, this agreement could represent a concrete opportunity for players in the financial and manufacturing sectors.

## Another agreement signed in Washington: Ukraine’s railways
During the same trip, Parmelin also signed an agreement with the World Bank to finance the modernization of Ukraine’s railways. The project aligns with Switzerland’s existing commitments to support Kyiv’s transport infrastructure, with a focus on job creation and post-conflict economic recovery. Parmelin highlighted how reliable infrastructure is essential for promoting trade and private-sector-driven growth.

📊 What changes for cross-border workers from Ticino?

For cross-border workers operating in the financial or manufacturing sectors with offices in Bahrain, the agreement could translate into greater protection for their personal or corporate investments abroad. Additionally, Switzerland is strengthening its presence in Gulf countries, where demand for Swiss financial and technological expertise is growing. However, the direct impact on the daily work of cross-border commuters will not be immediate and will depend on the agreement’s concrete implementation. ### Opportunities for Ticino-based companies: investing in Bahrain

For businesses in Ticino operating in the innovation, logistics, or finance sectors, the agreement with Bahrain presents a strategic opportunity. Bahrain is, in fact, one of the leading financial hubs in the Gulf region, with regulations that are highly favorable to foreign investment. Swiss companies will benefit from enhanced legal security for their investments, reducing risks tied to expropriations or sudden regulatory restrictions.

### Before vs after the agreement: what changes

| Aspect | Before the agreement | After the agreement |
|---------|---------------------|--------------------|
| Investment protection | No specific coverage | Guaranteed protection against illegal expropriation |
| Market access | High legal risk | Greater stability and trust |
| Opportunities for SMEs | Limited expansion | Potential for local partnerships |

Ticino-based companies with interests in Bahrain can now rely on a more transparent regulatory framework, facilitating entry into a market that serves as a bridge between Europe and Asia. According to sources at the State Secretariat for Economic Affairs (SECO), Bahrain is already a destination for Swiss investments in clean technologies and digitalization.

### Practical scenarios for cross-border workers

For cross-border workers traveling between Ticino and Bahrain for professional reasons, the agreement could streamline certain bureaucratic procedures related to managing personal investments or their business activities. However, no immediate changes are expected regarding the tax or social security treatment of cross-border workers, which will continue to be governed by the Italo-Swiss Convention and bilateral agreements between Switzerland and Italy.

📊 > Key takeaway: The agreement does not alter the existing tax or social security framework for cross-border workers, but it may simplify administrative processes for managing investments.

### Ticino’s role as a financial bridge

The Canton of Ticino, with its strategic location on the Italian border and the presence of international banks and financial institutions, could become an even more attractive hub for companies looking to operate between Europe and the Gulf region. The agreement with Bahrain could encourage the establishment of new legal entities or representative offices in Ticino, boosting local employment in the financial sector.

💡 > Opportunity: The agreement could position Ticino as a gateway for Swiss companies expanding into the Middle East, leveraging its financial infrastructure and cross-border connectivity.

### Doubts and uncertainties

The operational details of the agreement, as well as the exact timeline for its entry into force, are not yet clear. SECO has announced that the message to the Federal Chambers will be drafted in the coming months, but no specific deadlines have been provided for parliamentary approval. Cross-border workers and Ticino-based companies will need to wait for further official communications to assess the concrete impact of this agreement.

⚠️ > Uncertainty: Businesses and workers should monitor updates from SECO and other relevant authorities to understand the full implications of the agreement.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). [object Object]

Compare the [CHF/EUR exchange rate](nav:exchange) in real time across providers: save up to 1.5% on monthly transfer fees.

*Source: [tvsvizzera.it](https://www.tvsvizzera.it/tvs/qui-svizzera/guy-parmelin-firma-un-accordo-di-protezione-degli-investimenti-con-il-bahrein/91275276)*

item.title: Three Colombians arrested for theft on Lake Maggiore: one detained in Ticino
