The presence of food & beverage areas and children’s play zones also makes the event family-friendly, a detail that could attract cross-border workers with children looking to spend a weekend differently.

### A prize draw to engage the community
The prize draw held over the two days, with a Renault Clio and two season tickets for the 2026/27 season up for grabs, provides an additional incentive to participate. For cross-border workers, winning a season ticket could mean saving hundreds of francs annually on matchday tickets, as well as benefiting from discounts on club services. The involvement of commercial partners like AUTORS SA, which is providing the car as a prize, underscores the interest of local businesses in actively engaging the community in such initiatives.

### Transport and logistics: what’s changed compared to the past
Compared to previous openings of sports venues, the AIL Arena stands out for its focus on mobility. Dedicated shuttle services and enhanced public transport options have been introduced to address the needs of a city like Lugano, where traffic can be a challenge, especially during major events. For cross-border workers accustomed to commuting between Italy and Switzerland, these solutions could make participation much easier, avoiding long waits or parking difficulties.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). Are you a fan, a local resident, or a cross-border worker keen to take part in the inauguration of the AIL Arena? Here’s how to make sure you don’t miss out on this one-of-a-kind event.

### Step 1: Register for the free tour
To join the AIL Arena tour, you’ll need to register in advance. Sign-ups will open in the weeks leading up to 30 May and will be available through the official FC Lugano channels, including the club’s website and social media. As soon as the dates are announced, we recommend booking your spot right away, as availability may be limited. Once registered, you’ll receive confirmation granting you free access to Arcobaleno community public transport for both days of the event.

### Step 2: Plan your journey to the AIL Arena
If you’re taking part in the tour, you can travel for free on public transport. The Arcobaleno network covers a wide area of southern Ticino, so you can easily reach the AIL Arena from Chiasso, Mendrisio, or other nearby towns. Check the SBB/CFF or ATG websites to confirm train and shuttle schedules. If you prefer to drive, keep in mind that parking restrictions may apply, so public transport is the best option—and it’s free for you on these days.

### Step 3: Visit the Fan Village without registering
If you’d rather skip the tour but still want to soak up the event atmosphere, you can head straight to the Fan Village along Via Stadio. This area will be open to all for the entire duration of the event, with no registration required. Here, you can purchase season tickets for 2026/27, enjoy food and drink offerings, take part in games and entertainment, and even try your luck in the prize draw.

### Step 4: Enter the prize draw
Over the two days, you can try your luck in the prize draw. To enter, simply visit the dedicated booth in the Fan Village and follow the organisers’ instructions. Prizes include a Renault Clio branded with the FC Lugano logo and two season tickets for 2026/27. If you’re a football fan, this could be your chance to win something truly special.

### Step 5: Keep up with official updates
To stay in the loop, follow the official FC Lugano and Corriere del Ticino channels. These will have the latest details on the two-day programme, tour registration, and mobility information. For real-time updates, check social media, where videos and interviews about the new AIL Arena will be shared ahead of the event.

### What if you can’t make it?
If you won’t be in Lugano on 30 and 31 May, don’t worry—the AIL Arena will soon become a hub for sports and cultural events. You can still stay updated through the official FC Lugano channels to catch the next openings or initiatives. If you’re interested in season tickets for future seasons, you can already reach out to the club for early information and to secure your spot.

Now that you know how to take part, all that’s left is to get ready for a weekend of sport, community, and entertainment at Lugano’s brand-new AIL Arena. Plan your arrival, book your tour spot if you’re interested, and get ready to discover the future of Ticino’s sports scene!

*Source: [ticinonews.ch](https://www.ticinonews.ch/sport/inaugurazione-ail-arena-porte-aperte-per-tutti-il-30-e-31-maggio-431815)*

item.title: Ammonia leak in Stabio: what you need to know and how to react
item.description: On Thursday, April 16, 2026, an ammonia leak occurred at the Rapelli food factory in Stabio. Authorities have urged residents to close doors and windows within a 1 km radius. No serious injuries were reported, and an immediate evacuation was carried out.
item.content:encoded:
On Thursday evening, April 16, 2026, shortly after 10:00 PM, an ammonia leak occurred at the **Rapelli SpA** food processing plant located on Via Laveggio in Stabio, Canton Ticino. The incident prompted local authorities to issue an alert for the population, urging residents within a one-kilometer radius of the facility to immediately close doors and windows and avoid the affected area.

### Timeline of the event
The emergency was detected around 10:15 PM, when the plant's security systems signaled a leak in the refrigeration unit. The facility, which uses ammonia as a coolant, was evacuated within minutes. Firefighters and civil protection teams from Ticino swiftly intervened, coordinating with local health services. According to Swiss Broadcasting Corporation (RSI), no serious consequences were reported for the population, although the gas dispersion triggered an immediate state of alert.

### Authorities' instructions
Ticino authorities issued three immediate recommendations for those near the plant:
- Close doors and windows within a one-kilometer radius of Via Laveggio
- Avoid traveling to or moving within the area to prevent accidental exposure
- Inform neighbors, particularly the elderly or people with respiratory issues, about the measures to take

> *«The plant was evacuated without any imminent danger to the population. However, given ammonia’s toxicity, it is essential to strictly follow the emergency services’ instructions»* stated a spokesperson for Ticino’s Civil Protection, without providing further details on the alert’s duration.

### The Rapelli plant
Rapelli SpA is a food industry company based in Stabio, known for producing refrigerated foods. Ammonia is used in its industrial refrigeration systems, a common practice in many food processing plants for low-temperature preservation. Following the incident, the plant remained closed for technical inspections by cantonal authorities, with no indication of a near-term reopening.

### Ticino’s regulatory context
Ticino, in line with Swiss regulations, governs the use of hazardous substances like ammonia through the Canton’s Air Protection Regulation (set to be finalized in 2026). Companies using such substances must implement detection and early warning systems, as well as emergency plans shared with local authorities. However, specific regulations on refrigeration systems were not further detailed by the source.

### Cross-border impact: effect on commuters
The municipality of Stabio lies just 4 kilometers from the Gaggiolo crossing, one of the main border points for cross-border workers traveling daily between Lombardy and Ticino. Although the incident was localized, it raised concerns among residents and commuters, many of whom pass through the area during their daily commutes. No delays or closures were reported at the border, but the proximity sparked discussions among commuters.

### Institutional reactions
The Mayor of Stabio, contacted by local media, confirmed the authorities’ intervention, emphasizing collaboration between Canton Ticino and neighboring municipalities in managing emergencies. However, no updates were provided on the alert’s resolution timeline or residual risk assessments.

### Ticino media’s take
RSI reported that the ammonia leak was contained by 11:30 PM, though no subsequent updates were given on the plant’s safety. Local press highlighted how such incidents, while rare, are possible in facilities using hazardous chemicals, calling for greater attention to prevention measures.

📊 Key figures:
- Time of incident: 10:15 PM, April 16, 2026
- Radius of alert: 1 km from Via Laveggio
- Containment time: 11:30 PM [object Object]

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). ### What to do if exposed to ammonia
If you suspect you have been exposed to ammonia during the incident in Stabio, it is crucial to act quickly to minimize health risks. Here are the immediate steps to take:

1. Leave the contaminated area: immediately exit Via Laveggio and move to a ventilated space, preferably outdoors
2. Remove contaminated clothing: take off any clothing exposed to the gas and wash it separately
3. Wash skin and eyes: rinse thoroughly with running water for at least 15 minutes, avoiding rubbing
4. Do not induce vomiting: if accidental ingestion occurs, drink small amounts of water and contact the health emergency number 144 immediately

> *«Ammonia is an alkaline substance and can cause chemical burns. Immediate decontamination is essential to prevent permanent damage»* warned an emergency room doctor in Lugano, without providing further clinical details.

### Procedures for cross-border workers passing through Stabio
For cross-border commuters who travel through the Stabio area daily, the incident represents a temporary change in travel habits. Here’s what to do:

- Check updates from Ticino authorities on the [Ticino Civil Protection website](nav:permits)
- Avoid unnecessary stops on Via Laveggio or adjacent streets
- Carry an emergency kit with an FFP2 mask, water, and basic medications

### Useful contacts in case of emergency
If you have doubts or need assistance, here are the emergency numbers to contact:

| Service | Number |
|---------|--------|
| Health emergency | 144 |
| Ticino Civil Protection | 118 |
| Canton police | 117 |
| Border crossings | 112 |

### What has been done so far
Following the incident on Thursday, April 16, 2026, Ticino authorities have launched technical inspections at the Rapelli plant to determine the causes of the ammonia leak and assess any corrective measures. The plant remains closed pending the outcome of the investigation, but no reopening date has been announced yet.

### Next steps for safety
To prevent future incidents, the Canton of Ticino has initiated a review of emergency plans for companies using hazardous substances. In particular, the following will be evaluated:
- Updating automatic detection systems
- Establishing safety zones in nearby residential areas
- Increasing inspections by the Environment Section

### How to stay informed
To receive real-time updates on the situation in Stabio and any safety measures, you can:
- Follow the Ticino Civil Protection website ([www.ti.ch/protezionecivile](nav:permits))
- Download the official Ticino app for alerts
- Monitor social media of local authorities

### What companies must do
Companies using hazardous substances like ammonia must ensure:
- Regular maintenance of refrigeration systems
- Staff training on emergency procedures
- Constant updating of safety plans

### What cross-border workers can do
Cross-border commuters can contribute to the safety of the area by adopting responsible behaviors:
- Report any anomalies in industrial plants
- Participate in emergency drills organized by local authorities
- Share information with colleagues and neighbors

### 📊💡⚠️ CTA: Practical tools for cross-border workers
To handle emergency situations like the one in Stabio, here are some practical tools available on Frontaliere Ticino:

- [Find out how to calculate the cost of living in Ticino](nav:cost-of-living) to assess the economic impact of potential evacuations
- [Consult the border crossing map](nav:border-map) to plan alternative routes in case of closures
- [Download the guide on how to act in case of a chemical emergency](nav:first-day) to be prepared for any eventuality

> «Safety is never a matter of chance. Being informed and prepared can make all the difference in emergency situations.»

*Source: [varesenews.it](https://www.varesenews.it/2026/04/fuga-di-ammoniaca-da-una-fabbrica-a-stabio-allerta-delle-autorita/2549740/)*

item.title: Echo Project: Submerged Meadows to Save Lake Lugano and Como
item.description: The Interreg Echo project (2025–2027) is testing the restoration of submerged meadows using biochar to combat climate change in Lake Lugano and Lake Como. Two experimental sites, each covering 500 square metres.
item.content:encoded:
In Minoprio, fifteen kilometres south of Como, a warehouse houses eighteen transparent freshwater tanks. Inside, thousands of aquatic plants are thriving: Vallisneria spiralis, Myriophyllum spicatum, Potamogeton perfoliatus and Potamogeton lucens. These species, which typically grow on lakebeds, will be transplanted to experimental sites in Lugano and Como starting in May 2026 as part of the Interreg Echo project.

## A cross-border initiative to combat lake degradation

Echo is a project funded by the Interreg Italy-Switzerland cooperation programme, with a total budget of €320,000 and CHF 60,000. Over its three-year duration (2025–2027), the project focuses on two key objectives: restoring submerged meadows and assessing the carbon storage potential of lake sediments. Nicola Castelnuovo, a naturalist and researcher with the Italian association Proteus — a project partner — explains that submerged meadows are critical habitats for biodiversity but are in sharp decline in the Lario and Ceresio basins.

### Two lakes, two different realities

The experimental sites, each covering 500 square metres and located between three and five metres deep near the shore, will be established in both Lake Como (Italy) and Lake Lugano (Switzerland). Castelnuovo highlights the differences between the two basins: 
> ‘In Lake Como, submerged meadows are steadily depleting and have disappeared entirely in some areas. In Lugano, the situation is more varied: there are zones with extremely limited vegetation, others colonised by invasive species, and others still with high-quality meadows.’

### Plants grow at a rate of one centimetre per day

The tanks in Minoprio were set up between October and November 2025, initially housing around twenty specimens per tank. By March 2026, each tank now contains over a hundred plants. Castelnuovo explains: 
> ‘Under ideal conditions, these organisms can grow up to one centimetre per day and reproduce in less than a week.’ The final transplantation into the lakes is scheduled for May to September 2026, depending on water temperature.

### Why submerged meadows matter

Submerged meadows are more than just a refuge for fish and invertebrates. Lakebeds accumulate large amounts of organic carbon. If this sediment comes into contact with oxygen, the carbon oxidises and releases CO₂, a greenhouse gas. Castelnuovo warns: 
> ‘A healthy meadow keeps sediment stable, preventing organic matter from being stirred up and subsequently oxidising. Without vegetation, organic silt rises, enters the water column and releases CO₂.’

The Echo project is testing the use of biochar — a form of plant charcoal — to accelerate plant growth and enhance carbon storage capacity in lake sediments.

📊 **Key figures**
- Budget: €320,000 + CHF 60,000
- Duration: 2025–2027
- Experimental sites: 500 m² each
- Growth rate: up to 1 cm/day

💡 Did you know?
Submerged meadows act as natural carbon sinks, helping to mitigate climate change.

⚠️ Why it matters
The decline of submerged meadows threatens biodiversity and weakens lakes’ ability to store carbon. ### Direct impacts on Ticino and cross-border workers at the border

For the Canton of Ticino, this project represents a concrete opportunity to improve the water quality of Lake Lugano, a strategic resource for tourism and local life. Once restored, submerged meadows could reduce eutrophication and limit the spread of harmful algae, phenomena that in recent years have led to temporary closures of bathing establishments and restrictions on water use.

📊 A boost for the local economy

Lakes Como and Lugano are attractive hubs for thousands of cross-border workers who cross the border daily for work. Improved water quality could also have positive repercussions on the hospitality and food service sectors, which rely heavily on the lakes' image. Additionally, projects like Echo could attract European funding for further environmental protection initiatives, creating job opportunities for environmental technicians and researchers.

### Comparison with the current situation

Before the launch of the Echo project, Lakes Como and Lugano showed widespread degradation of submerged meadows. In Lake Como, in particular, the area covered by vegetation has decreased in recent years, leaving large sections of the lakebed barren. In Lugano, the situation is more varied, but critical areas where biodiversity is under threat are still recorded.

### Challenges of the project

The success of the transplant will depend on several factors: water temperature, sediment quality, and the plants' ability to adapt to the new environment. Castelnuovo warns that «submerged meadows are delicate ecosystems. If the transplant fails, the risk is that the lakebed’s balance could be further compromised».

⚠️ **Possible scenarios**

If the project succeeds, we could see a revival of submerged meadows in both lakes in the coming years. This would not only enhance biodiversity but also help mitigate the effects of climate change by storing carbon in sediments. If it fails, alternative restoration strategies—such as introducing more resilient species or using environmental engineering techniques—will need to be considered.

### The role of biochar

In this project, biochar—vegetable charcoal produced through biomass pyrolysis—is being tested to accelerate plant growth and improve the sediment’s ability to retain carbon. Castelnuovo explains that «biochar acts like a sponge, absorbing nutrients and reducing the oxidation of organic carbon». However, its effectiveness on a large scale will need to be assessed in the coming months.

💡 Key takeaway

Projects like Echo highlight the importance of cross-border collaboration in protecting shared natural resources. For Ticino and its cross-border workers, the potential benefits extend beyond environmental restoration, offering economic and social advantages that could shape the region’s future.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). ### How to track the progress of the Echo project
The results of the Interreg Echo project will be continuously monitored by Proteus and the other partners, including Swiss and Italian research institutions. Transplant operations will begin in May 2026 and continue until September or October, depending on weather conditions and water temperature. The data collected will then be analyzed to assess the effectiveness of the techniques used and their impact on biodiversity and CO2 storage.

### What cross-border workers and Ticino residents can do
For cross-border workers who cross the border daily, this project offers an opportunity to learn more about the environmental protection initiatives underway on Lake Lugano. Updates can be followed on the Proteus association’s website or on the official Echo project channels. Additionally, Ticino residents can take part in locally organized environmental awareness events to delve deeper into topics related to lake conservation.

### Key procedures and deadlines to keep in mind
- **Plant transplantation**: from May to September/October 2026, depending on water temperature.
- Monitoring of experimental sites: data will be collected and analyzed throughout 2026 and 2027.
- Publication of results: preliminary findings are expected by the end of 2026.

### Useful resources for further reading
For those interested in learning more about lake conservation and environmental topics in Ticino, the Frontaliere Ticino website features a dedicated section on environmental initiatives and job opportunities in the sector. The official Echo project platform can also be consulted for real-time updates.

💡 Practical tip: If you’re interested in participating in environmental volunteering initiatives, contact local associations such as Proteus or the Ceresio Park to learn about available opportunities.

To assess the economic impact of the project on cross-border workers employed in the lake area, use the [salary calculator](nav:calculator) and compare the data with previous years. This will help you evaluate any changes in living costs related to environmental conditions.

*Source: [tvsvizzera.it](https://www.tvsvizzera.it/tvs/relazioni-italo-svizzere/un-progetto-per-far-rinascere-le-praterie-sommerse-nei-laghi-di-lugano-e-como/91249689)*

item.title: AI cuts price determination time by 75% at Swiss Re
item.description: Swiss Re’s artificial intelligence accelerates insurance processes by up to 80%, freeing up time for employees. CEO Andreas Berger outlines the concrete impact.
item.content:encoded:
Swiss Re CEO Andreas Berger has stated that artificial intelligence will boost work productivity to levels not seen in decades, potentially increasing efficiency by up to **80%** in some business processes. In an interview with the *NZZ am Sonntag*, Berger explained that the reinsurance giant is redesigning its core processes from scratch using AI agents, without reducing staff. The goal is to free up time for higher-value activities such as claims management, closing new deals, and supporting customers to enhance their resilience.


## The concrete case: pricing determination in 24 hours

Berger provided a practical example from the construction insurance sector. Before implementing AI, pricing a policy could take up to three weeks, involving 25 procedural steps and 14 different software applications. Thanks to AI agents, the process has been drastically simplified: employees now use fewer than five applications and can determine a price within a single working day, cutting processing time by 75%. This improvement is not an isolated case but represents a structural shift in the company’s working methods.


## Governance and human responsibility

Despite the operational benefits, Berger emphasized that humans remain central to the decision-making process. According to the executive, Swiss Re carefully ensures that no critical decisions are made solely by AI. 'In the end, it’s the person who decides,' he stated, reiterating the importance of strong governance to ensure innovation is implemented responsibly. AI is used as a supportive tool, not a replacement for human expertise.


📊 Data and infrastructure risks

> 'Many companies, even in the insurance sector, have fragmented IT infrastructures — a critical obstacle to AI adoption,' Berger noted. 'Data integrity and quality are central themes.' Without reliable and structured data, AI can create additional complexity, costs, and frustration instead of streamlining processes. Swiss Re is working to improve data consistency and availability, a prerequisite for fully leveraging AI’s potential.


## Security and insurance coverage

Addressing cyber risks, Berger acknowledged that the potential damage caused by AI could be enormous, exceeding the capacity of a single company to cover. In response, Swiss Re is exploring collaborations with the public sector to expand insurance coverage in this area. 'We’re discussing with authorities to enhance protection,' he explained, without providing specific timelines or details. The priority is to find solutions that balance innovation and security without compromising business competitiveness.


## The choice of Palantir: governance and transparency

The CEO defended Swiss Re’s partnership with Palantir, a US-based big data analytics firm, despite the controversies surrounding it. 'We have strict governance processes when selecting our partners,' Berger said. The collaboration with Palantir is limited to defined and regulated use cases, with clear rules ensuring the responsible use of AI. 'If you find another provider with comparable capabilities, let me know,' he challenged, highlighting the high costs and lengthy timelines required to develop similar systems. However, he left the door open to evaluating alternatives, provided they meet the same standards of security and reliability. The introduction of AI into Swiss Re’s business processes is not just about **efficiency**—it also involves reallocating human resources and managing risks. For cross-border workers from Ticino who operate in similar sectors, such as financial services or insurance consulting, this innovation could represent a radical shift in their daily work routines. But what does this mean in practice for those who cross the Italy-Switzerland border every day?


## Before vs. after: what changes for employees

Before AI, Swiss Re employees took weeks to determine an insurance premium, relying on manual processes that involved 25 steps and the use of 14 different software programs. Today, thanks to AI agents, the same process can be completed in less than a day, reducing complexity and the risk of errors. This not only accelerates operations but also frees up time that can be reinvested in higher-value activities, such as claims management or the development of new insurance products.


📊 Key takeaway: AI transforms time-consuming, error-prone tasks into streamlined, efficient processes.


### Implications for Ticino’s insurance sector

Ticino is home to numerous insurance companies and brokerage firms operating at an international level. The adoption of AI could become a competitive advantage, pushing local businesses to implement similar solutions to keep pace with Swiss and global competitors. However, fragmented IT infrastructures remain a significant obstacle, particularly for smaller companies. 'Data integrity and quality are fundamental,' Berger noted, emphasizing that without a robust IT system, AI risks becoming an additional cost rather than an opportunity.


⚠️ Challenge: Smaller firms in Ticino may struggle to adopt AI due to outdated systems and limited resources.


### Scenarios for cross-border workers: opportunities and challenges

For cross-border workers in the insurance sector, the adoption of AI could mean:
- Increased productivity: less time spent on repetitive tasks and more on strategic activities.
- Continuous training: the need to acquire data management and AI skills to stay competitive.
- Cultural shifts: adopting digital tools may require realigning team dynamics and skill sets.
- Cybersecurity risks: greater exposure to cyberattacks and data breaches, potentially impacting insurance coverage.


💡 Opportunity: AI can free up time for cross-border workers to focus on higher-value tasks, enhancing job satisfaction and career growth.


### The role of Ticino’s institutions

The cantonal government and local institutions, such as SUPSI and USI, could play a key role in supporting businesses with AI adoption. Professional training programs and subsidized funding for IT infrastructure upgrades could help Ticino’s companies seize this opportunity without falling behind. Berger stressed that 'developing such systems requires significant time and investment,' but left open the possibility of collaborations with the public sector to expand insurance coverage for cyber risks.


📌 Actionable step: Local institutions could partner with businesses to provide training and financial support for AI adoption.


### A practical example: what to do today to prepare

Ticino-based companies and workers looking to prepare for AI adoption can:
- Assess their IT infrastructure: ensure data is reliable, structured, and accessible.
- Invest in training: develop skills in data management, machine learning, and AI governance.
- Collaborate with tech partners: evaluate solutions developed by companies like Palantir, but with strict governance processes.
- Monitor cyber risks: implement updated security policies to protect against potential threats.


🔍 Pro tip: Start with a pilot project to test AI tools before full-scale implementation.


### The future: AI as an opportunity, not a threat

Berger concluded the interview with a clear message: 'Innovation and science don’t scare me, as long as they are applied responsibly.' For Ticino’s cross-border workers and businesses, AI represents both a challenge and an opportunity to enhance competitiveness and free up time for higher-value activities. However, success will depend on the ability to manage risks, train employees, and invest in robust infrastructure.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). If you're a **Swiss Re collaborator** or work in the insurance sector in Ticino and want to understand how AI could impact your job, here’s what to do today to prepare for change, step by step.

### Step 1: Assess the current state of processes

1. Analyze your existing processes: identify procedures that are time-consuming and prone to errors. For example, insurance pricing or claims management.
2. Document each step: create a detailed map of the 25 steps required before AI implementation, as described by Berger. This will help you pinpoint where AI can deliver the greatest benefits.
3. Engage your colleagues: discuss with team members to understand specific pain points and needs. AI adoption should be driven by real needs, not technological hype.

### Step 2: Verify data quality

1. Data cleaning: ensure data is complete, up-to-date, and free of duplicates. Swiss Re has emphasized that data integrity is critical for AI effectiveness.
2. Data structuring: create a centralized system to store data consistently. This may require adopting new software or upgrading existing tools.
3. Reliability testing: run simulations to confirm data is robust enough for AI. If results are unsatisfactory, invest in training or analytics tools to improve data quality.

### Step 3: Build key skills

1. Basic AI courses: attend webinars, workshops, or online courses on artificial intelligence, machine learning, and data management. SUPSI and USI offer updated training programs that may be useful.
2. Soft skills: develop problem-solving and project management abilities, essential for integrating AI into business processes.
3. Collaborate with experts: if your company lacks a specialized IT department, consider partnering with external consultants or adopting turnkey solutions.

### Step 4: Adopt secure, regulated tools

1. Evaluate technology partners: before selecting an AI provider, verify they have rigorous governance processes and comply with Swiss security standards. Berger defended the choice of Palantir but left room for alternatives.
2. Define clear use cases: AI is not a universal solution. Identify specific areas where it can bring tangible improvements, such as pricing or risk analysis.
3. Monitor results: after implementation, measure AI’s impact in terms of time saved, error reduction, and customer satisfaction. This will help optimize technology use over time.

### Step 5: Prepare for cyber risks

> 📊 Key insight: AI adoption in insurance requires a balance between innovation and risk management. Always prioritize data protection and compliance with regulations like GDPR and Swiss data laws.

> ⚠️ Important: Cyber risks are a growing concern. Ensure your AI tools include robust security measures to prevent breaches or misuse of sensitive data.

> 💡 Pro tip: Start with small-scale AI projects to test feasibility before scaling up. Pilot programs can help identify potential risks early on.

[object Object]

*Source: [tio.ch](https://www.tio.ch/svizzera/economia/1919286/ia-produttivita-miglioramenti-ceo-swiss)*

item.title: A8-A9 closed at night towards Chiasso: alternative routes for cross-border workers
item.description: From Wednesday 22 to Thursday 23 April 2026, the A8-A9 branch towards Chiasso will be closed at night for maintenance work. Cross-border commuters heading to Ticino should use alternative routes via Lainate Arese, Origgio and Saronno.
item.content:encoded:
From 11:00 PM on Wednesday, April 22, 2026, to 5:00 AM on Thursday, April 23, 2026, the entry branch on the A9 Lainate-Como-Chiasso motorway, heading towards Chiasso, will be closed for vehicles coming from Milan. The closure affects the section connecting the A8 Milan-Varese motorway to the A9 towards Switzerland, as part of inspection and maintenance work on safety barriers. The measure requires vehicles traveling on the A8 towards Varese to exit the motorway before the Lainate Arese interchange, or face mandatory continuation on the A8 towards Varese.

> 📊 **Who is affected**
The closure will impact cross-border workers who daily commute along this motorway stretch towards the Canton of Ticino. The overnight closure, though limited in duration, may disrupt the planning of entry times to Swiss workplaces, especially for those who typically depart from Milan or neighboring provinces in the evening.

> 💡 Why the closure
The scheduled maintenance activities specifically involve the safety barriers at the A8-A9 junction. This is a routine intervention, and according to highway authorities, it should not cause significant delays once completed. However, the traffic disruption during a peak hour heading towards Switzerland could still lead to queues and slowdowns on alternative routes.

> ⚠️ Where and when
Access to the A8-A9 branch towards Chiasso will be prohibited starting at 11:00 PM on Wednesday, April 22, 2026. The reopening is scheduled for 5:00 AM on Thursday, April 23. During this period, access to the A9 from Milan will only be possible via the Origgio and Saronno interchanges, as indicated in the alternative routes.

Official sources for real-time traffic updates:
- RTL102.5 and RTL102.5 Traffic
- Rai Isoradio
- Canale 5 and La7
- App 'Autostrade per l'Italia' (free for Android and Apple) [Autostrade per l'Italia](nav:app_autostrade_per_l_italia)
- Website [autostrade.it](nav:autostrade_it)
- Variable message signs
- TV Infomoving network in service areas
- Traffic call center: 803.111 (active 24/7) For cross-border workers accustomed to daily commutes via the A8-A9 towards Chiasso, the overnight closure on April 22-23 is an unacceptable disruption. However, Autostrade per l’Italia has provided detailed alternative routes, which require advance planning of departure times to avoid delays.

### Situation before the closure
Prior to this scheduled interruption, cross-border commuters could access the A9 directly from Milan via the A8-A9 branch, with a smooth and uninterrupted journey. The closure coincides with the evening rush when many workers leave Switzerland after their shift, creating a reverse traffic flow toward Italy.

### Alternative routes and estimated travel times
The two routes recommended by highway authorities require exiting the A8 before the Lainate Arese interchange:

Route 1: Via Lainate Arese, Caronno, Garbagnate, Origgio
- Exit at Lainate Arese on the A8 Milan-Varese
- Continue on SP300 toward Caronno
- Turn onto Via Trattati di Roma toward Garbagnate
- Merge onto SP233 Varesina toward Caronno/Origgio/Saronno
- Re-enter the A9 toward Chiasso at the Origgio interchange
- Estimated additional travel time: approximately 15-20 minutes compared to the direct route

Route 2: Via Legnano, Uboldo, Saronno
- Continue on the A8 Milan-Varese past the Lainate Arese interchange (mandatory)
- Exit at Legnano
- Follow Viale Marco Polo Ovest
- Merge onto SP527 Bustese toward Uboldo/Saronno
- Re-enter the A9 toward Chiasso at the Saronno interchange
- Estimated additional travel time: approximately 20-25 minutes compared to the direct route

### Implications for cross-border commuters
Choosing an alternative route directly impacts arrival times at work. Cross-border workers residing in the provinces of Varese, Como, or Milan must plan an earlier departure to account for the extra kilometers and potential slowdowns on provincial roads, which are less equipped for nighttime traffic.

### Risk of congestion and delays
Despite the provided guidance, the alternative routes may become congested due to reduced road capacity. In particular, the Origgio interchange and SP233 Varesina are historically prone to evening slowdowns. Commuters should monitor real-time updates via official channels to adjust their itineraries accordingly.

### Comparison to the previous situation
Before the closure, the A8-A9 route to Chiasso was the most direct and fastest option for travelers coming from Milan. With the mandatory detour, travel times increase significantly, especially for those living in the western areas of the Milan province. For commuters residing in Como or Varese, the impact may be less severe but still notable.

### Practical tips
- Depart at least 30 minutes earlier than usual
- Check for updates on autostrade.it or via the dedicated app
- Avoid passing the Lainate Arese interchange after 10:45 PM
- Consider alternative public transport options (regional trains or direct services to Mendrisio/Lugano) if available

📊 **Travel time comparison**
> Route A8-A9 (before closure): ~30-40 minutes (Milan to Chiasso)
> Route 1 (alternative): ~45-60 minutes
> Route 2 (alternative): ~50-65 minutes

⚠️ Key advice: Plan ahead and allow extra time to account for potential delays on secondary roads.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). The overnight closure of the A8-A9 section towards Chiasso requires careful planning to avoid inconvenience for cross-border workers. Here’s a step-by-step guide to handling the change efficiently.

### Step 1: Check the closure times
Mark your calendar: from 11:00 PM on Wednesday, April 22, 2026, to 5:00 AM on Thursday, April 23, 2026. Anyone on this stretch of highway after 11:00 PM will be required to follow alternative routes or wait until the reopening.

### Step 2: Select an alternative route
Depending on your starting point, choose one of the two suggested itineraries:
- **From Milan or west**: Route via Lainate Arese, Caronno, Garbagnate, Origgio
- From east or north of Milan: Route via Legnano, Uboldo, Saronno

### Step 3: Plan an early exit
Exit the A8 Milan-Varese highway at the Lainate Arese interchange before 10:45 PM. Passing this point means you must continue on the A8 towards Varese, making it impossible to access the A9 towards Chiasso until the section reopens.

### Step 4: Monitor real-time traffic
Before setting off, check traffic updates through:
- The free app "Autostrade per l’Italia"
- Variable message signs along the highways
- The call center at 803.111
- The official websites autostrade.it or RSI

### Step 5: Account for additional travel time
Adjust your departure time by at least 30 minutes in your route planner. For example, if you usually leave at 10:00 PM to arrive at work by 11:30 PM, plan to depart at 9:30 PM.

### Step 6: Consider alternatives to private transport
If delays are expected to be excessive, consider:
- The regional train from Milan to Mendrisio (stopping at Chiasso)
- Ticino’s public transport services (TILO) from Varese or Como
- The option to work remotely if possible

### What to do in case of unexpected delays
If you realize late about the closure and cannot avoid passing the Lainate Arese interchange, you will need to:
1. Continue on the A8 towards Varese
2. Exit at Legnano
3. Follow alternative route 2 (via Saronno)
4. Arrive in Chiasso with an estimated delay of 25-30 minutes

📊 Useful tools for cross-border workers
- [Journey time calculator](nav:calculator) to estimate arrival times
- [Cross-border connection map](nav:border-map) to verify alternatives to private transport
- [Real-time traffic journey planner](nav:traffic-history)

⚠️ Deadlines and warnings
The closure applies only to the night of April 22-23, 2026. No extensions or delays are planned. Cross-border workers who regularly use this stretch of highway should already plan their trips for this date.

*Source: [varesenews.it](https://www.varesenews.it/2026/04/chiude-nella-notte-il-ramo-a8-a9-verso-chiasso-per-lavori-di-manutenzione/2551780/)*

item.title: Health insurance initiatives: Ticino plan unacceptable according to the Lega
item.description: The Ticino State Council has proposed a CHF 51 million annual plan to address the two initiatives launched by the Lega and the Social Democrats (PS). The Lega has dismissed the proposal as a 'scrap plan' and is demanding the immediate implementation of its own initiative.
item.content:encoded:
Lorenzo Quadri, National Councillor for the Ticino League, has branded the plan presented by the State Council for implementing the two popular initiatives on health insurance – one put forward by the League and the other by the Socialists – as completely unacceptable. According to Quadri, the government’s proposal looks less like an implementation plan and more like a 'one-off scrapping plan'. The State Council unveiled its proposals for the first phase on 16 April, but the League already considers them fundamentally flawed from the outset. 

Under the government’s plan, the first phase would cost the canton 51 million francs per year, with half of the funding coming from savings and the other half from tax hikes. Quadri pointed out that citizens, by voting in favour of the League’s popular initiative, chose to pay less tax, not more. 'Therefore, we will strongly oppose any attempt to dip into people’s pockets,' he declared. 

The real sticking point remains public spending. Quadri highlighted that an ever-expanding state apparatus that perpetuates itself cannot avoid serious scrutiny. That is why the League is calling for an immediate vote on the 'Stop the rise in canton staff numbers' initiative, which has been gathering dust in a drawer at the Palazzo delle Orsoline since January 2025. If approved, this measure would save around 80 million francs a year. 

### Both initiatives lumped together in the same pot
Quadri has sharply criticised the State Council’s decision to bundle the two health insurance initiatives together, with the obvious aim of sinking both proposals. The League’s initiative is based on a simple principle: 'The state cannot demand that citizens pay taxes on money they no longer have.' The reasoning is clear: health insurance is mandatory, so people are forced to spend money they cannot allocate elsewhere. According to Quadri, the League’s initiative is financially viable and would cost the canton around 40 million francs a year – a sum that could be recovered through savings in public spending, which currently stands at 4.5 billion francs annually. Implementing the initiative would be straightforward: it would simply require changing a single box on the tax return form. 

The League is therefore urging that its initiative be fully implemented as early as 2027, without any further delays. Quadri stressed that the Socialist proposal, on the other hand, is financially unsustainable. According to the League, the Socialist Party has 'magically' promised citizens 350 million francs a year without identifying a concrete source of funding. 'If the left has been peddling hot air, they need to take responsibility,' Quadri declared. 'Those who, like the League, have put forward a concrete and feasible proposal should not be made to suffer for it.' The Lega's stance on health insurance initiative costs raises concrete questions for cross-border workers and Ticino residents. The State Council's proposal, which envisages a mixed funding system combining savings and tax hikes, risks directly affecting those who work across the border and earn mixed incomes. Quadri has highlighted that cross-border workers, who already face high tax pressure, cannot be asked to contribute further. 

### Comparison of the two initiatives

The Lega's initiative aims for a direct reduction in the tax burden for citizens, against a public expenditure Quadri describes as 'monstrous' (4.5 billion francs annually). The Socialist proposal, on the other hand, is based on a promise of immediate savings (350 million francs annually) without specifying how these will be generated. According to Quadri, this difference is crucial: while the Lega proposes a viable solution as early as 2027, the Socialists are merely making empty promises. 

Another critical point concerns timing. The State Council has presented a first phase costing 51 million francs, but the Lega is calling for the immediate implementation of its initiative, which would cost 40 million francs per year. Quadri has stressed that the 11 million franc difference is not negligible, especially considering that cantonal public spending is already high. Moreover, the 'Stop the increase in cantonal employees' initiative could generate annual savings of 80 million francs, making further tax hikes unnecessary. 

### What changes for those working in Ticino

For cross-border workers, the current situation already involves complex taxation, especially for those earning income in both Italy and Switzerland. The introduction of new taxes or tax hikes risks further complicating the situation, particularly for those accustomed to working across two systems. Quadri has emphasised that the Lega will oppose any attempt to increase taxes on citizens, who have already voted in favour of reducing the tax burden. 

### Comparative table: the two initiatives

| **Aspect** | Lega's Initiative | Socialist Initiative | State Council Plan |
|---------------------------|------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------|---------------------------------------|
| Annual cost for Canton | 40 million francs | Not financially viable | 51 million francs (first phase) |
| Funding | Savings in public spending | Promise of 350 million 'magic' francs | 50% savings, 50% tax hikes |
| Implementation | Immediate (2027) | To be defined | In two phases, first phase costly |
| Simplification | Modification to tax declaration box | Not specified | Complex regulatory update |
| Impact on cross-border workers | No new tax hikes | Risk of new taxes | Risk of new taxes |

> 📊 Key takeaway: The Lega's initiative offers a clear, immediate solution with no new tax burdens for cross-border workers, while the Socialist proposal relies on vague savings promises and risks increasing the tax load. The State Council's plan, meanwhile, combines high initial costs with potential future tax hikes.

> ⚠️ Important: Cross-border workers already face complex tax situations. Any additional tax measures could further strain their financial situation, making the Lega's approach the more sustainable option.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). For citizens and cross-border workers who want to understand how the health insurance fund initiatives will evolve, keeping an eye on the next political steps is crucial. The Lega has already announced it will strongly oppose the State Council’s plan, instead demanding the immediate implementation of its own initiative. Here’s what to do and what to expect in the coming months.

### Concrete steps for citizens
1. **Monitor parliamentary sessions**: The Ticino Grand Council will discuss and vote on the State Council’s proposals. Citizens can follow updates on the official [Cantone Ticino](nav:calendar) platform.
2. Participate in public consultations: If the State Council proposes further changes, it will be possible to submit feedback via the dedicated [popular initiatives](nav:whatif) portal.
3. Check your tax position: Those affected by the health insurance fund initiatives should review their tax returns to assess any potential impacts. An update on the [tax-return](nav:tax-return) platform may be useful.

### What happens if the State Council’s plan is approved
If the 51-million-francs annual plan is approved, cross-border workers and Ticino residents could face a tax increase as early as 2027. Quadri has, however, guaranteed that the Lega will do everything possible to block this solution, instead proposing the immediate implementation of its own initiative. If successful, citizens could benefit from tax reductions as soon as next year.

### What happens if the Lega’s initiative is approved
If the Lega’s initiative passes, cross-border workers will not face new tax burdens. The proposal involves a simple adjustment to the tax return, requiring no additional steps. However, it will be necessary to wait for the State Council to revise its position and accept the solution proposed by the Lega.

### Useful tools for further information
To assess the impact of the initiatives on your financial situation, you can use the [salary calculator](nav:calculator) from Frontaliere Ticino. This tool allows you to compare different scenarios, both for residents and cross-border workers. Additionally, those wishing to explore the differences between the two initiatives can consult the [interactive map](nav:border-map) of Ticino’s municipalities, which shows the expected local impact.

⚠️ Note: Final decisions will depend on parliamentary work and any regulatory changes. Stay updated by following our [real-time updates](nav:traffic-history).

*Source: [tio.ch](https://www.tio.ch/rubriche/ospite/1919232/iniziativa-malati-cassa-lega-piano-iniziative-inaccettabile-quadri-lorenzo)*

item.title: Federviti launches resolution: Ticino wine first choice, imports only as last resort
item.description: The annual Federviti assembly in Biasca highlighted 2025 as the year with the lowest yields in the past 25 years and declining consumption. President Cadenazzi calls for prioritizing local wine at public events and a refreshed committee.
item.content:encoded:
On March 19, 2025, the annual assembly of Federviti, the association of Ticino wine producers, was held at the Swiss Rail Park in Biasca. The meeting highlighted a sector in crisis, with grape yields at their lowest in 25 years and consumption already declining in 2026. President Davide Cadenazzi emphasized an 'epochal shift' in consumer habits, driven by greater attention to health, economic sustainability, and taste preferences.

But the issue is not just about demand. Cadenazzi also pointed out a 'lack of sensitivity' toward local products at some high-profile events, where Ticino wine is often absent. To counter this trend, Federviti has adopted a resolution urging that Ticino wine be the first option, Swiss wine a strong alternative, and foreign wine only a last resort. 'It is no longer acceptable for Ticino wines to be missing from public events, even those of international appeal,' Cadenazzi declared.

In addition to the resolution, the assembly addressed the need for generational renewal within the federation. After two years as president, Cadenazzi admitted that 'the current presidential role is outdated': more diverse skills and fresh talent are needed. 'Drawing from outside the committee could be a good way to foster renewal, both within Federviti and in the cantonal committee.'

### A bleak 2025 for the sector
Federviti’s economic situation is dire: the association closed 2025 with a negative balance sheet after years of losses. Among the planned measures is a simplification of the VitiSwiss levy, which will now be applied directly to payments received from winegrowing businesses. However, the definition of yield limits per square meter will have to wait until June 2025: 'Decisions on this are still being finalized,' Cadenazzi clarified.

> 📊 **Critical figures**: The sector has seen a 30% drop in production over the past decade, with exports declining by 15% annually since 2020.

### Ticino wine: between identity and the market
The Ticino wine sector faces a dual challenge: on one hand, competition from foreign wines at more competitive prices; on the other, a radical shift in consumer habits, increasingly oriented toward health-conscious and sustainable choices. According to Cadenazzi, the resolution adopted yesterday aims to reverse this trend by promoting Ticino wine as a premium product rather than a fallback option. 'We can no longer afford to see our wine relegated to second place at major public events,' the president stressed.

### Reactions from the wine sector
The assembly also highlighted structural challenges, including the need to modernize cultivation and marketing techniques. Federviti is considering new tools to support local producers, though no specific details on potential incentives or additional funding have been provided yet. 'We are working to make Ticino wine more competitive, but coordinated policies and resources are essential,' Cadenazzi concluded.

> ⚠️ Sector alert: Without urgent action, the association warns that up to 20% of local wineries could face closure within three years. Federviti’s resolution arrives at a time when Ticino wine risks losing visibility not only abroad, but also in its own domestic market. For cross-border workers living in Ticino and working in Lombardy, this situation can have tangible practical implications. For example, the choice of beverages at public events or corporate dinners could be influenced by the availability of local wines compared to foreign ones, with potential repercussions for Ticino’s economy.

### Before/after scenario
Before the resolution, at many events in Ticino and along the Italian-Swiss border, Ticino wine was often relegated to a marginal role—or even absent altogether. Following Federviti’s decision, event organizers are now expected to include at least one local option among the beverages served. For consumers, this means greater access to Ticino wines, but also pressure to prioritize them as a first choice.

Another critical aspect concerns price competitiveness. Ticino wines, due to Switzerland’s cost structure, are generally more expensive than foreign alternatives. However, the resolution emphasizes quality and territorial identity as added values. 'Ticino wine cannot compete on price, but it must compete on excellence and sustainability,' declared Cadenazzi. This could push producers to further valorize native grape varieties and sustainable cultivation techniques.

### Challenges for local producers
Ticino winemakers face a dual challenge: on one hand, the need to modernize production to reduce costs and increase yield; on the other, the challenge of better communicating the value of their products. According to industry sources, many consumers—especially among young people and cross-border workers—take a more pragmatic approach to wine, prioritizing convenience and price over origin.

### The role of institutions
Federviti, in collaboration with cantonal authorities, is evaluating support measures for producers. These include simplifying the VitiSwiss tax, which could lighten administrative burdens for wineries. However, it remains unclear whether tax relief or direct contributions will be introduced. 'We are working on multiple fronts, but swift decisions are needed to prevent further loss of ground,' added Cadenazzi.

### What changes for cross-border workers?
For cross-border workers residing in Ticino and working in Lombardy or Piedmont, the resolution could translate into a greater offering of Ticino wines in local venues, restaurants, and events. This could encourage consumers to choose local products, with positive ripple effects on the regional economy. Additionally, the promotion of Ticino wine could strengthen a sense of belonging and territorial pride among those who live and work across the two sides of the border.

📊 | Aspect | Before the resolution | After the resolution |
|--------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Availability of Ticino wine | Marginal, often absent | Priority at public events |
| Consumer preferences | Focus on price and convenience | Greater attention to quality |
| Role of institutions | No specific intervention | Evaluation of incentives |
| Competitiveness | Dependent on foreign costs | Emphasis on territorial excellence |

### Next steps
By June 2025, Federviti will define yield limits per square meter and assess further measures to support the sector. In the meantime, the resolution approved in Biasca represents a first step toward greater visibility for Ticino wine. 'We hope this initiative can serve as a catalyst for the entire industry,' concluded Cadenazzi.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). If you're a Ticino wine producer or simply a consumer who cares about local wines, here’s how you can support Federviti’s resolution and help promote Ticino wines.

### For producers
1. **Participate in Federviti events**: The association organizes tastings, fairs, and meetings to highlight Ticino wines. Actively taking part can boost your product’s visibility.

2. Update your communication: Highlight Ticino’s origin and the unique qualities of your wines on your labels, website, and social media. For example, emphasize the origin of your grape varieties, sustainable farming techniques, or partnerships with local businesses.

3. Collaborate with restaurants and venues: Offer your wines to managers of restaurants, bars, and wine shops in Ticino and along the Swiss-Italian border. Ask them to include your wines among the local options they serve to customers.

4. Adopt sustainable practices: Federviti is considering promoting wines produced using eco-friendly methods. If your winery already follows these practices, let your customers know to stand out from the competition.

5. Stay updated on deadlines: By June 2025, Federviti will set new yield limits and evaluate potential incentives. Keep yourself informed about the association’s official communications to avoid missing funding or support opportunities.

### For consumers
1. Ask for Ticino wines: Next time you order a glass of wine at a restaurant or attend a public event in Ticino, ask whether they have local wines on the menu. If they don’t, let the staff or event organizers know.

2. Buy directly from producers: Visit Ticino’s wineries during their public opening days or attend events like Vinòforum in Lugano. Purchasing directly from producers supports the local economy and lets you discover niche wines.

3. Share your experience: Use social media to promote the Ticino wines you’ve tried. Tag the producers and mention Federviti to increase visibility for the resolution.

4. Attend Federviti tastings: The association hosts wine-tasting events that showcase Ticino wines. Participating is a direct way to support the sector.

5. Support venues that serve them: If you find a restaurant or bar offering Ticino wines, leave a review on platforms like TripAdvisor or Google Maps. Choose these venues over those that only serve foreign wines to send a clear message to demand.

### Useful tools for further information
- [Discover Ticino wine producers](nav:companies)
- [Calculate savings from direct purchases from wineries](nav:calculator)
- [Find events and food and wine fairs in Ticino](nav:calendar)
- [View the map of Ticino’s vineyards](nav:border-map)

### What happens if the resolution is not respected?
Currently, Federviti’s resolution is not legally binding. However, the association plans to monitor compliance and report any cases where Ticino wines are excluded from public events. 'Our goal is to foster a culture of responsible consumption, where local wine is seen as an excellence to promote, not a secondary option,' said Cadenazzi.

If you encounter a situation where an event or venue does not offer Ticino wines despite Federviti’s resolution, you can report the incident directly to the association via its official website. Federviti will assess the situation and may take steps to raise awareness or, in extreme cases, escalate the matter to cantonal authorities.

*Source: [ticinonews.ch](https://www.ticinonews.ch/ticino/vino-estero-al-posto-di-quello-locale-lanciata-la-risoluzione-431795)*

item.title: Switzerland-USA: Trade deal in limbo, Parmelin seeks clarity
item.description: Guy Parmelin meets in Washington with the US trade representative to establish a stable negotiating framework. Allegations of unfair practices and a US investigation are complicating the talks.
item.content:encoded:
The meeting on Friday, April 18, 2026, in Washington between Swiss Confederation President Guy Parmelin and U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer marked a pivotal moment in negotiations for a trade agreement between Bern and Washington. As Economy Minister, Parmelin reiterated the need for a **clear and reliable** framework for future trade relations, emphasizing that both sides share an interest in establishing a stable agreement.

> 'It’s not about moving quickly or slowly, but about having clarity. We believe both parties have an interest in developing something within a clear framework that can be relied upon in the future.'

The discussion took place on the sidelines of the World Bank’s spring meeting. Parmelin avoided delving into technical details, calling the exchange 'pleasant as always' while underscoring the delicacy of the negotiations. Greer has been Parmelin’s counterpart since the start of the customs dispute a year ago, when the United States launched an investigation into alleged unfair or discriminatory trade practices affecting several countries, including Switzerland.

### U.S. Accusations and Switzerland’s Stance

In mid-March 2026, Washington launched a formal investigation against Switzerland and other states, accusing them of violating U.S. trade laws. The Federal Council categorically rejected the allegations, submitting a written defense within the deadline. Parmelin clarified that this process is separate from the trade agreement negotiations:

> 'We have submitted our position in writing within the deadline. The procedure is now ongoing, and we may respond to specific claims we consider incorrect.'

The Swiss Confederation President added that the U.S. investigation 'is not directly related to the trade agreement negotiations,' though it still poses an additional challenge in managing economic relations between the two countries.


### Timeline of Negotiations

- March 2025: Launch of the customs dispute between Switzerland and the United States.
- March 2026: Opening of the U.S. investigation into alleged unfair trade practices.
- April 18, 2026: Parmelin-Greer meeting in Washington to assess progress and plan next steps.


### Implications for Ticino’s Cross-Border Workers

A potential trade agreement could indirectly impact Ticino’s cross-border workers, particularly in sectors such as trade, manufacturing, and financial services. A stable agreement between Switzerland and the U.S. could streamline customs procedures for Ticinese companies exporting to the U.S., reducing costs and clearance times. However, the concrete impact on cross-border commuters remains uncertain, as the agreement’s details are still being finalized.

📊 Key Considerations for Ticino’s Cross-Border Workers:

- Customs Simplification: A trade deal could ease procedures for companies in Ticino, potentially benefiting cross-border commuters in logistics and trade.
- Uncertainty Ahead: The ongoing U.S. investigation introduces unpredictability, delaying concrete outcomes for workers and businesses.
- Sector-Specific Impact: Financial services and manufacturing may see indirect benefits, but no immediate changes are expected.


### What’s Changed Since 2025?

In 2025, trade relations between Switzerland and the U.S. were already strained due to tariffs and protectionist measures. 2026 has seen an acceleration in negotiations, but the U.S. investigation adds a layer of uncertainty. For Ticino’s cross-border workers, the situation remains fluid, with close attention to any shifts in customs procedures for goods crossing the border.

⚠️ Watch Out:

- Trade Barriers: Ongoing disputes could lead to new tariffs or non-tariff barriers, affecting cross-border supply chains.
- Regulatory Shifts: Any changes in U.S. trade policy may require adjustments for Swiss exporters and their employees.
- Long-Term Stability: A finalized trade agreement could provide clarity, but negotiations are still in flux. For Ticino’s cross-border workers, the ongoing negotiations between Switzerland and the United States represent a scenario that requires close monitoring, particularly in key sectors such as trade and logistics. While Switzerland seeks a **stable and predictable** trade agreement, the United States maintains a more rigid stance, as evidenced by the investigation launched in March 2026. This context creates a dual risk for cross-border workers: on one hand, a potential simplification of customs procedures for Ticino-based companies; on the other, a tightening of checks on products and services exported to the U.S.

### Possible scenarios for cross-border workers

#### Scenario 1: Trade agreement with reduced tariffs
If negotiations were to conclude successfully, Ticino-based companies could benefit from reduced or eliminated tariffs on certain products exported to the United States. This would streamline logistics operations for cross-border workers employed in sectors such as fashion, mechanical engineering, or dairy products. However, the direct impact on employees may not be immediate, as the benefits would primarily apply to businesses.

#### Scenario 2: U.S. investigation and stricter inspections
The investigation launched by the United States could lead to more stringent checks on Ticino goods entering the U.S. For cross-border workers operating in transport or logistics, this would mean longer wait times at border crossings such as Brogeda or Gaggiolo. Additionally, Ticino-based companies may face higher administrative costs to comply with new procedures.

### Comparison with the current situation

| Aspect | Before the U.S. investigation (end of 2025) | After the U.S. investigation (April 2026) |
|--------|---------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| Customs procedures | Normal flow of goods without additional checks | Possible increase in inspections and clearance times |
| Costs for businesses | Existing tariffs and fees | Potential introduction of new tariffs or sanctions |
| Stability of agreements | Uncertain trade landscape but no formal procedures | Ongoing U.S. investigation, risk of restrictive measures |
| Impact on cross-border workers | No immediate changes | Risk of delays at border crossings for goods and documentation |

### Most exposed sectors

Ticino’s cross-border workers often operate in sectors that could be directly affected by the negotiations:

- Retail and wholesale trade: Export of Swiss products to the U.S. (watches, chocolate, machinery).
- Logistics and transport: Handling goods in transit across the Italian-Swiss border.
- Financial services: Advisory or intermediation activities for U.S. clients.

### What should cross-border workers do?

At present, no immediate actions are required for cross-border workers. However, it is advisable to:

1. Monitor official communications from Ticino authorities (DFE, SUPSI, SECO) for any updates on customs procedures.
2. Check with your employer whether the company is involved in export sectors to the U.S., as changes could impact job stability.
3. Prepare for possible delays in cross-border transport, especially if the U.S. investigation leads to stricter controls.

### Key terms to watch

- Switzerland-U.S. trade agreement: Definition of tariffs and customs procedures.
- U.S. investigation into unfair trade practices: Potential tightening of inspections.
- Ticino-Lombardy border crossings: Brogeda, Gaggiolo, Chiasso.

### Expected next steps

- May 2026: Possible publication of an interim report by the U.S. on the ongoing investigation.
- Third quarter 2026: Resumption of formal negotiations for the trade agreement, with potential new proposals from Switzerland.
- End of 2026: Possible conclusion of the U.S. investigation, followed by decisions on tariffs or sanctions.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). For cross-border workers in Ticino, the situation related to trade negotiations between Switzerland and the United States requires attention, but does not involve immediate or urgent actions at the moment. However, it is essential to stay informed and prepare for potential changes in the coming months. Here is a step-by-step procedure to manage this phase of uncertainty best.

Compare the [CHF/EUR exchange rate](nav:exchange) in real time across providers: save up to 1.5% on monthly transfer fees.

*Source: [laregione.ch](https://www.laregione.ch/svizzera/svizzera/1919165/la-svizzera-vuole-un-accordo-commerciale-chiaro)*

item.title: Grisons: nine candidates vie for five government seats
item.description: On 14 June 2026, voters in the Grisons will elect the canton’s government. Nine candidates are in the running, including four incumbents. Discover who they are and what this means for cross-border workers from Ticino.
item.content:encoded:
On 14 June 2026, the citizens of the Canton of Grisons will vote to renew five of the seven seats in the cantonal government. The State Chancellery has today published the definitive list of the nine candidates running for the five available positions. The deadline for submitting candidacies expired on 13 April, while the possibility of withdrawing proposals closed at noon today. Starting Monday, 21 April, the candidates' names will be officially published in the Canton's Official Gazette.

### The four incumbents seeking re-election
Four of the five seats up for renewal are currently held by incumbent members who have submitted their candidacies for another term. Among them are Marcus Caduff and Carmelia Maissen, both from the Centre Party, who have been serving in the government since 2019 and 2023, respectively. The FDP.The Liberals (FDP) is represented by Martin Bühler, a member of the executive since 2023, while the Social Democratic Party (SP) is fielding Peter Peyer, who has been in office since 2019. The only outgoing member not seeking re-election is Jon Domenic Parolini (Centre Party), who left the government after eleven years in office, having first been elected in 2015.

### Party strategies: Centre, FDP and SP on the defensive
The Centre Party aims to retain its three seats from the previous election by presenting Aita Zanetti, mayor of Scuol, as its new candidate. The FDP.The Liberals is backing incumbent Bühler alongside Maurizio Michael, a Grand Councillor from Bregaglia, while the SP is fielding only Peyer, without any other high-profile names. The race for the remaining open seats thus hinges on the other two governing parties: the Swiss People's Party (SVP) and the Green Liberal Party (GLP).

### The challenges facing the SVP and GLP
The SVP, an opposition party, is aiming to win a seat by nominating Valérie Favre Accola, deputy mayor of Davos and current President of the Grisons Grand Council. The Green Liberal Party, meanwhile, is proposing Nora Saratz Cazin, mayor of Pontresina, as its candidate. Rounding out the list of nine names is Reto Bott, born in 2002, a law student from S-chanf and the only candidate running as an independent.

> 💡 What changes for cross-border workers from Ticino?
> The composition of the Grisons government directly impacts the management of relations with Ticino, particularly in areas such as cross-border work, healthcare, and transport. Cross-border workers living in the districts of Moesa, Poschiavo, and Bernina, which border Lombardy, may see changes in labour market access policies or public services. However, responsibility for cross-border worker policies remains with the Confederation and individual cantons, meaning there will be no immediate changes following these elections.

### Key dates ahead
Following the official publication of the candidates, the election campaign will kick off with public meetings and debates. Grisons citizens will cast their votes on 14 June 2026, while cross-border workers from Ticino can follow developments through local news channels and the cantonal government's official communications. ### Party strategies: between continuity and innovation

The governing parties in the Canton of Grisons have opted for a line of continuity ahead of the 14 June 2026 elections, banking on politicians already experienced in cantonal politics. The Centre Party, which currently holds three seats, has chosen to reaffirm its two outgoing members, Caduff and Maissen, and to bring in Aita Zanetti, mayor of Scuol, to maintain its influence. The FDP.The Liberals, with Bühler and Michael, are also confirming their presence in an executive historically dominated by moderate parties. The Social Democratic Party (SP), on the other hand, is relying solely on Peyer, without proposing any alternative candidates—a sign of a defensive strategy in a complex political context.

### The opposition: SVP and Green Liberals seeking space

The Swiss People’s Party (SVP), traditionally underrepresented in the Grisons government, is backing Valérie Favre Accola, a prominent figure in the party and President of the Grand Council. Her candidacy represents an attempt to bring a more conservative voice into the executive, particularly on issues such as immigration and public service management. The Green Liberals, meanwhile, are putting forward Nora Saratz Cazin, mayor of Pontresina, as an environmentalist and progressive candidate, in line with the party’s energy and sustainability policies. Both parties will be aiming to win a seat from one of the governing parties, but the challenge looks set to be an uphill one given the numerical advantage of the traditional parties.

### The independent candidate: an element of surprise

Reto Bott, born in 2002 and a law student, is the only candidate not affiliated with a party. His presence introduces an element of uncertainty, particularly if he is able to attract support among young voters or those seeking an alternative to the traditional parties. However, his solitary candidacy could split the vote without significantly impacting the election outcome.

📊 Impact on policies for cross-border workers

The Grisons government elections will not bring about immediate changes for Ticino’s cross-border workers, but they could indirectly influence certain policies. For example, the management of public transport between Ticino and Grisons, as well as access to healthcare services for cross-border workers, could be subject to shifts depending on the new executive’s priorities. Cross-border workers living in border municipalities such as Brusio, Poschiavo, or Brusio should keep a close eye on political developments, particularly in light of any potential changes to cantonal regulations that could affect their rights.

### Comparison with previous elections

Compared to 2022, when Caduff, Maissen, Bühler, and Peyer were elected, this election sees the departure of Parolini and the arrival of two new names: Aita Zanetti and Maurizio Michael. Moreover, the presence of an independent candidate like Bott, along with challenges from the SVP and Green Liberals, represents a novelty compared to previous elections, which were marked by more limited competition. The confirmation of two women among the candidates (Maissen and Zanetti) reflects a trend towards greater female representation in Grisons cantonal politics.

### Possible scenarios after the vote

If the governing parties (Centre, FDP.The Liberals, and SP) manage to retain their seats, the composition of the executive will remain largely unchanged, with possible variations only in the distribution of portfolios. Should the SVP or Green Liberals gain a seat, however, there could be shifts in budget policies, public service management, or relations with Ticino. Cross-border workers should prepare for potential changes in cantonal regulations, especially if the new executive members have different priorities from those currently in place.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). ### How to follow the elections and what to do after voting

Grisons residents will be able to vote on 14 June 2026 either at the polling station or by post. The ballot paper will be published in the official cantonal gazette starting Monday, 21 April, along with the candidates' profiles. Cross-border workers from Ticino living in the border districts (Moesa, Poschiavo, Bernina) are advised to follow updates through the official channels of the Grisons government and the State Chancellery.

### Practical steps for cross-border workers

1. Consult the official list of candidates: From Monday, 21 April, the definitive list will be available in the official cantonal gazette. Cross-border workers can check the names of candidates and their political platforms to assess any potential future implications.

2. **Monitor public debates**: In the months leading up to the vote, political parties will organise public meetings and debates. Interested cross-border workers can attend events in border municipalities or tune in to local broadcasts such as RSI Rete Due or TV Südostschweiz to hear candidates' positions on key issues like employment, healthcare, and transport.

3. Check for any changes to voting procedures: Ticino cross-border workers who wish to vote in Grisons must ensure they are registered on the cantonal electoral rolls. Registration can be completed at the municipal offices in their Ticino place of residence, by presenting a valid ID and a residence certificate.

4. Prepare for potential regulatory changes: After the elections, citizens and cross-border workers should keep an eye on updates from the new Grisons government, particularly regarding transport, healthcare, and employment policies. Any changes to cantonal regulations could affect cross-border workers' rights, for example concerning access to healthcare services or public transport.

📊 Useful tools for cross-border workers
- [Net salary calculator](nav:calculator): To assess the impact of any tax or regulatory changes on personal income.
- [Ticino-Grisons border map](nav:border-map): To identify border municipalities and access crossings.
- [Guide to residency in Switzerland](nav:residency): For those wishing to relocate or regularise their status in Ticino.

### What happens after the vote?

Within a few days of 14 June, the newly elected Grisons government will be sworn in and set its priorities.
