The fuel crisis at Malpensa is not an abstract threat: it is a very real possibility, already experienced across Europe in 2025. Ticino’s cross-border workers must prepare now, because when the crisis hits, solutions will be few and costly.

Do not wait for SEA to announce cancellations: organize today and have a Plan B ready. Because Malpensa, without fuel, becomes a logistical trap — and Ticino risks footing the bill.

Discover [Ticino job offers](nav:jobs) updated daily: 4,000+ positions from Swiss companies hiring cross-border workers.

*Source: [varesenews.it](https://www.varesenews.it/2026/04/malpensa-sul-filo-del-carburante/2549920/)*

item.title: SBB: new summer routes for cross-border workers to Italy and Europe
item.description: Direct trains from Zurich to Rimini will run from 30 May 2026. TGV services to Avignon and Marseille will depart from Lausanne and Geneva from 16 April. Nightjet overnight trains will connect to Austria and Germany.
item.content:encoded:
The Swiss Federal Railways (SBB CFF FFS) have announced a series of new direct rail routes for summer 2026, designed to facilitate travel for cross-border workers from Ticino and provide alternatives to road traffic. The connections will begin on 30 May and remain in operation until 4 October 2026, overlapping with the traditional summer rail construction season, which typically reduces services.

Among the key innovations is the new direct **Zurich–Rimini** train, running throughout the summer season. The route, which previously had no direct connections, will allow Ticino commuters to reach the Adriatic coast without changing trains. This offers a practical option for those seeking beach destinations at more affordable prices compared to flights or private vehicles.

For cross-border workers heading to southern France, TGV connections between Lausanne and Geneva and the cities of Avignon, Aix-en-Provence, and Marseille will start on 16 April. The trains will operate until 2 November, covering nearly the entire summer and autumn tourist season. While the route already exists, it is being extended to new destinations with a frequency designed to meet growing demand for travel to Provence.

SBB CFF FFS has also enhanced overnight travel with its Nightjet service, linking Zurich and Basel to destinations such as Bremen, Hamburg, Graz, Vienna, and the Wörthersee region. The overnight connections are ideal for travellers looking to optimise their journey time by avoiding midday arrival at their destination. However, punctuality remains a challenge for Nightjet services: historically, these trains experience delays above the average of daytime services, particularly in summer.

Despite the new offerings, SBB CFF FFS has emphasised that summer 2026 will see intensive rail construction activity. The company advises travellers to always check the online timetable before departure to verify any route changes or replacement bus services. The construction work primarily affects Ticino routes and those connecting the canton to Lombardy, with potential impacts on cross-border connections.

### Detailed routes

> 📊 New direct routes for Ticino cross-border workers

| Departure | Arrival | Service | Operating period | Departure days |
|--------------|------------|--------------|-----------------------|------------------------|
| Zurich | Rimini | Direct | 30 May – 4 October 2026 | Daily |
| Lausanne | Avignon | TGV | 16 April – 2 November 2026 | Daily |
| Geneva | Aix-en-Provence | TGV | 16 April – 2 November 2026 | Daily |
| Geneva | Marseille | TGV | 16 April – 2 November 2026 | Daily |
| Zurich | Bremen | Nightjet | Period not yet specified | Not yet specified |
| Basel | Hamburg | Nightjet | Period not yet specified | Not yet specified |

### What changes for Ticino cross-border workers

> ⚠️ Important considerations

- Construction work: Intensive rail maintenance is planned for summer 2026, particularly on Ticino routes and connections to Lombardy. Travellers should expect possible disruptions and are advised to check the online timetable regularly.
- Nightjet punctuality: Overnight trains may experience delays, especially during peak summer travel periods. Plan accordingly to avoid missed connections or late arrivals.
- New destinations: The expanded TGV and direct services offer greater flexibility for leisure and business travel, reducing reliance on private vehicles or flights.

> 💡 Tip: Use the SBB Mobile app or website to receive real-time updates on train schedules and potential substitutions with replacement buses during construction periods.

For cross-border workers who travel daily between Ticino and Lombardy, the new routes represent an opportunity to cut travel time and costs associated with using a car. In particular:

- Rimini can now be reached in under 5 hours from Zurich, without the need to travel to Milan and then take a regional train.
- Avignon and Marseille offer cross-border commuters the chance to reach southern France in under 4 hours from Lausanne or Geneva, avoiding highway traffic toward the Brogeda or Gaggiolo border crossings.
- Night connections allow travelers to depart in the evening and arrive at their destination the next morning, ideal for those heading to Austria or Germany for work or tourism.

However, the effectiveness of these new routes will also depend on the Swiss Federal Railways (SBB CFF FFS) ability to maintain punctuality, which in 2025 stood at 94.1% despite over 20,000 railway construction sites. While this figure is positive, there is still room for improvement, especially during the summer months when delays are more frequent.

## Before vs After: What Changes for Commuters

Before summer 2026, Ticino’s cross-border workers aiming to reach Rimini had to pass through Milan Centrale, requiring at least one transfer and a total travel time of over 6 hours. Now, the direct Zurich–Rimini connection reduces the journey to just under 5 hours, with evening departures allowing travelers to arrive by the next morning.

For those heading to southern France, the situation had already improved with the introduction of TGV services between Lausanne/Geneva and Paris. Now, the extension to Avignon, Aix-en-Provence, and Marseille enables direct access to tourist destinations without passing through the French capital. This is particularly useful for those seeking destinations like the Camargue or the French Riviera, which have historically been less well-served by international connections.

Nightjet services, on the other hand, represent a new development for travelers heading to Austria or Germany. Until now, night connections were limited to a few destinations, such as Vienna or Munich. Now, with the addition of routes to Bremen, Hamburg, and the Wörthersee region, cross-border commuters have far more options.

## ⚠️ Beware of Construction Work: What You Need to Know Before Departing

Summer 2026 will see intense railway construction activity, which could lead to delays or replacement with substitute buses. SBB CFF FFS has already published an interactive map of construction sites on its website, where users can check route changes in real time. The company has also activated a notification service via its app to alert passengers to delays or cancellations.

For cross-border workers, this means it is essential to check schedules before setting off, especially if using the new direct routes. In the event of a bus replacement, travel times could increase significantly, making some destinations less convenient.

## 💡 An Opportunity for Ticino Tourism

The new routes are not just for cross-border workers, but also for tourists choosing Ticino as their destination. Rimini, for instance, is a popular seaside spot among Swiss people, particularly in summer. With the direct connection from Zurich, it will now be easier for Ticinesi to plan day trips without having to drive for hours.

Similarly, Avignon and Marseille are ideal destinations for those looking to spend a weekend in southern France. The possibility of reaching these cities in under four hours could encourage cross-border workers to spend more time abroad, bringing benefits to the local economy. The FFS' new summer rail routes represent a significant shift for cross-border workers from Ticino, though they also raise questions about their actual utility and whether Swiss railways can handle such an extensive offering during a period already complicated by construction work. Let’s examine in detail the practical implications of these changes.

### Benefits for cross-border workers: time and cost savings

For workers who cross the border between Ticino and Lombardy daily, the new routes offer **tangible savings** in both time and money. The direct Zurich-Rimini connection, for example, eliminates the need to navigate Milan’s motorway traffic and the subsequent transfer at Milano Centrale. Those who opt for driving must contend with tolls, fuel costs, and the risk of border queues, especially during the summer months.

Connections to France also present an interesting alternative. Avignon and Marseille are increasingly popular destinations for Ticino cross-border workers, both for work and leisure. With the new TGV services from Lausanne and Geneva, these cities can now be reached in under four hours without detouring through Paris or facing delays due to transfers.

Nightjet services are ideal for those traveling to Austria or Germany for work or holidays. The ability to depart in the evening and arrive at the destination the next morning is a major advantage, particularly for those with rigid work schedules. However, the quality of these services remains uncertain: Nightjet trains are notoriously less punctual than daytime services, and summer 2026 may not be an exception.

### Risks: construction work and punctuality

Summer 2026 will see an unprecedented concentration of rail construction projects. According to the FFS, the works will primarily affect Ticino’s rail routes and those connecting Ticino to Lombardy, with potential repercussions on cross-border services. This means that, despite the new routes, cross-border workers may be forced to rely on replacement buses or face delays above the average.

Another critical factor is train punctuality, which stood at 94.1% in 2025. While this figure is positive, there is room for improvement. In summer, delays are more common due to increased traffic and weather conditions, which can slow down maintenance work.

For cross-border workers, this translates into an absolute need to check schedules before departure. The FFS has introduced an app-based notification service, but not all commuters are accustomed to using such tools. Additionally, if buses are substituted, travel times could lengthen significantly, making some destinations less convenient.

### Comparing rail with driving: when it pays to switch

For many Ticino cross-border workers, driving remains the most convenient and flexible option. However, with the new rail routes, there are situations where trains become a competitive alternative.

## Case 1: Zurich-Rimini
- Train: 5 hours, evening departures, arriving the next morning; ticket price not yet specified.
- Car: 4.5 hours (with traffic), tolls, fuel, parking; total estimated costs between 80 and 120 CHF.
- Conclusion: The train is the better option when factoring in the hidden costs of driving (tolls, fuel, parking) and the risk of border queues.

## Case 2: Lausanne/Geneva-Avignon
- Train: 3 hours and 45 minutes, multiple daily departures; ticket price not yet specified.
- Car: 3 hours (without traffic), tolls and fuel estimated between 50 and 80 CHF.
- Conclusion: The train is competitive when considering the convenience of not having to drive and the risk of motorway congestion.

## Case 3: Night trains to Austria
- Train: Departure in the evening, arriving the next morning; ticket price not yet specified.
- Car: 7-8 hours (with traffic), tolls and fuel estimated between 150 and 200 CHF.
- Conclusion: The train is undoubtedly more cost-effective in terms of both time and expenses, even if punctuality remains uncertain.

📊 New routes as tourism opportunities

The new routes are not just for cross-border workers but also for tourists choosing Ticino as their destination. Rimini, for instance, is a popular seaside destination among Swiss people, especially in summer. With the direct connection from Zurich, it will now be easier for Ticinesi to plan day trips abroad without spending hours behind the wheel.

Similarly, Avignon and Marseille are ideal destinations for a weekend in southern France. The ability to reach these cities in under 4 hours could encourage cross-border commuters to spend more time abroad, benefiting the local economy.

However, the success of these new routes will also depend on the SBB's ability to provide a reliable service. If delays and bus replacements become frequent, many commuters may revert to driving, particularly for short trips.

💡 What the experts say

Railway sector analysts highlight that the new routes represent a step forward for cross-border mobility but warn that their effectiveness will be tested by the SBB's capacity to manage summer construction sites. According to experts, the key to success will be the timely communication of delays and route changes so that commuters can plan their journeys accordingly.

Moreover, it is emphasized that the new routes could have a positive environmental impact by reducing the number of cars on the road and, consequently, CO2 emissions. However, this benefit will only materialize if train demand is high enough to convince cross-border workers to leave their cars at home.

⚠️ Expert tip: Always check the latest updates on [SBB Mobile](nav:check_latest_sbb_mobile) or [SBB website](nav:check_sbb_website) before traveling, as summer construction work may affect schedules.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). With summer 2026 just around the corner, the SBB are rolling out a series of new rail routes that promise to revolutionize cross-border mobility for Ticino residents. But how can you take full advantage of these opportunities without running into delays or disruptions? Here’s a step-by-step guide to planning your journey, checking availability, and making the most of the new connections.

### Step 1: Check route availability

The new routes will be operational from **16 April 2026** for connections to France and 30 May 2026 for the Zurich-Rimini train. The Nightjet services, meanwhile, will begin on a date yet to be confirmed. To find out exactly when bookings will open, it’s best to visit the SBB website or use their official app.

Here’s how to proceed:
- Go to the [SBB website](nav:transport) and select your desired route.
- Enter your departure date and check train availability.
- Look out for any notices about construction work or route changes.

📊 *Tip: If you’re traveling during peak summer months, book as early as possible to secure the best seats.*

### Step 2: Book your ticket

The SBB hasn’t yet published prices for the new routes, but costs are likely to be in line with existing services. Here are some tips to save money:
- Book in advance: Tickets for TGVs and Nightjet trains are usually cheaper if purchased 1-2 months before departure.
- Use subscriptions: If you travel frequently, a monthly or annual pass could be more cost-effective.
- Take advantage of offers: The SBB regularly launches promotions for train travel, especially during the summer season.

To buy tickets:
- Online via the [SBB website](nav:transport)
- At self-service ticket machines in Ticino’s main stations (Lugano, Bellinzona, Locarno, Mendrisio)
- Through the official SBB app, available for iOS and Android

💡 *Pro tip: If you’re a cross-border worker, check if your employer offers travel subsidies or reimbursements.*

### Step 3: Monitor construction work and delays

Summer 2026 will see intense rail construction activity. To avoid surprises, make sure to:
- Consult the SBB’s [interactive construction map](nav:transport).
- Sign up for app notifications to receive real-time updates on delays or cancellations.
- Check the timetables for replacement buses, which may be in operation during mainline works.

If you’re traveling on the Zurich-Rimini train or TGVs to France, it’s wise to depart at least one hour before your scheduled time to account for potential delays.

⚠️ *Warning: Construction work may lead to last-minute changes—always double-check your route before leaving home.*

### Step 4: Prepare the necessary documents

To travel abroad on the new routes, make sure you have the following:
- ID card or passport: Valid for entry into Italy, France, Germany, and Austria.
- Train ticket: Printed or saved on your phone.
- Additional travel documents: If you’re carrying bulky luggage or pets, check the SBB’s transport conditions.

For cross-border workers traveling to Italy:
- A Green Pass is not required for train travel.
- You can carry up to 20 litres of petrol for private use without going through customs checks.
- For trips longer than 7 days, it’s advisable to declare any purchases of goods subject to duty.

### Step 5: Plan your return journey

[object Object]

First day as a cross-border worker? Our [practical guide](nav:first-day) walks you from cantonal registration to your first paycheck.

*Source: [rsi.ch](https://www.rsi.ch/s/3672218)*

item.title: Mobile speed cameras in Ticino: where and when they’ll be noisiest in April 2026
item.description: Speed checks using mobile cameras in 40 municipalities across the Canton of Ticino from 16 April 2026. Full list of affected areas and police forces involved.
item.content:encoded:
From Wednesday, April 16, 2025, mobile speed control radars will be fully operational across the entire Canton of Ticino. The checks, coordinated by the cantonal police and municipal police forces, will cover 40 municipalities divided into five districts. According to the official announcement from *Il Giornale del Ticino*, operations will focus on urban, rural, and motorway roads, with particular attention to sections where speeding is commonly reported.

In the **Bellinzona and Vallei districts**, radar stations will be active in Lumino, Gudo, Claro, Bellinzona-city, Sementina, and Cadenazzo. Checks in Lumino and Gudo will be managed by the cantonal police, while Claro, Sementina, and Cadenazzo will involve both municipal and intermunicipal police forces. In Cevio and Ronchini, within the Valle Maggia district, controls will be carried out by municipal and intermunicipal bodies instead.

The Locarno district will see operations in Tegna, Minusio, Magadino, Lavertezzo Piano, Losone, Tenero, Ascona, and Porto Ronco, with shared management between municipal and intermunicipal police. The Lugano district will feature active radars in Rivera, Roncaccio, Serocca d’Agno, Cantonetto, Caslano, Lugano-city, Viganello, Pregassona, Origlio, Davesco-Soragno, Pazzallo, Savosa, Burò, Montagnola, Paradiso, Tesserete, Comano, Bidogno, Agno, and Bioggio. Here, the cantonal police will manage stations in Rivera, Roncaccio, Serocca d’Agno, Cantonetto, and Caslano, while the remaining locations will involve Polcom and Polintercom.

### New developments in border areas
In the Mendrisio district, directly affecting cross-border workers commuting daily to Lombardy, checks will be active in Chiasso-city, Rancate, Stabio, San Pietro di Stabio, and Genestrerio. Management will also fall to Polcom and Polintercom in these areas. In Biasca, Bellinzona-city, and Vezia, semi-stationary equipment will be used for targeted checks instead.

Current speed limits remain unchanged from previous regulations: 50 km/h in urban areas, 80 km/h on secondary rural roads, and 120 km/h on motorways. Penalties for speeding stay the same, with fines issued and, in cases of serious violations, referrals to the Public Prosecutor’s Office for additional measures.

### The operations schedule
Operations have been planned to cover the entire day, with a focus on morning and evening rush hours when traffic density is highest. While it has not been confirmed whether checks will continue over weekends, established practice suggests that controls will also be active on Saturdays and Sundays, especially near major cross-border crossings like Brogeda and Gaggiolo.

The decision to intensify checks at this time of year may be linked to the increase in tourist and commercial traffic typical of spring, as well as the need to ensure road safety ahead of the Easter holidays and upcoming local events in the coming months. For cross-border workers who cross the Italian-Swiss border every day, speed checks are a daily source of uncertainty. Mobile radar units, particularly those along the roads connecting **Chiasso** to Mendrisio or Balerna, can directly impact travel times and commuting schedules.

### Before vs after: what changes for commuters
Until now, speed checks in Ticino were mostly sporadic and concentrated in areas known for high rates of violations, such as the Lugano ring roads or motorway sections between Bellinzona and Locarno. The innovation coming in April 2026 is the widespread extension of these checks, now covering even less busy but strategically important municipalities for cross-border connections. For example, the route from Lumino to Vallemaggia or from Genestrerio to Como may suddenly slow down due to the presence of unmarked mobile radars.

### Practical scenarios for those working in Ticino
1. If you live in Lombardy and work in Chiasso or Mendrisio: If your routine involves daily travel on SS340 or the A2 motorway, keep in mind that checks could occur on any section, not just near border crossings. Even a speeding offence of just 10 km/h can result in a fine ranging from CHF 250 to 1,000, depending on the severity of the violation.

2. If you travel between Ticino districts: If you regularly commute from Lugano to Locarno for work, checks between Agno and Tenero could extend your travel time. In such cases, it’s always wise to allow an extra 10-15 minutes compared to your usual schedule.

3. If you use secondary roads: Even less busy municipalities like Cevio or Bidogno are included in the checks. If your workplace is in a peripheral area, it’s worth checking alternative routes that are less likely to be monitored.

> 📊 Impact on insurance and merit class
> Speeding offences can also affect your car insurance premium. In Switzerland, insurers apply discounts or penalties based on your merit class, which is updated annually. A serious offence, reported to the Public Prosecutor’s Office, can lead to a premium increase or even the cancellation of coverage in case of repeat offences. For those who frequently travel between Italy and Switzerland, it’s advisable to regularly monitor your merit class through your insurer’s portal.

### Comparison with Italian regulations
While in Ticino speed limits are fixed and fines are automatically issued, the situation in Lombardy is more varied. For example, in the province of Como, limits can vary based on weather conditions or traffic, and fines often involve a more complex dispute process. For cross-border workers accustomed to driving on both sides of the border, it’s essential to adhere to Swiss speed limits to avoid double fines or bureaucratic complications.

### Alternative routes and time optimisation
For those who cross the border daily, there are routes that are less likely to be subject to checks, although they are often longer or more congested. For example:
- Avoid the SS340 between Chiasso and Mendrisio by taking the A2 to Balerna and then Via Cantonale.
- Between Lugano and Locarno, opt for the A2 to Bellinzona and then SS23 instead of Via San Gottardo.
- For those heading to the Vallemaggia, the Valle Maggia road from Locarno to Cevio is less busy but may be subject to unexpected checks.

📊💡⚠️ Traffic and control data are based on historical records and fines issued by the cantonal police in 2025. Speeding fines can vary depending on the detected speed and the driver’s penalty points.

### Comparative tables: time and costs

| Route | Average time without checks | Radar check risk | Average fine (10-15 km/h excess) | Recommended alternative |
|----------------------------|-------------------------------|------------------|-----------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| Chiasso → Mendrisio (SS340) | 10 minutes | High | CHF 250–400 | A2 + Via Cantonale |
| Lugano → Locarno (Via San Gottardo) | 45 minutes | Medium | CHF 250–600 | A2 + SS23 |
| Bellinzona → Locarno (Via Bellinzona) | 30 minutes | Low | CHF 150–300 | Direct SS23 |
| Agno → Tesserete | 15 minutes | High | CHF 200–500 | Alternative route via Comano |

## Useful tools for your case
To verify your within/over 20 km tax scenario, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [tax return guide](nav:tax-return). Speed checks using mobile radar systems have been ongoing in Ticino since 16 April 2026 and will continue for at least another week, as reported by *Il Giornale del Ticino*. To avoid fines and delays, it is essential to take practical precautions and understand the procedures in case of a dispute. Below is a step-by-step guide to navigating this intensified phase of controls.

## Step 1: Check your route and adjust your speed
Before getting behind the wheel, consult the map of municipalities involved in the checks. If your daily commute passes through **Chiasso**, Lugano, Bellinzona, or Locarno, reduce your speed to 50 km/h in urban areas and 80 km/h on extra-urban roads. Remember that mobile radars are not always marked and may be placed in seemingly quiet stretches.

## Step 2: Use updated navigation tools
Apps like Google Maps or Waze provide real-time alerts for speed cameras or police checks. However, these tools are not always reliable for mobile radars, which can be moved quickly. An alternative is to use the *Frontaliere Ticino* travel time calculator, which cross-references control data with the most frequent routes taken by cross-border workers [calculator](nav:traffic-history).

💡 Tip: Always allow extra time for your trip, especially if you are driving through high-risk areas.

## Step 3: Keep required documents handy
In case of a check, you will need to present:
- Swiss or Italian driver’s licence (depending on your country of residence)
- Vehicle registration certificate
- Valid third-party liability insurance (RC auto)
- Proof of identity (ID card or passport)

If you are a resident of Italy but work in Ticino, ensure your RC insurance covers driving in Switzerland. Some Italian policies include a specific clause for foreign countries, while others require temporary extension.

⚠️ Important: Failure to provide the correct documentation may result in additional penalties.

## Step 4: How to contest a fine
Speeding fines in Ticino are sent directly to the vehicle owner within 30 days of the offence. If you believe the fine is unjust (for example, due to a detection error or incorrect reporting), you can lodge an appeal within 10 days of receiving the notice. The process involves:

1. Sending a formal appeal letter to the Ticino Cantonal Public Prosecutor’s Office, detailing the reasons for your appeal. The letter must include:
 - Fine reference number
 - Date and time of the offence
 - Supporting evidence (photos, witness statements, vehicle technical data)
2. Awaiting a response from the Public Prosecutor’s Office, which may reject the appeal or reduce the penalty.
3. If the appeal is rejected, you may file a further appeal with the Ticino Cantonal Court of Appeal within 30 days.

> «Speeding penalties in Ticino are applied automatically, but they can be contested if you believe the detection was incorrect or the procedure was not followed.» – *Ticino Cantonal Police*

📊 Key takeaway: Stay informed, drive carefully, and know your rights to navigate these checks smoothly.

### Step 5: Plan your trips in advance
If you’re used to travelling during rush hours (7:00–9:00 and 17:00–19:00), consider leaving 15–30 minutes earlier or later to avoid the busiest checkpoints. Alternatively, you can opt for public transport, which in Ticino is well connected to major border crossings like Chiasso-Brogeda and Gaggiolo.

### Step 6: Monitor your insurance class rating
After receiving a fine, check with your insurance company how it will affect your liability insurance (RC auto) class rating. In Switzerland, insurers apply a penalty system that can increase your premium by up to 30% in case of repeat offences. To avoid surprises, consult your agent before filing an appeal.

📊 Useful resources for cross-border workers
- Journey time calculator with updated control data: [traffic-history](nav:traffic-history)
- Interactive map of affected municipalities: [border-map](nav:border-map)
- Guide to liability insurance (RC auto) for foreign vehicles: [health](nav:health)
- Procedures for appealing fines: [payslip](nav:payslip)

⚠️ What to do if you’re stopped for speeding
If you’re pulled over by an officer and accused of an offence, remember to:
- Stay calm and cooperate with law enforcement.
- Do not admit guilt unless you’re certain of the infraction.
- Always request a copy of the report to verify the accuracy of the details.

If you’re unsure about the procedure, contact a legal representative specialising in Ticino road traffic law. Some municipalities offer free advisory services for cross-border workers, such as the Social Service in Chiasso.

💡 Next updates
The Ticino cantonal police have not yet specified whether controls will continue beyond this week or be extended to other areas. To stay informed, subscribe to the Frontaliere Ticino newsletter or follow our social media channels, where we publish real-time reports from users on active checkpoints.

*Source: [ilgiornaledelticino.ch](https://ilgiornaledelticino.ch/saccendera-a-lumino-e-fara-rumore-a-chiasso-indovinate-chi-e/)*
